metadata.title: Awesome Group Generators
metadata.description: A transparent, curated review dataset for group generators, team pickers, classroom grouping tools, breakout-room planners, and social-golfer schedulers.

tools.name: GroupMixer
tools.bestFor: Almost every group generation usecase
tools.summary: GroupMixer is much more capable than the simple randomizers on this list. It can also do a quick random split, but its real value is constraints, repeated sessions, partial attendance, imports/exports, repeat-pairing reduction, diagnostics, and local-first scenario workflows. The tradeoff is that the full workspace is more complex than a toy picker.
tools.pricing: Free browser-based tool. No account, subscription, or paywall for the solver, imports, exports, scenario storage, or result diagnostics.
tools.overallComment: The strongest all-around group generator here if the user needs more than a one-off random split, but the advanced workspace is naturally denser than toy randomizers.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The basic landing-page flow is easy: paste names, choose groups or group size, and generate. The advanced grid/workspace is necessarily denser than one-click splitters because it exposes sessions, capacities, constraints, imports, diagnostics, and saved scenarios.
ratingComments.design: Modern and much more polished than most utility sites. Some screens are naturally dense because the tool exposes advanced setup, and parts of the expert workspace/mobile experience could be smoother.
ratingComments.features: Extremely deep for a group generator: multi-session scheduling, participant availability, editable groups, custom capacities and minimums, per-session capacity overrides, hard/soft constraints, weighted objectives, repeat reduction, imports, exports, saved scenarios/workspaces, saved result history, scenario import/export bundles, solver telemetry/result metadata, diagnostics, pair matrices, and API access. The main missing areas are presentation gimmicks, self-join links, and arbitrary non-group-generator customization.
ratingComments.outputs: Excellent output coverage: CSV-style exports, copy/print/share workflows, Zoom breakout CSV export, scenario bundles, result diagnostics, and API output. Not a literal 5/5 because it does not cover every presentation/export format such as polished image/PDF result exports or embeddable result widgets.
ratingComments.privacy: Very strong for a web-based group generator, mainly because normal solving runs in the browser and does not upload participant names, group assignments, or full scenario contents to GroupMixer servers. Drafts, saved scenarios, saved results, and UI state are stored locally in the browser via IndexedDB/localStorage/sessionStorage/service-worker cache. It does not deserve a perfect score because Vercel hosting sees normal technical request logs, Vercel Web Analytics / Convex telemetry may receive page views and coarse product events using a sessionStorage-based session id, solver failures may send anonymized scenario structure, and feedback can transmit whatever the user submits. Far ahead of ad-tech-heavy competitors: no account is required, no participant roster upload is needed for normal use, and there is no mass third-party vendor consent stack.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: This is the result-quality outlier in the comparison. GroupMixer goes above and beyond: it uses a custom-built solver engine designed specifically for social-golfer-like, multi-session group assignment cases. For this problem class, that domain-specific machinery can outperform general tools such as OR-Tools and enterprise constraint solvers such as Gurobi or Hexaly by a huge margin, because the solver encodes extensive SGP-specific theory, construction logic, local search, repeat minimization, constraints, capacities, partial attendance, and diagnostics directly into the search. It is plausibly at or beyond the level a user can expect from current academic SGP literature for many practical cases.

pros: Purpose-built solver engine for social golfer-style cases
Runs in the browser with no account
Strong import/export options, including Zoom CSV and API access
Scenario manager with saved workspaces and result history
Scenario import/export bundles, diagnostics, and storage management
cons: Advanced workspace has a learning curve
No self-join flow or presentation gimmicks
tools.reviewNote: Best fit when group quality matters. This is not just a one-shot roster splitter: it has a real scenario manager, saved workspaces, saved results, import/export bundles, storage cleanup/export tooling, API access, and solver/result metadata. The solver should be described as a purpose-built social golfer / multi-session assignment engine, not a generic randomizer. Overkill if the user only wants a novelty randomizer.

tools.name: ChatGPT Pro Extended Reasoning
tools.bestFor: Complex natural-language grouping drafts when cost and verification are acceptable
tools.summary: ChatGPT Pro with extended/pro reasoning is a stronger version of the ChatGPT grouping workaround. It has access to an internal scratchpad workspace where the agent can write ad hoc scripts, implement scenario-specific optimisation algorithms, conduct extensive online research, and validate its results before providing the groups/schedules/whatever the user asked for in essentially any format the user asks for.
tools.pricing: Paid ChatGPT Pro plan; pricing has varied by Pro tier/region and is far more expensive than typical group-generator tools. Business/Enterprise privacy defaults are different from consumer Pro.
tools.overallComment: Extremely capable, but also extremely expensive.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Its about as easy as it gets for basically arbitrary features/any feature you can imagine. You just describe whatever you want in natural language.
ratingComments.design: Same polished ChatGPT interface as the free version, with better model/tool access. It is chat-first rather than a group-generator UI.
ratingComments.features: Very flexible in natural language: arbitrary group sizes, multi-round schedules, balancing requests, keep-together/keep-apart rules, pinned people, custom output formats, and iterative revisions. It can basically do whatever you ask for. The limitation is validation: these are model-generated plans, not hard solver features with guaranteed feasibility.
ratingComments.outputs: Can produce the output in whatever format the user asks for.
ratingComments.privacy: A poor default fit for sensitive rosters. Pro is a consumer plan, not the same as Business/Enterprise no-training defaults; data use depends on settings and policies. Users should not paste student/personnel data unless their account and consent setup supports it.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Requires a paid Pro subscription and account. The cost and plan management are real friction compared with free web group generators.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Extremely strong result quality because the model can perform highly advanced reasoning and implement advanced algorithms matching the exact problem ad-hoc, leading to almost always extremely high quality results.

pros: Can handle essentially arbitrary grouping requirements
Can write and run ad hoc optimization scripts
Excellent multi-round/repeat-minimization capability when prompted well
Can validate results and compute diagnostics
Can produce CSV, Excel-style tables, Zoom-ready CSV, Markdown, JSON, or custom formats
Can research, reason about edge cases, and iterate quickly
cons: Very expensive for this use case
Consumer Pro privacy caveats for sensitive rosters
Requires an account and paid subscription
Can be extremely slow, 10 min - 1 hour for generations with more complex constraints
Quality depends on prompting and verification
tools.reviewNote: Review consumer ChatGPT Pro extended/pro reasoning, not ChatGPT Business/Enterprise, the API, or a custom solver. It deserves higher capability than free ChatGPT, but privacy/cost/validation keep it below purpose-built solvers.

tools.name: Social Golfer Online
tools.bestFor: Golf leagues, trips, tournaments, and other multi-round schedules where avoiding repeat pairings matters
tools.summary: Social Golfer Online is a serious social-golfer-problem scheduler, not a toy random team splitter. It uses a genetic-style algorithm and local search to create low-repeat pairings across many sessions, supports absences, fixed tee-time positions, dynamic group sizes, session history, separation rules, matrix reports, share links, offline calculation, drag-and-drop corrections, JSON data export, and event management. It is golf-first and account-based, but the underlying grouping problem is exactly the kind of hard multi-round assignment most random team generators cannot handle.
tools.pricing: Free during beta for individual non-commercial use. The site says beta registrants are grandfathered into Lifetime Premium at $0 if paid tiers are introduced; agent/club/pro features may require direct access or future tiers.
tools.overallComment: One of the strongest real competitors for repeat-minimizing multi-round grouping. Narrowly golf-branded and account-gated, but algorithmically far beyond normal random splitters.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Powerful but not instant. Users need to register, add members, create sessions, and understand a golf scheduling workflow.
ratingComments.design: Modern, clear, and purpose-built. The landing page explains complex scheduling well, though the app is more specialized than a generic group maker.
ratingComments.features: Excellent: multi-session scheduling, repeat minimization, absences, fixed positions, separation rules, dynamic sizes, session locks, matrix report, offline calculation, drag/drop correction, JSON export, and share links. However it does not support arbitrary attribute balance/keep together/keep apart except through the workaround of pinning people.
ratingComments.outputs: Strong: guest share links, print-ready itinerary reports, matrix reports, data archive/JSON export, and group instructions. Some print/PDF features are tier-limited.
ratingComments.privacy: No ad stack and the site says it does not sell or share data, but it stores email/profile/event data and uses PostHog analytics/session infrastructure. Not local-only.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Full use requires account registration. Guests can view shared assignments without login, but organizers cannot just paste names and generate anonymously.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Very high for multi-round repeat minimization. Genetic/local search, history-aware scheduling, conflict scoring, and separation rules are far beyond random assignment.

cons: Not a quick anonymous random splitter
Stores event/member data on the service
tools.reviewNote: Golf-first product, but the algorithm and workflow are directly relevant to social-golfer-style group generation.

tools.name: TourneyCard Golf Randomizer
tools.bestFor: Social-golfer-style golf schedule optimizer with repeat diagnostics, partner preferences, and Excel/Markdown output
tools.summary: TourneyCard Golf Randomizer creates multi-round golf groups for twosomes, threesomes, or foursomes. Live testing generated an 8-player, 2-round schedule with a pairing matrix, summary metrics, repeat-play diagnostics, iteration counts, and Excel/Markdown outputs. The names textarea accepts CSV-style preferred partners and avoid partners, making this a genuinely interesting social-golfer-style solver.
tools.pricing: Free web tool with optional Clerk sign-in.
tools.overallComment: A notably strong social-golfer scheduler with diagnostics and exports.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Reviewed with a live browser pass and sample names where the page allowed it.
ratingComments.design: Reflects observed page clarity, workflow, and surrounding clutter.
ratingComments.features: Multiple rounds, group size, iteration count, preferred/avoid partners, pairing matrix, and summary diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Excel and Markdown outputs are available.
ratingComments.privacy: Clerk, PostHog, Umami, fonts, and Buy Me a Coffee scripts/cookies observed; sign-in is optional for reviewed flow.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required for the reviewed basic flow unless noted.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Directly optimizes repeated encounters and reports solution quality, unlike basic randomizers.

tools.reviewNote: Especially interesting social golfer solver; consider deeper benchmark later.

tools.name: Clever Groups
tools.bestFor: Preference-based classroom, camp, event, and accommodation grouping
tools.summary: Clever Groups is a serious preference-based group generator. It supports participant preferences, blocking preferences, categories, custom group sizes, student/guest self-entry, spreadsheet import/export for registered users, group satisfaction scoring, group history, drag-and-drop adjustments, and CSV export. The free try-it mode works without registration, but the full workflow is account/cloud-based.
tools.pricing: Free try-it mode without registration. Registration unlocks saved teams, multiple teams, spreadsheet import, and student self-entry workflows; site copy says the service has been free, but subscription/payment infrastructure exists in the privacy policy.
tools.overallComment: One of the strongest reviewed tools for single-session preference/category grouping. It is cloud/account-heavy and not a repeat-pair optimizer, but the actual grouping model is serious.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The no-registration try-it flow is usable, but the full product has a lot of concepts: teams, students, preferences, blocking preferences, categories, group entries, category entries, satisfaction scores, and guest links. Powerful, not trivial.
ratingComments.design: Functional but not slick. It feels like a mature school/admin web app rather than a modern lightweight tool. The cookie banner is heavy, but the grouping workflow is understandable once learned.
ratingComments.features: Very strong for preference-based grouping: participant preferences, teacher blocking preferences, categories, category balancing, custom group sizes, guest preference entry, spreadsheet import/export, saved teams, multiple teams, group history, drag-and-drop adjustments, satisfaction scoring, and CSV export. Missing multi-round repeat minimization, pair matrix, and advanced solver tuning.
ratingComments.outputs: Good output surface: CSV export, displayed groups, group history, satisfaction metrics, and registered workflows for importing/exporting student and preference lists. No Zoom-specific export, print/PDF focus, or public share result page in the try-it flow.
ratingComments.privacy: Mixed. The site has detailed GDPR/CCPA/COPPA-style policy language and says it does not sell/share personal information, but the full workflow stores names/preferences in a hosted service with accounts, cookies, Fathom/Cookiebot/Cloudflare, Zoho/Backblaze/Hostgator-style subprocessors, and child-consent complexity. Not local/private like browser-only tools.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Try-it mode works without registration, which is good. Real use strongly pushes account creation for saving, spreadsheet import, multiple teams, and student self-entry; students/guests may also join via links/cookies.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Strong for single-session preference/category grouping. Satisfaction scoring and category/blocking-preference handling make it far better than random splitters. It is not a multi-round social-golfer optimizer and may need manual tweaking for hard real-world allocations.

pros: Category balancing and custom group sizes
cons: Account needed for the full useful workflow
Hosted roster/preference data and child-consent complexity
tools.reviewNote: Review as Clever Groups itself, not Clever/other education products. It deserves high feature/result scores because preferences, categories, satisfaction, and guest entry are real grouping capabilities. Privacy/account friction should stay lower because this is a hosted roster/preference system.

tools.name: ClassroomGroupGenerator.com
tools.bestFor: Teachers who want browser-saved rosters, constraints, presentation mode, and classroom-tool integration
tools.summary: ClassroomGroupGenerator.com is a serious classroom grouping workspace rather than a toy randomizer. In guest mode it let us create a class roster, open the group-generator board, choose group size or group count, apply gender distribution modes, enforce always-together and do-not-group-with rules, generate groups, reshuffle, download, and use presentation-style classroom tools. The tradeoff is that it is tied to a broader classroom whiteboard/tool suite and uses analytics/support scripts around student-roster workflows.
tools.pricing: Free guest workflow with browser-only storage and no signup required. Pro is advertised at $2.99/month for cloud saving, multi-device sync, AI tools, support, and related classroom-suite features.
tools.overallComment: One of the stronger classroom-specific group generators reviewed: real roster management, constraints, presentation mode, and usable exports without requiring signup. Privacy is decent but not local-only because the site uses analytics/session tooling and cloud sync is part of the product.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The initial class setup takes a couple of steps, but the actual grouping workflow is clear: create roster, open Group Generator, choose size/count, generate. Guest mode helps a lot because no signup is needed for the basic workflow.
ratingComments.design: Modern, readable classroom-tool interface with a good whiteboard/presentation feel. It is busier than a single-purpose generator because it is part of a whole classroom tools suite.
ratingComments.features: Strong classroom feature depth: saved rosters, CSV/TXT import, group size/count, gender modes, always-together and do-not-group-with rules, absent handling via roster state, task assignment, presentation mode, reshuffle, and saved boards.
ratingComments.outputs: Good output surface for teachers: generated group cards, reshuffle, download, presentation/whiteboard use, saved local class state, and share/import/export claims. It is not a spreadsheet-first or Zoom-specific exporter.
ratingComments.privacy: Better than ad-heavy utilities and the free mode can keep class data in browser storage. The live page loaded Google Analytics, Microsoft Clarity, Crisp, and related scripts; the privacy policy also describes account/cloud storage of student names and classroom data.
ratingComments.accountFriction: The reviewed workflow works in guest mode without signup. Account/pro upgrade is mainly for cloud sync, multi-device use, and paid classroom-suite features.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Much better than basic random splitters because it supports keep-together, keep-apart, gender modes, group size/count, and balancing strategy. It is a single-session classroom generator rather than a multi-round optimizer.

pros: Saved class rosters in browser storage
cons: Broader classroom-tool suite adds UI complexity
Analytics/session replay/support scripts around roster workflows
tools.reviewNote: Review the group generator/whiteboard workflow, not the unrelated AI lesson/rubric/quiz tools. Guest mode is a major advantage; analytics/session tooling keeps privacy below local-only tools.

tools.name: Group Balancer
tools.bestFor: Reusable group generator with bags, multi-person slips, custom group sizes, editing, sharing, and history
tools.summary: Group Balancer is a more productized random group generator. It supports reusable bags of participants, multi-person slips for keeping people together, custom group sizes, manual editing before confirmation, instant sharing, browser guest mode, and account-based sync/history. It is significantly more capable than a one-off randomizer, especially for repeated group generation.
tools.pricing: Free web tool with guest mode and optional account sync.
tools.overallComment: A strong group generator with reusable rosters, keep-together slips, custom sizes, editing, sharing, and history.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Reviewed with a pasted eight-name sample where possible; the main controls were understandable.
ratingComments.design: Usability reflects the observed page layout and surrounding clutter.
ratingComments.features: Reusable bags, multi-person slips, custom group sizes, manual edits, sharing, and history.
ratingComments.outputs: Formatted copy/share is described; file exports were not the focus.
ratingComments.privacy: Guest mode stores data in browser; optional account sync is available.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account was required for the reviewed basic flow unless otherwise noted.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Better than pure randomizers thanks to keep-together slips, custom sizes, edits, and history.

tools.reviewNote: One of the best additions in this batch.

tools.name: Educatarea Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Teachers who need roles, restrictions, multiple pools, history, PDF, and print without an account
tools.summary: Educatarea Random Group Generator is a surprisingly capable classroom grouping tool. It supports multiple name lists/pools for balanced composition, number-of-groups or names-per-group setup, group roles, restrictions to prevent specific names from being placed together, configurable group names, color display, generation history, copy, PDF, and print. The UI is utilitarian rather than polished, and the site uses Google Tag Manager, but the grouping feature set is far above normal random splitters.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: One of the better lightweight classroom grouping tools reviewed. Not a full multi-session optimizer, but it has real classroom grouping controls and strong outputs.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The basic flow works, but the settings panel is dense. It trades simplicity for real controls.
ratingComments.design: Plain and utilitarian. Not beautiful, but the controls are organized well enough.
ratingComments.features: Strong: multiple name lists, group count or names-per-group, roles, restrictions, group-name modes, colors, and generation history. Missing true multi-session repeat optimization and detailed diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Excellent for a free tool: copy, PDF, print, and history are visible in the result workflow.
ratingComments.privacy: No account required and state is stored locally, but the page loads Google Tag Manager and has no obvious dedicated privacy page for the tool.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Above simple random splitters because multiple pools, restrictions, and roles can materially improve classroom groups. Not a solver-grade repeat/balance optimizer.

pros: Number of groups or names per group
tools.reviewNote: This should be treated as a serious lightweight classroom competitor, not another toy randomizer.

tools.name: Good-Enough Golfers
tools.bestFor: Simple social-golfer-style multi-round schedules
tools.summary: Good-Enough Golfers is a browser-based near-solver for the Social Golfer Problem. It creates multiple rounds of groups while trying to minimize repeat pairings, supports group count, people per group, number of rounds, optional group leaders, hard never-together pairs, softer prefer-split groups, player names, conflict score, printing, CSV export, and local autosave.
tools.pricing: Free and open source; optional tip jar. No account or subscription required.
tools.overallComment: Strong at one important thing: multi-round repeat minimization. The UI is rough and constraint support is narrow, but this is much more serious than basic random splitters.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The sliders and text boxes are manageable, but the mental model is mathy. Users need to understand groups, people per group, rounds, leaders, conflict score, and pair constraints. Not hard for technical facilitators, less friendly for casual teachers.
ratingComments.design: Plain and dated. It is usable, but it looks like a small open-source solver demo rather than a polished product.
ratingComments.features: Very strong for social-golfer-style repeat minimization: group count, group size, number of rounds, group leaders, player names, hard never-together pairs, softer prefer-split groups, conflict score, local autosave, print, and CSV export. Weak outside that niche: no arbitrary capacities, partial attendance, rich attributes, per-session constraints, or UI-guided balancing.
ratingComments.outputs: Useful but not flashy: clean print view and CSV download with a pivot-style view of player/group sequences. No share links, Zoom export, PDF button, or visual reporting beyond the conflict score.
ratingComments.privacy: Excellent. The page explicitly says names are never sent to servers and all processing happens on the user’s own computer. No obvious ads, Google Analytics, cookies, or tracking scripts were visible; source is public. Only caveat is the CDN-loaded Immutable dependency.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account, no signup, no payment. Just open the page and run it.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Good for its niche. It uses a genetic/near-solver approach and conflict score to reduce repeated pairings across rounds, with hard and soft pair constraints. It is not guaranteed optimal and can struggle with hard cases, but it beats almost every simple random splitter for multi-round mixing.

cons: No partial attendance or per-session setup
No share links or Zoom export
tools.reviewNote: This is a serious social-golfer niche tool. It should be compared against other social-golfer and repeat-minimization solvers rather than one-off random team generators. Feature breadth is narrower than broad planning tools, but result quality for repeat minimization is credible.

tools.name: Grouper.school
tools.bestFor: Account-based classroom grouping with Google Classroom/Clever rosters and student attributes
tools.summary: Grouper.school is a polished classroom grouping product built around account-based rosters and student attributes. It connects to Google Classroom or Clever, supports student attributes such as gender/proficiency/leader/conflicts, marks students absent, forms randomized or customized smart groups, reuses recent groupings, and has Live Mode for presenting groups. It is not a no-account tool: signup uses Google/Clever or an email magic link before using the actual app.
tools.pricing: Free plan includes randomized/custom groups, smart groups using up to two student attributes, do-not-group-with, absent marking, recent groupings for 7 days, Live Mode, Google Classroom/Clever auto-roster, and up to 40 students per class. Pro is advertised at $6.99/month per teacher annually and adds unlimited attributes, roles, permanent saved groupings, larger classes, AI-assisted imports, co-teacher support, and priority support.
tools.overallComment: A strong dedicated classroom grouping product if an account-based roster workflow is acceptable. It is polished and privacy-conscious for a hosted education tool, but less attractive for users who want instant no-login grouping or local-only roster handling.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The product looks easy once set up, but signup and roster integration come before actual grouping. That is more friction than paste-and-generate tools.
ratingComments.design: Very polished, classroom-specific interface and landing page. The attribute demos and Live Mode positioning are clear.
ratingComments.features: Strong classroom feature set: Google Classroom/Clever rosters, custom attributes, gender/proficiency/leader/conflict examples, do-not-group-with, absent marking, recent grouping reuse, Live Mode, and paid expansion to unlimited attributes/roles.
ratingComments.outputs: Good classroom presentation and saved/recent grouping workflow, but no obvious spreadsheet/Zoom/export-first output surface from the public materials.
ratingComments.privacy: Good for a hosted education product: no advertising, no sale of personal data, student records not used for targeted advertising, COPPA/FERPA language, encryption, and vendor list. Hosted/account-based with analytics, Intercom, Google, Posthog, and roster integrations.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Account is required. Email signup sends a magic link; Google/Clever are also offered.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Strong single-session classroom grouping quality because groups are built around student attributes and teacher knowledge rather than pure randomization. Not a multi-round repeat minimizer.

pros: Google Classroom and Clever roster import
Free tier with useful grouping features
cons: Permanent saved groupings and unlimited attributes are paid
tools.reviewNote: Review as an account-based classroom grouping product. Do not penalize it for not being no-account in feature scoring, but account friction and hosted roster handling should be explicit.

tools.name: Not Random Groups
tools.bestFor: Teacher group generator with random and keep-together/keep-apart modes plus optional Google Classroom import
tools.summary: Not Random Groups is a teacher-focused group generator offering Random mode and Not Random mode for keeping students together or apart. It supports typing or pasting names, optional Google Classroom import, first-name formatting, number-of-groups controls, Generate Groups, and full-screen display. No signup is required unless using Google Classroom import.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. Google sign-in optional for Classroom roster import.
tools.overallComment: A strong teacher group maker with explicit keep-together/keep-apart workflow.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Reviewed with a live browser pass and sample names where the page allowed it.
ratingComments.design: Reflects observed page clarity, workflow, and surrounding clutter.
ratingComments.features: Paste/type names, random mode, not-random keep-together/keep-apart mode, Google Classroom import, formatting, and fullscreen.
ratingComments.outputs: Display/fullscreen output; no CSV/PDF export observed.
ratingComments.privacy: Google sign-in, Google Analytics, and fonts observed; account optional for roster import.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required for the reviewed basic flow unless noted.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Keep-together/keep-apart support makes it stronger than most classroom randomizers.

tools.reviewNote: High-value teacher constraint tool.

tools.name: Optimal Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Open-source repeat-avoidance group maker with previous records and no-login local storage
tools.summary: Optimal Group Generator is an open-source group maker designed for repeated group activities where everyone should meet new people as much as possible. The app lets users manage members, keep previous records, generate optimized groups, and manually arrange groups with drag and drop. The GitHub README says it can be used without signing in with data stored in local storage, while sign-in stores data on a server.
tools.pricing: Free open-source web app. Optional sign-in.
tools.overallComment: A rare repeat-avoidance group maker aimed at maximizing new contacts.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Reviewed with a live browser pass and sample names where the page allowed it.
ratingComments.design: Reflects observed page clarity, workflow, and surrounding clutter.
ratingComments.features: Member management, previous records, optimized groups, manual drag/drop, and repeat-avoidance objective.
ratingComments.outputs: No file export verified; results are app views/records.
ratingComments.privacy: Only site and unpkg observed in reviewed run; README says no-login data is local storage, optional sign-in stores on server.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required for the reviewed basic flow unless noted.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Explicitly optimizes repeated group activities so people meet new people.

tools.reviewNote: Especially interesting; GitHub: https://github.com/Group-Maker/Group_Maker

tools.name: PickTeams.net
tools.bestFor: Featureful team picker with skill/gender tags, locked players, CSV export, and share links
tools.summary: PickTeams.net accepts pasted player lists with optional gender and skill modifiers, supports pre-assigning players to teams, generates balanced random teams, and then offers reshuffle while keeping locked players, Copy Results, Download CSV, and Share Link. Live testing generated two teams successfully.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A strong team picker with balancing metadata and good outputs.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Reviewed with a live browser pass and sample names where the page allowed it.
ratingComments.design: Reflects observed page clarity, workflow, and surrounding clutter.
ratingComments.features: Skill/gender tags, pre-assignment, locked reshuffle, and balanced/random creation.
ratingComments.outputs: Copy, CSV download, and share permalink controls are available.
ratingComments.privacy: Google Analytics cookies observed; no account required.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required for the reviewed basic flow unless noted.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Useful balancing metadata makes it stronger than most pure randomizers.

tools.reviewNote: High-value addition for sports/classroom/team workflows.

tools.name: QuizQuestions.org Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Advanced no-account team generator with labels, locks, drag-and-drop, added teams, and exports
tools.summary: QuizQuestions.org Team Generator is one of the stronger reviewed tools. It accepts pasted players, creates teams, supports adding/renaming teams, locking players, drag-and-drop adjustments, advanced label/gender balancing, and an Export menu. The generated teams are stored in browser state and the page uses Cloudflare, but the tool itself is much more capable than most randomizers.
tools.pricing: Free web tool with optional site account links.
tools.overallComment: A strong classroom/game team maker with adjustment, balancing, and export features.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Paste names and generate quickly, then tweak with cards and teams.
ratingComments.design: Modern card interface with clear team outputs.
ratingComments.features: Locks, add/rename teams, drag/drop, labels, and gender/label balancing.
ratingComments.outputs: Export menu advertises CSV, print, and PDF options.
ratingComments.privacy: No account needed for use; Cloudflare and local state cookie were observed.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Sign-in links exist but are not required for basic use.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Supports balanced team sizes and label/gender balancing, though not full constraint solving.

tools.reviewNote: One of the best additions from this batch.

tools.name: Shuffly
tools.bestFor: Skill- and gender-balanced random teams with strong free exports
tools.summary: Shuffly is a polished step-by-step random team generator with meaningful balancing controls. Live testing entered participants with skill numbers and gender labels, advanced through the team setup step, enabled gender balance and same-surname splitting, generated three teams, and verified per-team skill totals plus export/copy controls. The free no-account flow supports manual paste entry, skill-based balancing, optional gender balance, same-surname split-apart behavior, custom team names, reshuffle, copy-as-text, PNG/image export, Excel export, and CSV export. Paid or signed-in features include file import, PDF export, saved participants/groups, saved team setups, and history. It does not handle multi-round repeat minimization, hard pair constraints, custom capacities, partial attendance, self-join, or solver-style diagnostics beyond team sizes and skill totals.
tools.pricing: Free plan supports up to 20 participants, 2 groups, manual entry, skill balancing, copy, PNG, Excel/CSV export, reshuffle, and 1 saved setup. Base is €3.99/month with 100 participants, 10 groups, file import, PDF export, and 5 saved setups. Premium is €7.99/month with 999 participants, 50 groups, unlimited saved setups, custom integrations, and advanced algorithms.
tools.overallComment: One of the stronger lightweight web tools: real skill/gender balancing and excellent free exports, but not a multi-round or constraint-heavy optimizer.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The three-step flow is clear, accepts one participant per line, parses skill/gender annotations, and worked without signup. File import is visible but paid, and advanced saved-data workflows require registration.
ratingComments.design: Modern, readable, and purpose-built. Parsed participants, team settings, options, results, and export controls are all presented clearly.
ratingComments.features: Strong for a lightweight team generator: skill balancing, gender balancing, same-surname split option, editable team names, manual paste entry, reshuffle, exports, saved participants/groups/setups for accounts, and paid file import/PDF. Missing hard arbitrary constraints, capacities, multi-round repeat planning, self-join, APIs, and deeper diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Excellent for the category. Live results exposed text copy plus visible image, PDF, XLSX, and CSV controls; free-plan copy/image/XLSX/CSV exports are advertised, while PDF is paid.
ratingComments.privacy: Better than ad-tech randomizer sites but not perfect. The no-account FAQ claims local/browser processing and no server storage for anonymous use. The privacy policy also mentions Firebase storage for logged-in data, Stripe for payments, Google Analytics, Microsoft Clarity, IP/location collection, and localStorage preferences.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account was needed for the tested generation flow. Registration is optional for saving participant libraries, groups, setup history, and account-based reloads.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Balances team size and skill totals and can incorporate gender and same-surname spread. It reports skill totals, but it does not optimize repeats, enforce arbitrary constraints, or expose solver quality metrics.

pros: Generated balanced teams successfully in live testing
Skill-based balancing from inline numeric ratings
Copy, image, Excel, and CSV outputs on the free plan
No account required for basic generation
Saved participants, groups, setups, and history for registered users
cons: Free plan limits shuffles to 20 participants
Excel/CSV file import and PDF export require paid plans
No custom group capacities or partial attendance
Uses analytics/Clarity and stores account data in Firebase for logged-in users

tools.name: Team Shake
tools.bestFor: Mobile-first balanced team generation for teachers and coaches
tools.summary: Team Shake is a real group generator, not just a spinner. It can create 1–64 teams, choose by number of teams or people per team, balance by strength or gender, handle absences, keep people together or apart, save rosters, import lists, and export finished teams. The tradeoff is that it is an app-store/mobile workflow with an old-looking interface, not a frictionless web tool.
tools.pricing: Paid app-store purchase; developer lists it at $0.99 USD, with actual pricing varying by platform/region. No separate Team Shake account or subscription is needed after purchase.
tools.overallComment: One of the stronger actual group generators on the list for single-session teams. It lacks multi-session/repeat optimization and modern web sharing, but its grouping controls are legitimately useful.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Basic use is simple once the app is installed: add names, choose number of teams or people per team, and generate. The advanced controls add setup work, and the app-store/mobile-only workflow is more friction than a browser tool.
ratingComments.design: Functional but dated. The app is clearly built around utility rather than polish. The mobile workflow is practical for teachers/coaches, but it does not feel modern.
ratingComments.features: Strong for group generation. It supports 1–64 teams, selection by team count or team size, balanced or random teams, strength balancing, gender balancing, separate male/female teams, equal-ability groups, absences, keep-together subteams, keep-apart oppose teams, saved rosters, configurable team names/colors, and list import/export. Missing the higher-end stuff: multi-session scheduling, repeat minimization, diagnostics, and arbitrary weighted constraints.
ratingComments.outputs: Very good output surface for a mobile app: share teams as text, image, CSV, or Excel; email/social sharing; print support; saved lists; backups for moving lists/settings between devices. It lacks web-native share pages, Zoom breakout export, and schedule-style outputs.
ratingComments.privacy: Strong. The app privacy policy says Rhine-o does not collect, store, or process user information; entered names are not shared with the developer; App Store privacy says data is not collected; and COPPA compliance is stated. Caveats are normal platform backup/sync services and user-triggered sharing through email/social apps.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No Team Shake account is needed, which is good. It is not an instant web tool: users need an app-store account/device, installation, and usually a paid app purchase.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Good single-session team quality. It can balance by strength/gender and honor keep-together/keep-apart rules where possible, which is far above plain random splitters. It does not optimize repeated sessions or provide solver diagnostics, so it is not in the same class as a multi-round optimizer.

pros: Can choose by team count or team size
cons: No quality diagnostics or pair matrix
tools.reviewNote: Review as a mobile team-generation app. Its feature depth should be credited because the group-generation controls are real, not just presentation extras.

tools.name: TeamMaker App
tools.bestFor: Local, no-account team making with strong classroom presentation and exports
tools.summary: TeamMaker App is a surprisingly capable browser-based team maker. Paste names or import a CSV, generate by members per team or number of teams, add together/apart constraints, manually drag people between teams, use projector mode, build seating plans, and export JPG/PDF or JSON backups. It stores data in the browser rather than requiring an account. It is not a repeat-minimizing or attribute-balancing optimizer, but for practical classroom/team setup it is one of the stronger no-account tools.
tools.pricing: Free, with a Ko-fi tip link. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: A strong practical team maker: local storage, constraints, projector mode, seating plans, and PDF/JPG/JSON exports. It lacks attribute balancing and repeat optimization, but it is far above normal random splitters.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The main workflow is easy: paste names, load roster, choose team mode/size, generate. The app has many extra panels, so it is more complex than one-button splitters, but the basics are clear.
ratingComments.design: Modern, responsive, and practical. The interface is busier than tiny randomizers because it includes rosters, teams, seating, export, projector mode, and saved teams.
ratingComments.features: Excellent practical feature depth for a free browser tool: CSV import, random picker, together/apart constraints, manual drag editing, staging, projector mode, seating plans, multiple saved teams, JSON backup/restore, and export. Missing advanced optimization like attributes and repeat minimization.
ratingComments.outputs: Very strong output surface: JPG, PDF, JSON backups, team JSON import/export, seating-plan JSON, and projector mode. It lacks spreadsheet/Zoom CSV export and a copy button.
ratingComments.privacy: Good for a hosted free tool because roster/team data is stored in the browser and no account is required. The page loads Google Analytics/Tag Manager, external CDNs, and Ko-fi resources, so it is not a fully private/offline tool.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup or login is needed.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Together/apart constraints and manual editing make results more usable than random splitters. It does not balance attributes or optimize repeated pairings across sessions.

pros: CSV import and pasted-name roster loading
Browser-local storage for rosters and teams
cons: No repeat avoidance across generated rounds
No spreadsheet or Zoom breakout CSV export
More complex than a tiny one-shot randomizer
tools.reviewNote: This is a strong no-account app and should not be grouped with weak one-page randomizers. Keep result-quality capped because the generation logic is random-with-constraints, not attribute balancing or repeat minimization.

tools.name: Instructron Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Teachers who want quick random groups with basic balancing and keep-apart rules
tools.summary: Instructron Group Generator is a surprisingly capable teacher-focused group maker. The free tool supports quick name entry, group size or number-of-groups mode, balanced-vs-similar performance grouping, keep-apart rules, keep-together preferences, drag-and-drop edits, locked groups, CSV export, and print/PDF output. Account/paid workflows unlock classroom import, saved plans, and richer student-profile signals such as interests, learning preferences, barriers, gender, and behavior.
tools.pricing: Free no-account group generator for quick manual use. Account creation is pushed for classroom import and saved/reusable plans; paid plans appear to unlock deeper student filters/profile signals and broader Instructron classroom features.
tools.overallComment: One of the better modern classroom group generators. It is not a multi-session repeat optimizer, and some of the best profile-based features sit behind the account/upgrade path, but the free tool already beats normal random splitters.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The basic flow is clear: add names, choose group size or group count, optionally set levels and rules, then generate. There are more controls than a simple splitter, but they are relevant and mostly understandable.
ratingComments.design: Modern, readable, and teacher-oriented. The page is much more polished than most utility randomizers, with sensible controls and result cards. The upgrade prompts are visible but not overwhelming.
ratingComments.features: Strong for a classroom grouping tool: group size/count modes, performance-level strategy, keep-apart rules, keep-together preferences, drag-and-drop edits, locked groups, manual add/remove groups, CSV export, print/PDF, initials-only and neutral-color display. Advanced profile signals and classroom import are gated behind account/upgrade paths. No multi-session repeat minimization or pair matrix.
ratingComments.outputs: Good output surface: CSV export, print/PDF, editable result cards, initials-only display, neutral-color display, and local/account plan saving in the broader workflow. No Zoom breakout export or public share link.
ratingComments.privacy: Better than most free web tools. The policy says no selling, no targeted advertising, no student-data model training, minimal student identifiers, encryption, subprocessors, and teacher controls. The page uses analytics.instructron.com and Cloudflare, and account/classroom use stores roster/profile/activity data, so it is not a local-only privacy story.
ratingComments.accountFriction: The quick group generator works without an account, which is good. Classroom import, saved plans, and richer profile-based grouping push users toward signup and paid plans.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Clearly above plain random splitting because it can balance or cluster by performance level, honor keep-apart rules, prefer keep-together pairs, and use profile signals in upgraded workflows. Not a social-golfer-style repeat optimizer and likely heuristic rather than a transparent solver.

pros: Stronger privacy posture than ad-heavy randomizers
cons: Best roster/profile features require account or upgrade
No pair matrix or repeat diagnostics
Hosted classroom data if using full workflow
Heuristic quality is not very transparent
tools.reviewNote: Review the group-generator tool, not the whole Instructron AI/classroom platform. Credit the real grouping controls, but keep multi-session/repeat-optimization separate.

tools.name: BuddyMatcher
tools.bestFor: Teachers who need pairs/trios, local saved lists, and optional paid control features
tools.summary: Buddy Matcher is one of the stronger tools in this part of the list. The free tool supports pasted names, pairs/trios, avoiding the last grouping where possible, random picking, locally saved lists, copy, download, share tool, and print. Pro adds larger/custom group sizes, do-not-group pairs, leader spreading, saved lists, CSV export, and print clean layouts for a one-off £3.99. It is not a full multi-session solver, but it has real group-making controls and a refreshingly low tracker footprint.
tools.pricing: Free basic tool. Buddy Matcher Pro is advertised as a £3.99 one-off upgrade for larger/custom groups, keep-apart pairs, leaders, saved lists, export, and print layouts.
tools.overallComment: A genuinely good lightweight group maker. The free tier is limited, but privacy, output controls, and paid constraint features are better than most random splitters.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The quick path is clear, though the add-names/generate flow is a little more button-heavy than the simplest tools.
ratingComments.design: Clean, teacher-friendly, and focused. Not flashy, but much better than utility-site clutter.
ratingComments.features: Good lightweight depth: pairs/trios, avoid last grouping, random picker, local saved lists, copy/download/print, plus Pro keep-apart pairs, leaders, larger/custom groups, export, and saved lists.
ratingComments.outputs: Strong for this tier: copy, download, print, and Pro CSV/clean layouts.
ratingComments.privacy: Strong. The page claims local browser storage for names and no account for free use; no obvious third-party scripts were loaded on the tested page.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Free use needs no account. Pro/payment is required for almost anything, even creating groups of 4 or more people.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Above normal random splitters because it can avoid recent repeats where possible and Pro adds keep-apart/leaders. Not a full optimizer.

cons: Groups of 4+ and custom sizes are Pro
tools.reviewNote: This is closer to a controlled classroom grouping tool than a generic randomizer, but several useful controls are Pro-only.

tools.name: Calcbe Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Privacy-conscious one-session random teams with simple constraints and strong exports
tools.summary: CalcBE Team Generator is one of the better simple random team tools. It runs locally in the browser, uses crypto.getRandomValues in secure mode, supports split-by-team-count or team-size, deduplication, seeded reproducible mode, keep-together and avoid-pair constraints, custom team naming, copy, TXT/CSV/JSON downloads, per-team copy, and settings-only share URLs. It is a one-session random team generator, not a balancing or repeat-optimization solver.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: Strong for a simple random team generator: local processing, real constraint options, reproducible mode, and excellent exports. It does not solve multi-session or attribute-balancing problems.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The basic flow is easy: paste names, choose team count or team size, generate. Advanced options add complexity but are hidden well enough.
ratingComments.design: Clean, modern, and information-dense without being chaotic. The cookie banner exists, but the actual tool UI is solid.
ratingComments.features: Very good for a one-session random team tool: secure CSPRNG mode, seeded reproducibility, deduplication, team count/size modes, keep-together and avoid-pair constraints, custom team names, and settings-only share URLs. No multi-round repeat optimization, attendance by session, attribute balancing, or pair matrix.
ratingComments.outputs: Excellent output surface for this tier: copy all, copy each team, download TXT/CSV/JSON, clear results, and copy a settings URL that excludes names by default.
ratingComments.privacy: Good for roster privacy because names/results run locally and are not uploaded. The site has consent-based analytics/advertising cookies, Google tags, and an ad/cookie policy, so it is not completely tracker-free.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Above plain random splitting because avoid/keep constraints and seeded/security modes are real. It is a bounded-retry random assignment, not a full optimizer for balance or repeats.

cons: Constraints are best-effort, not a full solver guarantee
tools.reviewNote: Playwright test generated 3 teams from 9 names and verified copy/download controls. Keep/avoid constraints are best-effort, so constraint features are marked partial rather than hard solver-grade.

tools.name: Random Roster
tools.bestFor: Teacher group generator with capability balance, preferences, history, and seating charts
tools.summary: Random Roster is a classroom group generator for teachers. It offers no-signup quick groups, capability-balanced groups, saved rosters/history, keep-apart and always-together preferences, drag adjustments, presentation mode, random name picker, and seating-chart features. The free quick generator is easy, while the richer classroom workflow uses accounts and a one-time Pro upgrade.
tools.pricing: Free forever tier with no-signup quick grouping. Pro Lifetime is listed as a one-time $19 upgrade for unlimited rosters, constraints, CSV import/export, presentation mode, seating charts, and share links.
tools.overallComment: A strong classroom-focused group generator. Capability balancing, preferences, history, and seating-chart workflow put it above ordinary randomizers.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Quick no-signup grouping is easy; the full classroom workflow has more setup.
ratingComments.design: Polished teacher-focused product UI.
ratingComments.features: Capability balance, preferences, history, presentation, and seating charts are substantial.
ratingComments.outputs: CSV import/export and share links are Pro; presentation and print workflows are useful.
ratingComments.privacy: Student data can be saved/synced in the product, so privacy depends on account use.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Quick grouping is no-signup; many strongest features are account/Pro features.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Capability balancing and student preferences should outperform pure random grouping.

tools.reviewNote: No-signup quick grouping exists, but the strongest classroom features are tied to saved roster/account/Pro workflows.

tools.name: Randomify Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Well-rounded team generator with shareable parameters, captain mode, themed team names, copy, and CSV download
tools.summary: Randomify Team Generator is a polished random team tool. It accepts pasted names, supports number-of-teams or people-per-team modes, offers themed team names, has an optional captain mode, produces shareable links, keeps recent results, and provides Copy plus Download CSV controls. It remains random-only, but its output and sharing features are notably strong for a free web randomizer.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A top-tier basic randomizer with copy, CSV, sharing, recent results, and captain mode.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Clear roster textarea, mode selector, presets, and generate button.
ratingComments.design: Polished card-based output with useful metadata.
ratingComments.features: Count/size modes, team-name themes, captain mode, and recent results.
ratingComments.outputs: Copy, Download CSV, and shareable links are available.
ratingComments.privacy: No account and limited observed tracking, though analytics are present.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Random teams only, with no balancing constraints.

cons: Prefilled URL can expose roster names if shared carelessly
tools.reviewNote: The supplied URL includes prefilled entries/parameters; the underlying tool is worth listing.

tools.name: RandomPicker Random Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Advanced browser-side teams with CSV/TXT import, locks, exclusions, skills, copy, and TXT download
tools.summary: RandomPicker Random Team Generator is one of the most capable tools in this batch. Live testing pasted eight names, selected three teams, clicked Generate Teams, and produced balanced team cards containing the tested names. The UI supports typing, pasting, or dropping .txt/.csv files; grouping by number of teams or team size; numbered or named-preset team names; advanced rules for locks, exclusion pairs, and skill levels; card/list views; Copy Results; Download TXT; Reset Settings; and Reshuffle Same Teams. The page claims client-side processing and crypto random values. It still does not cover multi-session repeat minimization, Zoom/Excel exports, pair matrices, or solver diagnostics, but it is meaningfully stronger than simple randomizers.
tools.pricing: Free web tool; no account required in tested flow.
tools.overallComment: A strong browser-side random team generator with real advanced rules and usable outputs, though not a multi-round optimizer.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Main paste-and-generate flow worked smoothly; advanced rules add complexity only when opened.
ratingComments.design: Modern, structured UI with cards/list views and clear setup sections.
ratingComments.features: File import, team count/team size, named presets, locks, exclusion pairs, and skill balancing are significant for a free randomizer.
ratingComments.outputs: Copy Results and Download TXT are available. No CSV/Excel/PDF/Zoom export.
ratingComments.privacy: Claims client-side generation and local behavior, but source includes Google/analytics strings and localStorage/cookies.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Balanced team sizes plus optional skill/exclusion/lock rules; no repeat-minimization diagnostics.

cons: No pair matrix or detailed diagnostics

tools.name: Smart Group Maker
tools.bestFor: Free visual group maker with random mode and attribute-balanced custom grouping
tools.summary: Smart Group Maker is a dedicated group-making app landing page. It advertises simple random grouping, custom groups based on attributes such as genders, skills, and interests, a two-type balance sample, and a 2D canvas for arranging groups in a room-like layout. The launch flow was a landing/sample app rather than a quick textarea test, but the product is clearly in scope and more ambitious than basic randomizers.
tools.pricing: Free web app. No signup or install required according to the page.
tools.overallComment: A promising visual group maker with attribute-balance positioning.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Reviewed with live page controls and an eight-name sample where possible.
ratingComments.design: Reflects the observed page layout, clarity, and surrounding clutter.
ratingComments.features: Random groups, custom groups, attribute metadata, two-type balance, and 2D canvas layout are described.
ratingComments.outputs: Output/export controls were not verified during the landing-page review.
ratingComments.privacy: No account required claim; Google Tag Manager observed.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required for the reviewed basic flow unless noted.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Attribute balancing makes it stronger than pure randomizers, though full solver behavior was not deeply tested.

tools.reviewNote: One of the stronger relevant finds in this batch.

tools.name: Teach Arcade Group Maker
tools.bestFor: Teacher group maker with repeat-avoidance history, print/share, and pairing records
tools.summary: Teach Arcade Group Maker accepts class names, supports grouping by size or number of groups, and generated four groups successfully. It includes Share Link, Print, Record Today’s Pairings, Reset Pairing History, and an Avoid prior pairings feature that uses anonymous browser history to reduce repeated partnerships. This makes it stronger than most one-off classroom randomizers.
tools.pricing: Free ad-supported web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A strong classroom group maker with session/history-based repeat avoidance.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Reviewed with live page controls and an eight-name sample where possible.
ratingComments.design: Reflects the observed page layout, clarity, and surrounding clutter.
ratingComments.features: By-size/by-number grouping, avoid prior pairings, pairing records, and history reset.
ratingComments.outputs: Share link and print controls are available.
ratingComments.privacy: Ads/analytics/fonts were observed; history is stored in browser according to the page.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required for the reviewed basic flow unless noted.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Repeat-avoidance history makes it stronger than pure randomizers, though not a full multi-session optimizer.

tools.reviewNote: High-value classroom addition.

tools.name: Team Randomizer Classroom Generator
tools.bestFor: Classroom team randomizer with participant/team uploads, seed, CSV, screenshot, and sharing
tools.summary: Team Randomizer supports participant and team entry from typed CSV lists or uploaded .txt/.csv files, optional random seed for reproducible assignments, named teams, randomization, CSV output, screenshot output, and sharing. It is a classroom-focused direct tool.
tools.pricing: Free ad-supported web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A featureful classroom randomizer with reproducibility and outputs.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Reviewed with a live browser pass and sample names where the page allowed it.
ratingComments.design: Reflects observed page clarity, workflow, and surrounding clutter.
ratingComments.features: Participant/team file upload, named teams, and optional random seed.
ratingComments.outputs: CSV, screenshot, and share controls are visible.
ratingComments.privacy: Google Ads/Analytics and PHP session cookies observed.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required for the reviewed basic flow unless noted.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Random assignment only; no full multi-session optimizer.

cons: No generated output captured after incomplete setup
tools.reviewNote: Good classroom output/reproducibility coverage.

tools.name: TeamMixer
tools.bestFor: Korean AI-assisted team builder with tiers, positions, presets, and no-server-data claim
tools.summary: TeamMixer is a Korean AI-assisted team builder for sports, games, classrooms, and board games. It supports participant entry, team count, presets, AI fill, tier balance, position randomization, and claims no server data transfer. It is more ambitious than a simple randomizer, especially for sports/esports team balancing, though English support and export details are limited.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: An ambitious AI/team-balance tool with tiers and position options.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Reviewed with a pasted eight-name sample where possible; the main controls were understandable.
ratingComments.design: Usability reflects the observed page layout and surrounding clutter.
ratingComments.features: Presets, AI fill, tier balancing, position randomization, and team count controls.
ratingComments.outputs: Output export was not verified; primary focus is generated team composition.
ratingComments.privacy: No account and page claims data is not sent to a server, though ads/analytics were observed.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account was required for the reviewed basic flow unless otherwise noted.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Supports tier/position balancing beyond pure random splitting, but not full constraint solving.

tools.reviewNote: Useful for Korean-speaking users and sports/esports team balancing.

tools.name: UTEKAR Random Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Advanced browser-side team generation with attributes, seed, exports, and multi-round repeat reduction
tools.summary: UTEKAR Random Team Generator is one of the stronger free browser tools in this batch. It accepts line or CSV-style participant input, parses optional attributes such as role, group, and weight, supports by-count and by-size modes, optional uneven sizes, keep-groups-together logic, deterministic seeds, attribute balancing, custom team names/themes, multiple rounds, and repeat-pairing reduction. Generated results include Copy, CSV, and JSON outputs. It is not as transparent as a dedicated solver and has no deep scoring report or pair matrix, but it meaningfully goes beyond plain random splitting.
tools.pricing: Free. No account is required. The page includes an upsell banner for custom software/MVP work.
tools.overallComment: A genuinely capable free team generator with constraints, attributes, seed, multi-round options, and exports.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Easy for simple lists, with a denser advanced panel for attributes, seeds, rounds, and team names.
ratingComments.design: Clean modern tool layout. The parsed-list and settings controls are compact but readable.
ratingComments.features: Strong for a free browser tool: CSV/line input, attributes, weights, group hints, seed, balancing, custom team names, multiple rounds, repeat-pairing reduction, copy/CSV/JSON exports, and manual adjustment claims.
ratingComments.outputs: Copy, CSV, and JSON output buttons were visible after generation.
ratingComments.privacy: No account is required and the workflow appears browser-side, but Google Tag Manager/Analytics loaded and an upsell banner is present.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup or login is needed.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Better than plain random because it can balance attributes, honor group hints, use seeds, and reduce repeat pairings across rounds. It does not expose a full solver score report or pair matrix.

cons: Advanced options may confuse casual users
tools.reviewNote: The page documentation claims drag/drop manual adjustment and lock members; these were not deeply retested beyond visible/generated output and controls.

tools.name: WebToolNexus Random Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Skill-aware random teams with balance report and text export
tools.summary: WebToolNexus is much stronger than the average random splitter. It supports team-count or team-size modes, optional skill levels and roles, skill-balanced snake-draft assignment, sample data, reshuffling, copyable teams, and an exported text report with balance information. It is still not a multi-round constraint solver, but it is a credible lightweight team balancer.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. The site presents itself as no-account and no-tracking.
tools.overallComment: A genuinely useful lightweight team generator. Skill balancing and diagnostics make it stand out among simple web tools.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Adding people individually is slower than paste-first tools, but sample data and controls are clear.
ratingComments.design: Clean utility UI with a lot of surrounding navigation.
ratingComments.features: Skill levels, roles, snake draft, team-size mode, and balance reporting are real depth.
ratingComments.outputs: Copy teams and export a formatted report; no spreadsheet-specific CSV export seen.
ratingComments.privacy: No observed third-party requests in the tested page and a no-tracking positioning.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Better than random-only tools because it can balance skill and explain imbalance.

cons: No bulk textarea paste workflow visible
No hard keep-together or keep-apart rules
No repeat avoidance or multi-round planning
tools.reviewNote: Roles are captured as labels; the meaningful balancing feature is skill-based snake draft.

tools.name: Classroomscreen Group Maker
tools.bestFor: Teachers already using Classroomscreen who want visual classroom groups, saved name lists, exclusions, and manual adjustment
tools.summary: Classroomscreen Group Maker is a real classroom widget rather than a disposable randomizer page. It supports student name lists, manual entry, group size/group count settings, editable group names and colors, rotating groups, auto-generated names, drag-and-drop movement between groups, and keep-apart/prioritize rules through name-list settings. It is much more classroom-useful than basic splitters, but it lives inside the Classroomscreen platform and saving/reusing workflows depend on Basic/Pro account features.
tools.pricing: Basic is free and includes all widgets plus saving up to three name lists. Pro is about $36/year and adds saving/sharing screens, folders, unlimited name lists, custom backgrounds, widget themes, and other classroom-screen features.
tools.overallComment: A strong classroom grouping widget with meaningful teacher controls. Not a multi-session optimizer, and the platform/account context adds friction, but it is much more serious than basic random splitters.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The widget is approachable for teachers, but using it as a group maker means entering the Classroomscreen app/settings flow rather than a single standalone form.
ratingComments.design: Excellent classroom-first visual design. This is built to be projected and used during lessons.
ratingComments.features: Strong for classroom grouping: saved name lists, group count/size controls, editable names/colors, rotate groups, drag/drop movement, and keep-apart/prioritize rules. No multi-round repeat optimizer or pair matrix.
ratingComments.outputs: Good visual display and screen-saving workflow, but not a strong export tool. No clear CSV/spreadsheet/PDF group export surface for results.
ratingComments.privacy: Much better than ad-funded utilities: no ads, explicit no-sale language, EU company, and clear processor/security disclosures. Student names can be stored when using accounts, so it is not local-only.
ratingComments.accountFriction: A no-account app flow exists, but saved name lists and practical reuse require Basic/Pro account features; Pro is needed for saving/sharing screens beyond the free limits.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Above random splitters because teacher exclusions/priorities and manual drag/drop adjustment materially improve classroom assignments. Not solver-grade repeat minimization.

cons: Account needed for saved list workflows
Student names may be stored in account workflows
tools.reviewNote: Scope this to the Group Maker widget, not the whole Classroomscreen classroom dashboard.

tools.name: digtools Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Browser-side teams with skills, NG pairs, leaders, save/load, CSV, text, and image output
tools.summary: digtools Team Generator is a capable browser-side team generator. It accepts names, supports number-of-teams and team-size modes, has advanced options for skill balancing, exclusion pairs/NG pairs, leader or organizer picks, seed/fixed assignment concepts, local saved lists, reshuffle, text copy, CSV export, and image export. tools.name: Zapadisk Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Balancing sport/player teams by level, gender, and multi-day attendance
tools.summary: Zapadisk Team Generator is a surprisingly serious browser-side optimizer for player/team balancing. Instead of only shuffling names, it expects spreadsheet-style rows with name, numeric level, gender, and one or more attendance-day columns, then runs repeated local-search swaps to minimize differences across team size by day, gender, mean level, and gender-by-day. Live testing with generated sample data produced balanced teams plus summaries, team differences, score, run count, and swap statistics. The tradeoff is usability and output: the UI is old, input format is strict, output is page-only text, and there are no modern import/export buttons.
tools.pricing: Free open-source web tool. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: A niche but genuinely smarter team balancer. It is rough and export-light, but much more assignment-aware than ordinary random team splitters.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Powerful but not beginner-simple. Users must prepare spreadsheet rows in the exact name/level/gender/attendance-day format and understand runs, team count, and objective weights.
ratingComments.design: Old, sparse Materialize-style page. It is readable and transparent, but not polished, mobile-first, or friendly compared with modern group tools.
ratingComments.features: Strong for its niche: spreadsheet paste, numeric level balancing, gender balancing, attendance-by-day balancing, weighted criteria, local-search runs, generated sample data, score/difference diagnostics, and hacky together/apart control through attendance values. No generic attributes, custom capacities, saved rosters, multi-round schedules, or modern result editing.
ratingComments.outputs: Results, summaries, and diagnostics are displayed on the page, but there is no copy button, CSV/spreadsheet export, print/PDF, share link, Zoom export, or saved result history.
ratingComments.privacy: Good but not perfect. The optimizer appears to run locally in the browser and the project is open source, with no observed generation API upload. The page still loads Google Analytics and has no clear privacy policy on the tool page.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account, signup, or payment is needed.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Much better than random-only splitters for its target use case because it optimizes level, gender, attendance per day, and gender per day, then reports remaining differences and score. It is not a broad constraint solver.

tools.name: GroupEng
tools.bestFor: Open-source command-line grouping with distribute, aggregate, cluster, and balance rules
tools.summary: GroupEng is an old but serious open-source Python grouping tool, not a modern web app. It works from CSV-style class data and a text description of group size plus ordered grouping rules. Its operators are meaningful for real assignment quality: Distribute attributes across groups, Aggregate people with matching attributes, Cluster underrepresented students so they are not isolated, and Balance numeric scores such as GPA or pretest. It outputs groupings plus statistics for how well rules were met. The tradeoff is usability: it requires downloading Python code and writing input/config files, and the site describes it as experimental and rough.
tools.pricing: Free and open source under AGPLv3.

ratingComments.design: Visually more engaging than most utility randomizers, especially for live reveals. The downside is that the interface is busy, ad-supported, and cluttered compared with focused group-planning tools.

It is not a serious multi-session optimizer.
ratingComments.outputs: Very strong output options for this category: CSV download, result image export/share, result links, share links, and browser/cloud file storage. No Zoom-specific export, Excel-native export, or true solver diagnostics.
ratingComments.privacy: Bad for sensitive rosters. The tool uses Google Analytics plus AdThrive/CafeMedia advertising infrastructure and a consent-manager stack. The privacy policy says user content/inputs, IP address, device/software identifiers, analytics data, advertising data, and share-link data may be collected; share links include entries and are stored in an online database.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Basic host grouping works without an account. Persistent cloud file storage, subscriber storage, and some self-join/file workflows push users toward login/subscriber storage, so it is not frictionless.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Better than pure random-only splitters because it supports equal groups, gender/label balancing, simple preset together/separate rules, and representative picking. Fundamentally a single-session randomizer, not a repeat-minimizing or constraint-optimizing scheduler.

cons: Advanced storage/self-join features push toward account/cloud use
Busy interface compared with simple list splitters

tools.name: Playpass Golf Schedule Maker
tools.bestFor: Golf rotating-partner and round-robin schedules
tools.summary: Playpass has a genuinely usable free golf schedule generator, not just a marketing page. Live testing created an eight-golfer rotating-partner schedule through the public form without signing in, producing 14 pair-vs-pair games plus standings, sharing, draft publishing, and score-editing controls. It is useful for league-style golf rotations, but it is not a broad group assignment solver: it does not import a normal roster spreadsheet, optimize arbitrary constraints, or explain repeat/fairness quality beyond the generated schedule.

ratingComments.outputs: The generated schedule page, share/publish controls, standings, and game list are much better than display-only tools, though CSV/PDF export was not the primary visible flow in the tested draft.
ratingComments.privacy: Participant labels and schedules are hosted by Playpass; the draft can exist without an account, but this is not a local/private tool.
ratingComments.accountFriction: The tested generator created a usable draft without signup. Broader management and publishing workflows push users toward the platform.
ratingComments.resultQuality: For its narrow rotating-partner/round-robin use case, the schedule is coherent and complete. It does not provide solver diagnostics or broad fairness controls.

tools.name: TeamGenerator.net Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Sports and game teams balanced by skill ratings and roles
tools.summary: TeamGenerator.net is a sports/game-oriented team balancer rather than a plain paste-and-shuffle page. Live testing added twelve players with 1–5 star skill ratings and roles, generated three teams of four, and produced evenly balanced averages with role distribution across teams. It supports by-number-of-teams and by-players-per-team modes, role/position input, local saved lists, import/export for saved lists, Copy All, per-team copy, and Generate Again. The downside is that participants must be added one by one; there is no simple bulk paste roster workflow, no CSV/spreadsheet export, no repeat planning, and privacy is weakened by Google Analytics/AdSense/Funding Choices.
tools.pricing: Free ad-supported web tool. No account is required.

ratingComments.features: Above-average for sports/game teams: skill ratings, roles/positions, by-team-count and by-players-per-team modes, local saved lists, import/export, Copy All, per-team copy, and regenerate. Missing bulk paste, arbitrary attributes, hard constraints, repeat planning, saved result history, and deeper diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Copy All and per-team copy are useful, and saved-list import/export helps with rosters. There is no CSV/spreadsheet result export, TXT/JSON download, image/PDF, Zoom export, share link, or report format.
ratingComments.privacy: Poor. ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup, login, or payment is needed.
