tools.summary: RandAnimal Random Group Generator accepts a group title, a list of names, and a number of groups. Live testing generated Group Alpha/Beta assignments and the page offers Find, Load Example, Save, New, and Print controls.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A useful basic group generator with save/print niceties.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Reviewed with a live browser pass and an eight-name sample where possible.
ratingComments.design: Reflects observed page clarity, workflow, and surrounding clutter.
ratingComments.features: Group title, names input, group count, find, autosave, and examples.
ratingComments.outputs: Save and Print are available; exact save format not verified.
ratingComments.privacy: PHP session and StatCounter cookies observed.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required for the reviewed basic flow unless noted.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Random assignment only; no full multi-session optimizer.

tools.reviewNote: Solid basic randomizer coverage.

tools.name: Random-Group-Generator.com
tools.bestFor: Single-session browser grouping with copy/export/share controls and lightweight balance modes
tools.summary: Random-Group-Generator.com is much more capable than a bare random splitter, but the marketing oversells it. The actual tool supports pasted names, file upload, normal/level/gender modes, group-count control, local-processing messaging, regenerate, auto-balance, copy result, export, share, drag-and-drop refinement, login-gated saving, and a simple group-analysis score. That is a decent one-session workflow. It is not, however, a serious multi-session repeat-minimization solver, and the page has Google Ads, Google Tag Manager, Microsoft Clarity, and a Funding Choices consent stack.
tools.pricing: Free for basic generation. Saving/history uses login. The page advertises optional sync/account features rather than a clearly priced paid plan.
tools.overallComment: A genuinely useful one-session group generator with good outputs and some balancing controls, held back by ad-tech/privacy issues and marketing claims that go beyond the visible product.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The basic flow is straightforward: paste names, set group count, generate. Extra mode/export/save controls are visible without making the quick path too hard.
ratingComments.design: Modern, readable, and more product-like than most randomizer pages. The long marketing page and ad/consent layer make it feel less clean.
ratingComments.features: Good for one session: file upload, normal/level/gender modes, regenerate, auto-balance, drag-and-drop refinement, analysis score, export, share, and login-gated history. No verified multi-round repeat optimization or serious constraint solver.
ratingComments.outputs: Strong output surface: copy result, export, share, and login-gated saving/history are available from the result panel. Not a verified Zoom-specific CSV workflow despite the marketing copy.
ratingComments.privacy: Mixed to bad. The tool says names are processed locally and the privacy policy says saved/account data is handled for sync, but the page also loads Google Ads, Funding Choices, Google Tag Manager, and Microsoft Clarity.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Basic generation works without login. Saving/history and some reuse workflows are account-gated.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Better than plain random splitting because balance modes, auto-balance, drag-and-drop refinement, and analysis exist. Not comparable to a repeat-optimization or hard-constraint solver.

cons: Some marketing claims exceed the visible tool
Google Ads, Funding Choices, GTM, and Microsoft Clarity
No clearly documented pricing for account features
tools.reviewNote: The visible product is a useful browser group generator, but many deeper claims on the page read like roadmap or marketing copy rather than proven solver capability.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Reviewed with a pasted eight-name sample where possible; the main controls were understandable.

ratingComments.features: Character UI, member entry, team count, add member, share, and API endpoints.

ratingComments.accountFriction: No account was required for the reviewed basic flow unless otherwise noted.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Random grouping only; no full optimizer.

tools.name: Spin of Wheel Team Generator

tools.summary: Spin of Wheel Team Generator accepts pasted names, a number of teams, and generates team cards with member counts. It provides Copy All and emphasizes balanced team sizes and instant regeneration. It is a nice basic randomizer, but not an advanced planner.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A polished basic team generator with copy-all output.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Reviewed with a pasted eight-name sample where possible; the main controls were understandable.
ratingComments.design: Usability reflects the observed page layout and surrounding clutter.

tools.name: Sport Scheduler Pro Free Schedule Generator
tools.bestFor: Free round-robin team schedules with links and exports
tools.summary: Sport Scheduler Pro is a legitimate self-serve schedule generator. Live testing entered six team names, configured an eight-week schedule, and generated a hosted schedule with admin/public links, CSV and PDF controls, week-by-week games, rosters, scores, standings, venues, and schedule management tabs. It deserves inclusion because it freely generates group/team schedules, but it is a league scheduler, not a general participant group optimizer.
tools.pricing: Free schedule generator with no signup required for the tested workflow. Pro features are advertised for deeper league management, extra edits, and larger/managed workflows.
tools.overallComment: Strong for free league round-robin schedule generation; only moderately relevant for people who need arbitrary workshop/classroom grouping.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Entering teams and generating a schedule was straightforward. The post-generation admin surface is large because it is a league product.
ratingComments.design: Readable modern interface with clear steps and generated weeks, though there are many Pro prompts and management tabs.
ratingComments.features: Team count, custom names, weeks, dates/times, public/admin links, rosters, scores, standings, venues, and schedule tabs are useful. It lacks general grouping constraints.
ratingComments.outputs: CSV, PDF, copyable admin/public links, public schedule pages, and week-by-week schedule views are strong for a free generator.
ratingComments.privacy: Schedules and team names are hosted. That is normal for league sharing but weaker than local/browser-only generators.
ratingComments.accountFriction: The tested schedule was created without signup. Some editing/management features are locked behind Pro.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Round-robin output was coherent for teams. It does not optimize arbitrary participant attributes, repeat goals beyond the schedule format, or constraints.

pros: No signup required for tested schedule creation
cons: League schedules only, not general participant grouping
No roster spreadsheet import in the tested flow
tools.reviewNote: Live test entered six named teams, generated an eight-week schedule, and observed admin/public links plus CSV and PDF controls on the generated schedule page.

tools.name: WebRandom Random Team Generator
tools.bestFor: No-account random teams with custom names, copy, text download, and share link
tools.summary: WebRandom Random Team Generator is a strong simple team splitter. Live testing pasted twelve names, set three teams, and generated three balanced teams of four. It supports number-of-teams or members-per-team modes, optional custom team names, Copy All Teams, per-team copy, Download as Text, Share Link, reshuffle, and clear. It does not offer constraints, attributes, repeat minimization, saved rosters, or diagnostics, but it is clean and low-friction for one-off random teams.
tools.pricing: Free. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: One of the better simple random splitters thanks to copy/download/share controls and a light footprint.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Paste, choose split mode/count, and generate. The workflow is direct.
ratingComments.design: Clean, focused tool with readable result cards.
ratingComments.features: Two split modes, custom team names, reshuffle, and share are useful for a basic splitter.
ratingComments.outputs: Copy all, per-team copy, text download, and share link were visible after generation.
ratingComments.privacy: Only WebRandom and Google Fonts hosts were observed; the page claims names are not stored.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup or login is needed.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Balanced random teams only.

pros: By team count or members per team
tools.reviewNote: Live test generated three teams of four and exposed copy/download/share controls.

tools.name: Wooclap Team Picker
tools.bestFor: Polished simple random teams with strong export options
tools.summary: This review is only about Wooclap's free group generator, not Wooclap's main audience-engagement product. As a standalone group maker it is one of the best simple random splitters: polished, no-account, and unusually strong on exports. The grouping logic itself uses basic random balanced teams, not real optimization.
tools.pricing: Free standalone group-generator page. No account is required for this specific free team picker; do not confuse it with Wooclap’s broader paid audience-engagement product.
tools.overallComment: One of the best simple random group makers: polished, no-account, and excellent exports. The assignment logic uses basic random grouping, not real group optimization.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Very easy to use: paste names, choose number of teams or participants per team, and generate. Visible login/signup/product-marketing buttons keep it just shy of the cleanest tiny utilities.
ratingComments.design: One of the best-designed simple tools in this list. Clean, modern, readable, and much nicer than the older utility pages, though some Wooclap marketing remains.
ratingComments.features: Better than a bare random splitter: team leaders, random/editable team names, two split modes, shuffle/regenerate, and local session restore. No constraints, repeat avoidance, balancing rules, or multi-round planning.
ratingComments.outputs: Excellent output options for this category: CSV, JPG, PNG, PDF, and copy to clipboard. Not full marks because it is a simple export menu, not integration-grade output.
ratingComments.privacy: Much better than the ad-heavy tools. In testing the free group-generator page was light, and Wooclap has a serious GDPR/ISO 27001 privacy posture. Not 5/5 because participant names are stored in browser localStorage and Wooclap is a hosted product with broader analytics, marketing, and cookie policies.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required. Login and signup are visible, but the group maker works without them.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Random balanced groups only, with a small practical lift from leader and team-name handling. Fine for one-off splits, but no constraints, repeat reduction, or quality diagnostics.

pros: Team leaders and editable/random team names
cons: No advanced rules or repeat reduction
Names are kept in browser localStorage
tools.reviewNote: Review scope: the free group generator page specifically, not Wooclap as a full audience-engagement software product.

tools.name: Any One Will Do
tools.bestFor: Privacy-friendly browser-only random picker and group drafter
tools.summary: Any One Will Do is a privacy-focused random picker and group generator. It runs in the browser, has no ads, no account, import/export controls, saved local sessions, and a draft-style group assignment workflow. It is more of an interactive randomizer than an automatic group optimizer, but it fits classrooms, meetups, and games well.
tools.pricing: Free browser tool. No account required; support/donation links are separate.
tools.overallComment: A tasteful, privacy-friendly group randomizer. The draft workflow is less direct than one-click team generators, but the privacy posture is strong.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Powerful but less obvious than paste-and-generate tools.
ratingComments.design: Polished and distinctive.
ratingComments.features: Import/export, sessions, absent/sitting-out style workflows, and picker modes are useful.
ratingComments.outputs: Import/export and session sharing are useful, though not spreadsheet-specific.
ratingComments.privacy: Strong no-ads/no-account/browser-only positioning; lightweight analytics domains were observed.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Fair random assignment, but no balancing constraints or diagnostics.

pros: Random picker plus group generator modes
cons: Group generation is less direct than standard team splitters
tools.reviewNote: The group workflow is draft-style rather than a conventional generated team table.

It accepts one name per line, optionally reads numeric skill scores from comma-separated names, creates teams by count or team size, offers random or skill-balanced modes, supports team-name templates and color styles, and can save/copy generated team configurations.
tools.pricing: Free ad-supported web tool. No account required for the generator.
tools.overallComment: A useful mid-tier random team maker. Skill-balanced mode and saved configurations put it above pure random splitters, but it is still a simple single-round tool.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Paste names, choose method, choose balancing mode, and generate. Skill scores use a simple comma format.

ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.

tools.summary: DigitaleTools Groepjesmaker is a Dutch random group maker for teachers. tools.name: FastRounds
tools.bestFor: Small free speed-networking round rotation events
tools.summary: FastRounds provides a working no-signup host flow for speed-networking and team-building rotations. Live testing solved the simple captcha, created a free host event, and reached a host dashboard with a code, maximum rounds, time-per-round, true round-robin versus half-room-fixed matching, manual/automatic round starts, transition timing, event sounds, and optional post-event messaging. The free version is capped at six attendees, so it is useful as a small live rotation tool or trial rather than a broad group generator.
tools.pricing: Free host event with no signup or credit card for up to six attendees. Larger events require paid event packs or subscriptions.
tools.overallComment: A real self-serve live rotation tool with a strict free cap and limited static export/reporting value.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Creating the host event was quick once the anti-spam question was answered. Running a live event requires attendees to join by code.
ratingComments.design: Functional and readable, but plain. The host dashboard prioritizes operations over polished presentation.
ratingComments.features: True round-robin, half-room-fixed mode, round count, timing, manual/auto starts, transition timing, sounds, messaging, and attendee join codes are meaningful live-event controls.
ratingComments.outputs: The product is designed for live operation rather than exporting a static schedule. No strong CSV/PDF-style output was observed in the tested setup.
ratingComments.privacy: Attendees join a hosted event and may use messaging. Better than ad-heavy randomizers, but not local/private.
ratingComments.accountFriction: The free capped event required no signup or credit card in testing.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Round-robin matching is a legitimate quality feature for live speed networking, though the free cap is tiny and diagnostics are minimal.

pros: No signup or credit card for a capped free event
cons: Free version capped at six attendees
Weak export story for offline planning
tools.reviewNote: Live test created a no-signup free host event and observed host code 27767 with true-round-robin/half-room-fixed modes, round timing, manual/auto starts, and messaging settings.

tools.name: Flipiffy Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Basic random groups with leaders, exclusions, saved list, CSV, and copy
tools.summary: Flipiffy Random Group Generator is more capable than the average SEO randomizer. It accepts pasted names, supports TXT/CSV upload, lets users prefix leaders with an asterisk, adds keep-apart exclusion rules, chooses number-of-groups or people-per-group mode, saves a list locally, and exposes CSV download plus copy-all output after generation. The core assignment remains random and the UI/implementation felt rough in automated testing, but the leader and exclusion features are useful for classroom and workshop grouping. Privacy is weak because the page loads ad, consent, analytics, Clarity, and sharing scripts.
tools.pricing: Free. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: A surprisingly featureful random-group tool, held back by rough execution and poor tracking/privacy.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The main controls are understandable, though the page and consent layer make interaction less clean than it should be.
ratingComments.design: Functional but generic. The form is usable, surrounded by SEO content and site chrome.
ratingComments.features: Good for a free randomizer: upload, by-count/by-size modes, leader spreading, keep-apart exclusions, local save, CSV, and copy-all. No attributes, multi-round repeat optimization, diagnostics, or manual editor.
ratingComments.outputs: CSV download and copy-all are visible after generation. No PDF/image, share link, or Zoom-specific export.
ratingComments.privacy: Very weak for rosters: Google ads/consent, Microsoft Clarity, AddToAny, Google Tag Manager/Analytics, and related third-party scripts loaded.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup or login is needed.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Better than plain random only because leaders and keep-apart rules can shape groups, but there is no optimization or scoring.

pros: By group count or people per group
tools.reviewNote: Generation was verified by calling the page generator after setting the visible inputs; the UI is worth a manual retest before promoting heavily.

tools.name: Grouperapp.net
tools.bestFor: Google Classroom teachers who want paid group history and preferences
tools.summary: Grouper is a paid teacher-focused grouping product built around Google Classroom. The public site says it creates balanced groups, avoids repeating the same groups, supports preferred and blocked student pairs, keeps group history, exports CSV, and includes data analytics. It may be useful for teachers who already manage rosters in Google Classroom and want a dedicated paid workflow. The downside is that the actual app sits behind Google sign-in and a 14-day trial, so the grouping UI and output quality could not be fully verified without creating an account.
tools.pricing: 14-day free trial, then advertised at $6.99/month on the Professional plan.
tools.overallComment: Promising for Google Classroom group management, but account/paywall friction and limited public verification hold it back.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The landing page is clear, but use requires Google sign-in and a trial before the real workflow can be tested.
ratingComments.design: Modern teacher-SaaS presentation with clear feature/pricing sections.
ratingComments.features: Advertised features are relevant: smart formation, preferred/blocked pairs, group history, CSV export, analytics, and Google Classroom focus. Public page does not expose the actual controls.
ratingComments.outputs: CSV export is advertised. No hands-on export test was possible without signing in.
ratingComments.privacy: The site claims encryption plus FERPA/COPPA compliance. It also loads Google/Atlassian/Loom/Sentry-related third-party services, and classroom data requires an account connection.
ratingComments.accountFriction: High: Google sign-in and paid-trial workflow are required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Likely better than random if blocked/preferred pairs and group history work as advertised, but the algorithm was not independently tested.

cons: Actual grouping workflow not publicly testable
tools.reviewNote: Public review is based on the landing page and sign-in/trial boundary. Retest with a Google Classroom account before treating feature claims as fully verified.

tools.name: Groupify.it
tools.bestFor: Classroom peer-group tool with roster upload, sex field, group-size/count modes, and animation controls
tools.summary: Groupify.it is a classroom-focused random group generator. It supports roster file upload, a student textarea with optional sex data, add/clear students, group size or number-of-groups modes, optional shuffle animation, and Generate Groups. It is more education-specific than most simple randomizers and may support basic sex/gender-aware roster metadata.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A classroom-oriented group generator with roster upload and optional demographic fields.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Reviewed with a pasted eight-name sample where possible; the main controls were understandable.
ratingComments.design: Usability reflects the observed page layout and surrounding clutter.
ratingComments.features: Roster upload, optional sex field, group-size/count modes, and animation controls.
ratingComments.outputs: No copy/export controls were observed beyond roster input.
ratingComments.privacy: No account; Cookiebot and Google Tag Manager were observed.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account was required for the reviewed basic flow unless otherwise noted.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Random grouping only; no full optimizer.

tools.reviewNote: Useful for classroom peer-group workflows.

tools.name: Keamk
tools.bestFor: Random teams with basic gender or skill balancing
tools.summary: Keamk is one of the more capable random-team tools in this set. It can create normal random teams, balance by gender or skill level, import participant/team lists, share public/admin links, export to Excel, embed results, and manage comments/draws. The tradeoff is a clunky old form UI and a bad privacy/ad-consent posture.
tools.pricing: Free basic team generator with optional account/admin features for saving and managing groups. The free workflow is usable, but persistence and classroom-management convenience depend more on the account side.
tools.overallComment: One of the more capable random-team tools, with real balancing and sharing/export features, but the clunky UI and terrible ad-consent/privacy setup hold it back hard.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Usable, but not effortless. The form is dense, participants are entered into rows unless you use the import control, and gender/skill/team controls make it feel heavier than it needs to be.
ratingComments.design: Dated and clunky. It looks like an old web form, and the results page is functional but messy.
ratingComments.features: Real features compared with most randomizers: normal random teams, gender balancing, skill-level balancing, participant/team imports, saved lists, editable team names, draw management, comments, embed, and history/account workflows.
ratingComments.outputs: Strong output options for this category: public share link, private admin link, Excel export, embed code, social sharing, and comments. Not Zoom-friendly and not as polished as Wooclap.
ratingComments.privacy: Bad. The privacy policy says IP addresses are stored when you make a draw or post a comment, Google Analytics is used, and data is hosted in Canada. The page also uses a heavy Google ad-consent setup with many vendor/device-storage options.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account is required for a basic draw, but several useful persistence and management features push account or email workflows, including saved lists, draw history, and administration links.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Better than pure random because it can balance by gender or skill level. No advanced constraints, repeat reduction, capacity modeling, or diagnostics.

pros: Share link, admin link, embed, and Excel export
cons: Many management features push account/email workflows
No repeat reduction or advanced constraints
tools.reviewNote: This is more powerful than the basic random-only tools, but the page feels old and the privacy setup is a major drawback.

tools.name: PrepPanel Classroom Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Chrome-extension classroom tool with ability-balanced groups and relationship rules
tools.summary: PrepPanel is a classroom Chrome extension that bundles a student picker, smart group generator, seating chart maker, participation tracker, and roadmaps. The group-generator pitch is specifically about creating balanced student groups while respecting ability levels and relationship rules. It is more of a classroom management product than a quick anonymous web randomizer.
tools.pricing: Free Chrome extension. No paid plan was required by the public landing page.
tools.overallComment: Relevant and more capable than basic randomizers, but heavier because it is an installed classroom product rather than a simple web form.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Likely convenient for teachers after extension setup, but not as quick as a paste-and-generate page.
ratingComments.design: Polished teacher-focused landing page and product positioning.
ratingComments.features: Ability balancing, relationship rules, seating charts, participation tracking, and roadmaps are substantial classroom features.
ratingComments.outputs: Useful inside the classroom workflow, but export specifics for groups are not clear from the public page.
ratingComments.privacy: A classroom/student-data product with analytics and extension permissions; better than ad utilities, but not a simple local-only page.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Requires installing a Chrome extension; account/friction details are heavier than web randomizers.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Ability and relationship rules should produce better classroom groups than pure random assignment.

cons: Not a quick anonymous web form
Student-data privacy depends on product use
tools.reviewNote: Review as the classroom group-generator feature inside PrepPanel, not as a generic Chrome extension directory listing.

tools.name: PrintYourBrackets Round Robin Generator
tools.bestFor: Tournament organizers who need printable round-robin schedules, pools, Excel export, and share links
tools.summary: PrintYourBrackets Round Robin Generator is a tournament schedule generator, not a normal classroom/workshop group maker. It can create round-robin schedules for 3–64 teams, 1–32 pools, optional locations, random or manual pool assignment, bulk team entry, custom tournament name/logo/rules, printable output, Excel export, and save/share links. That is useful for sports and game tournaments, but it does not assign people into balanced social groups or optimize attributes across sessions.
tools.pricing: Free web generator. No account required for the printable schedule flow. The site promotes a free online tournament manager as a separate upgrade path/workflow.
tools.overallComment: Strong enough for tournament schedules, but it is a niche round-robin bracket tool rather than a general group generator. Output options are good; privacy and UI are weak.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The basic form is understandable, with bulk team entry and dropdowns. The page is old-fashioned and a bit cluttered, and some advanced options are easier to miss than they should be.
ratingComments.design: Dated tournament-site layout with lots of navigation, ads, and promotional blocks around the generator.
ratingComments.features: Good for tournament scheduling: pools, team counts, bulk team names, optional locations, random/manual pool assignment, logo/rules, printable schedules, Excel export, and share links. Weak for actual people-to-groups constraints or classroom balancing.
ratingComments.outputs: One of its strongest areas: preview schedule, printable page, Excel export, save/share link, and optional online management workflow.
ratingComments.privacy: Poor. The page shows a consent banner and disclosed processing by 210 partners with precise geolocation language. The privacy policy says Google ad cookies are used and Google automatically receives IP addresses. Not a good fit for sensitive roster data.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required for the generator itself; the online manager is optional.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Good for the narrow round-robin tournament problem because it produces repeated rounds where teams meet. It is not a solver for balanced social groups, repeated workshop groups, or attribute constraints.

cons: Some options are tournament-specific and clunky for non-sports use
tools.reviewNote: Scope this review to the free round-robin generator page. Do not credit the broader online tournament manager except where the generator explicitly links to it as an optional output/management path.

tools.summary: RandomPickerTool Team Generator is a minimal tool for pasting names and splitting them by number of teams or people per team. Live testing generated two balanced teams and exposed Rebalance and Copy teams controls. No third-party requests or cookies were observed in the reviewed run.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A clean, low-friction basic team generator.

ratingComments.privacy: No third-party hosts or cookies observed.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required for the reviewed basic flow unless noted.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Random assignment only; no full multi-session optimizer.

tools.name: RandomTeamBuilder.com
tools.bestFor: Visual random teams with drag-and-drop adjustment and exclusion-rule claims
tools.summary: RandomTeamBuilder.com is a classroom/team randomizer with a more interactive feel than most basic splitters. It accepts pasted names, lets users choose a number of teams or names per team, randomizes teams, supports animations and confetti, and advertises exclusion rules, drag-and-drop fine-tuning, custom team names, saved lists, and Google Classroom import. Live testing generated teams, but the page also produced repeated JavaScript errors and the plain-text output was less clean than card-based tools. It is useful for interactive classroom display, but it is not a transparent optimizer.
tools.pricing: Free for the visible randomizer. Account/login surfaces exist for list/account features.
tools.overallComment: Featureful and interactive for classrooms, but rough technically and surrounded by account/Google integrations.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Pasting names and randomizing is easy. Advanced features such as exclusions and saved lists are less obvious.
ratingComments.design: Interactive and projector-friendly, with animations/confetti, but the page text output and controls are somewhat rough.
ratingComments.features: Notable features include exclusion-rule claims, drag-and-drop adjustments, custom team names, animations, saved lists, and Google Classroom import. No attribute balancing, multi-round repeat optimization, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: No strong export workflow was verified. The tool emphasizes display, drag/drop, saving lists, and sharing the site more than CSV/PDF output.
ratingComments.privacy: Loads Google sign-in/classroom APIs, Google Tag Manager/Analytics, ipapi, many CDNs, and other third-party assets. Not ideal for student rosters.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Basic randomization works without signup, but list/account features and Google Classroom integration introduce account friction.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Better than plain random if exclusion rules are used, but no scoring or optimization transparency was found.

cons: Repeated JavaScript errors in live testing
Advanced features are not especially transparent
tools.reviewNote: Live page produced repeated getBoundingClientRect JavaScript errors. Retest exclusion-rule workflow separately before relying on it.

tools.name: AppSorteos Random Teams Generator
tools.bestFor: Polished free random teams with leaders and share/export buttons
tools.summary: AppSorteos Random Teams Generator is a polished simple team splitter. Paste one name per line, choose either number of teams or participants per team, optionally mark team leaders with an asterisk, add a title, and generate equal random teams. Results include download, save, share, print, copy, and copy-for-Excel actions, although save/share push users into an AppSorteos account.
tools.pricing: The random team generator is free and works without an account. AppSorteos also has paid plans for its broader giveaway/promotion platform; saving or sharing results directly from the app requires login/account creation.
tools.overallComment: A good simple random team generator with better output tools than most. Random-only, with no balancing or repeat optimization, and the privacy/ad-tech/social-platform footprint is not great.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Very easy. Paste names, choose number of teams or participants per team, optionally add a title/leaders, then generate. The leader convention using * is simple once explained.
ratingComments.design: Clean and modern compared with many randomizer pages. It is surrounded by giveaway-platform navigation, share widgets, and marketing content, but the actual tool is readable and straightforward.
ratingComments.features: Good for a basic splitter: number-of-teams mode, participants-per-team mode, automatic equal distribution, optional group leaders, title editing, and regenerate. No constraints, balancing, repeats, attributes, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Strong for a simple tool. Generated results have download, print, copy, copy-for-Excel, save, and share actions. Save/share require account creation, and there is no Zoom-specific export or true spreadsheet import.
ratingComments.privacy: Weak for classroom/personnel rosters. The broader AppSorteos platform collects account, payment, social-media, log, cookie, web beacon, and third-party-button data; the page loads Google/analytics, AddThis/ShareThis-style sharing, Stripe, and social integrations. Better than the worst ad farms, but not privacy-first.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Generating, downloading, printing, and copying works without an account. Saving or sharing directly from the app triggers login/signup.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Slightly above plain random splitting because leaders can be placed first in each team. Otherwise it is standard random equal teams with no quality optimization.

pros: Download, print, copy, and copy-for-Excel actions
No account required for basic generation
cons: No balancing, constraints, or repeat optimization
Privacy footprint includes analytics, social widgets, and broader giveaway-platform data collection

tools.name: RandTools Random Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Privacy-conscious basic random team splitting with copy output
tools.summary: RandTools Random Team Generator is a clean browser-only random team splitter. Live testing pasted 12 names, selected three teams, generated three balanced teams of four, and verified a Copy All result button. The tool supports one-name-per-line paste input, quick team-count buttons for 2–6 teams, repeated reshuffling by clicking Generate again, and clipboard output. RandTools says it uses Fisher-Yates shuffling and keeps randomization client-side. It does not support team-size mode, custom team names, attributes, skill/gender balancing, constraints, repeat minimization, multi-round schedules, saved rosters, CSV/Excel/PDF/image export, share links, or diagnostics.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No signup, download, or account is required.
tools.overallComment: A very good minimal splitter: clean, browser-only, and copyable, but intentionally lacks advanced grouping features.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Simple and fast: paste names, choose 2–6 teams, click Generate. Names stay in the input for reshuffling.
ratingComments.design: Clean, modern, and uncluttered compared with many ad-heavy randomizer sites. The tool area is clear, though the site-wide tool catalog/sidebar takes some space.
ratingComments.features: Covers the basic random-split workflow well: paste input, 2–6 team buttons, balanced team sizes, regenerate, and copy all. No team-size mode, custom team names, imports, attributes, constraints, repeats, saved lists, or solver controls.
ratingComments.outputs: Generated teams are displayed clearly and can be copied with Copy All. No CSV, Excel, PDF/image, share-link, Zoom, or saved-result export was visible.
ratingComments.privacy: Strong for this category. The page is labeled browser-only, the privacy page says randomization and outputs stay on-device with no accounts and no first-party cookies, and live testing showed Google Analytics plus internal Next.js requests but no ad-tech auction traffic. Google Analytics and Cloudflare logs remain caveats.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup, login, payment, or verification was required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Fair random balanced sizes via Fisher-Yates, but no skill balance, attribute balance, constraints, repeat avoidance, or quality diagnostics.

No ad-tech scripts observed beyond Google Analytics
cons: Only supports 2–6 teams via visible controls

The page documents Fisher–Yates shuffle backed by crypto.getRandomValues and round-robin distribution so team sizes differ by at most one. It is a strong basic splitter, though it still lacks CSV/file import, custom team names, constraints, attributes, repeat avoidance, multi-round planning, share links, and detailed diagnostics.

tools.name: Begoodtool Smart Grouping Tool
tools.bestFor: Experimental rule-based grouping with tags, violations, drag/drop, and Excel export
tools.summary: Begoodtool Smart Grouping Tool has a much more ambitious feature set than plain randomizers. It parses pasted names, lets users add tags, create hard or soft together/separate rules, choose total groups, run grouping, inspect hard/soft violations, drag-and-drop people, auto-fix violations, restore a clean state, and export Excel. It claims client-side processing. The catch is execution quality: in a simple 12-person, 3-group live test, it produced group sizes 5, 5, and 2 while claiming a 4±1 target. That makes it risky despite the strong rule UI.
tools.pricing: Free. No account is required. The page includes ads/consent and donation prompts.
tools.overallComment: Ambitious and potentially useful for rules, but a basic balancing miss keeps it out of the top tier.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The simple parse/generate path is manageable, but tags, hard/soft rules, priority tags, violations, and fixes create a dense workflow.

ratingComments.features: Strong controls: tags, hard/soft together/separate rules, priority grouping, drag/drop, violation list, auto-fix, restore clean state, and Excel export.
ratingComments.outputs: Excel export is available after grouping and includes violation details according to the UI. No CSV/PDF/share output was verified.
ratingComments.privacy: Weak despite client-side claims: Google ads/consent, Microsoft Clarity, Facebook, Google Tag Manager/Analytics/DoubleClick, Cloudflare, and ad-quality domains loaded.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup or login is needed.
ratingComments.resultQuality: The rule model is promising, but the tested simple case produced badly uneven group sizes, so output quality is not consistently trustworthy.

cons: Simple balance test produced a 5/5/2 split for 12 people into 3 groups

tools.name: ChatGPT (Free Version)
tools.bestFor: Natural-language grouping when users are willing to verify the output
tools.summary: ChatGPT Free can create group assignments from a prompt, including arbitrary team counts, rough balancing, keep-together/keep-apart requests, and CSV-like output. It is far more flexible than basic random splitters, but it is not a deterministic group solver: users must check the roster, duplicates, constraints, and privacy implications themselves.
tools.pricing: Free tier available with usage/model/tool limits. Higher limits, stronger models, and business privacy defaults require paid ChatGPT plans; free consumer chats may be used to improve models depending on data controls/settings.
tools.overallComment: Flexible enough to be useful, but risky as a group generator because correctness is not guaranteed. Good for drafts, not trustworthy without manual verification.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Easy if the request is simple and the user knows how to prompt. Harder than a purpose-built splitter because you need to describe rules clearly and then audit the answer for missing names, duplicates, or violated constraints.
ratingComments.design: The chat interface is polished and readable. It is not a group-generator UI, but the general interaction design is much better than most randomizer sites.
ratingComments.features: High flexibility, but not productized group-generation depth. It can attempt arbitrary team counts, group sizes, balancing, keep-together/keep-apart rules, multi-round schedules, comments, and custom output formats. The catch is that these are language-model behaviors, not validated solver features; free-tier limits/context can also get in the way.
ratingComments.outputs: Can produce tables, Markdown, CSV-like text, explanations, and revised versions on request. There is no native group roster export button, Zoom export, persistent scenario file, or structured diagnostics unless the user asks and verifies the text.
ratingComments.privacy: Poor fit for sensitive rosters unless configured carefully. Free/consumer ChatGPT may use submitted content to improve models depending on settings; chats are stored unless deleted or temporary chat is used; and users should not paste student/personnel data without the right account, consent, and data controls.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Free access exists and some use is possible without signing in, but practical saved workflows, higher limits, file/context features, and data controls depend on account state and plan limits.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Can be surprisingly good for small, clearly specified grouping tasks, but it can also make silent mistakes. No deterministic seed, no guaranteed constraint satisfaction, no repeat matrix, and no solver validation. Manual checking is mandatory.

pros: Can handle unusual constraints in a draft
Can format output as tables or CSV-like text
cons: Must manually check duplicates, omissions, and rule violations
Poor default fit for sensitive rosters
No native group-generator export or diagnostics
tools.reviewNote: Review the free consumer ChatGPT workflow, not paid Team/Enterprise/API or custom solver code. The main risk is unvalidated output and privacy, not lack of flexibility.

tools.name: DiceFlipper Team Generator

tools.summary: DiceFlipper Team Generator accepts one name per line, asks for the number of teams, and generated two balanced teams in live testing. It is a straightforward, low-friction random team splitter with no cookies observed.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A clean basic team splitter.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Reviewed with a live browser pass and sample names where the page allowed it.
ratingComments.design: Reflects observed page clarity, workflow, and surrounding clutter.
ratingComments.features: Textarea and number-of-teams control.
ratingComments.outputs: Display-only output; no copy/download observed.
ratingComments.privacy: Fonts/jQuery/CDN hosts observed; no cookies observed.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required for the reviewed basic flow unless noted.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Random assignment only; no full multi-session optimizer.

tools.reviewNote: Good basic low-tracking addition.

tools.name: Diverse Group Generation
tools.bestFor: Small attribute-balanced grouping experiments
tools.summary: Diverse Group Generation is a prototype-style balanced grouping tool. Instead of pasting names, you build a member table made of attributes and options, then choose the number of groups. It runs an optimization/genetic-algorithm approach to make each group’s attribute distribution resemble the overall member distribution. That is much more interesting than a plain random splitter, but the workflow is narrow: members are IDs, setup is manual, there is no roster import, no names-first workflow, no constraints, no export, and no saved scenarios.
tools.pricing: Free. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: A genuinely interesting balancing prototype with better assignment logic than most simple randomizers, but too rough and output-limited for normal classroom or workshop use.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The concept is clear once understood, but the names-first workflow most people expect is missing. You have to build attributes/options and edit member rows manually.
ratingComments.design: Clean enough for a prototype, with collapsible sections and visible distribution counts. Some UI text and duplicate-looking options are rough.
ratingComments.features: The important feature is real attribute-distribution balancing across multiple attributes. It lacks many practical features: import/export, named rosters, constraints, repeat handling, saved scenarios, and result editing.
ratingComments.outputs: Results are shown as tables of member IDs and attribute distributions. There is no obvious CSV, copy, PDF/image, share link, or structured export.
ratingComments.privacy: No account is required and the app appears lightweight, but the page loads Google advertising infrastructure and Vercel analytics. There is no strong privacy posture for roster data.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup or login is needed.
ratingComments.resultQuality: For attribute-balanced grouping, the assignment logic is more serious than random splitting. It only optimizes the simple attribute table it is given and offers no constraints or repeat history.

cons: Members are displayed as IDs rather than normal roster names
Prototype-like UI and limited practical workflow
tools.reviewNote: Score as a balancing prototype, not as a polished classroom roster tool. Its result quality deserves credit for attribute optimization, while usability/output should stay low until it supports names, import/export, and practical review/edit workflows.

tools.name: DJPlaner GroupGenerator
tools.bestFor: Tiny GitHub Pages random group generator with save/load allocation controls
tools.summary: DJPlaner GroupGenerator is a minimal static-page group splitter. It accepts pasted names, groups by people per group, displays the current allocation, and includes save/load allocation buttons. The interface is sparse but fast, and the GitHub Pages deployment had no observed third-party tracking in testing.
tools.pricing: Free static web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A bare but privacy-friendly random group generator with surprisingly handy save/load controls.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Paste names, set people per group, generate.
ratingComments.design: Very plain utility UI.
ratingComments.features: People-per-group generation plus save/load allocation.
ratingComments.outputs: Displayed results and allocation save/load, but no copy/download affordance was obvious.
ratingComments.privacy: Static GitHub Pages tool with no third-party hosts observed.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Random grouping only.

tools.reviewNote: A good coverage entry for users who want a tiny low-tracking group splitter.

tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A simple multilingual classroom group generator.

ratingComments.design: Reflects observed page clarity, workflow, and surrounding clutter.
ratingComments.features: Names textarea, language selector, group count, and shuffle-again.
ratingComments.outputs: Display-only output; no copy/download observed.

tools.name: FateWheel Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Visual random team assignment with team-count/team-size modes
tools.summary: FateWheel Team Generator is a polished browser wheel-style team splitter. Live testing generated two balanced teams from the default four-member roster and showed an animated assignment flow plus a Generate Again button. The UI supports editable member rows, one-by-one add/remove, drag reorder, file import for CSV/Excel/TXT, generate by number of teams or desired team size, optional random team leaders, sound/confetti effects, and dark mode. Gender/label balancing appears only as work-in-progress options, and there are no verified constraints, repeat avoidance, multi-round planning, diagnostics, or dedicated export buttons; the FAQ suggests copying text or taking a screenshot.
tools.pricing: Free web tool with no account required for the tested generation flow. The wider site has login, saved-wheel, pricing, and ad-supported surfaces.
tools.overallComment: A good visual random splitter with file import, team-size mode, and leader assignment, held back by weak outputs and no real balancing/constraint solver.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The default roster makes it immediately testable, and generation worked without login. Adding members is primarily row/input based, while bulk workflows rely more on file import than a clear paste textarea.
ratingComments.design: Polished, modern, and visual. The animated assignment flow, effects, dark mode, and readable team cards are stronger than many basic randomizers.
ratingComments.features: Useful basics: CSV/Excel/TXT import, editable member rows, drag reorder, number-of-teams mode, team-size mode, random team leader assignment, and effects. Gender/label balancing is explicitly marked work-in-progress, and there are no real constraints, repeat handling, or multi-session controls.
ratingComments.outputs: Weak. Results are visible on screen and the FAQ suggests copying text or taking a screenshot, but no copy, CSV, PDF/image, share-link, or Zoom breakout export button was visible in live testing.
ratingComments.privacy: Mixed. The privacy policy says local wheel entries/settings stay in browser unless explicitly saved/shared, and the tested assignment did not require an account. However, the site uses Google Analytics/Clarity-style tracking, records spin statistics, supports ads, and saved/shared/account features send data to servers.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account, signup, payment, or verification was required to generate teams in the tested flow.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Balanced random team sizes and optional random leaders, but no attribute balancing beyond WIP controls, no constraints, no repeat minimization, and no quality diagnostics.

pros: Generated teams successfully in live testing
Generate by number of teams or desired team size
Editable member rows with drag reorder
cons: No dedicated copy/export/share controls visible for results
No constraints or attribute balancing verified
No repeat avoidance or multi-round planning
Analytics, ads, and saved/shared account features complicate privacy

ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup or login is needed.

tools.name: Lekktura Random Name Picker & Group Maker
tools.bestFor: Classroom name picking plus basic random groups
tools.summary: Lekktura combines a random name picker wheel with a basic group maker. For grouping, paste names, apply the list, choose by size or by count, use quick presets, create groups, shuffle, copy, or export CSV. It stores the current list locally in the browser and does not require an account. The group maker is useful and more export-friendly than many simple tools, but it has no lock-together/keep-apart rules, attribute inputs, repeat handling, saved multiple class lists, or diagnostics.
tools.pricing: Free tool. No account is required for the picker/group maker, though the surrounding Lekktura product has login, school, pricing, and Stripe/payment surfaces.
tools.overallComment: A good classroom utility with CSV/copy output and a name picker, limited to basic random grouping.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The grouping workflow works, but it requires applying names before group creation. That extra step makes it less immediate than the simplest splitters.
ratingComments.design: Modern and classroom-oriented. Some emoji/icons rendered garbled in testing, and the page is embedded in a broader product-marketing site.
ratingComments.features: Useful basic grouping features: by-size/by-count modes, presets, shuffle, copy, CSV, and local current-list storage. No constraints, attributes, repeated rounds, or saved multi-class roster management.
ratingComments.outputs: Copy and CSV export both exist, and CSV download worked. No PDF/image, share link, Zoom export, or richer result format.
ratingComments.privacy: The tool says names stay in browser local storage, which is a good design choice. The page also loaded Google Analytics/Tag Manager, Microsoft Clarity, Contentsquare, Ahrefs analytics, Stripe, and other product-site scripts.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup or login is needed for the free tool.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Balanced random grouping only. It is practical for one-off classroom groups, not for constraints or optimized group quality.

pros: Name picker and group maker in one page
tools.reviewNote: Scope the review to the free random name picker/group maker page, not Lekktura’s broader school dashboard.

tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A strong basic random team generator with nice copy/share/history touches. It is not a solver, but it does the simple job well.

ratingComments.privacy: The tool says randomization is client-side and stores history locally, which is good. The page loads Google AdSense, Funding Choices, and Google Analytics.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Slightly above bare random because captains mode can nominate leaders, but it is random-only assignment.

tools.name: Pandos Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Guided team formation workflow when email/server processing is acceptable
tools.summary: Pandos Team Generator is a guided, server-backed team formation assistant rather than a tiny paste-and-split randomizer. Live testing created a workspace, chose random generation, manually entered six members, selected generation by number of teams, generated two teams, and opened the preview showing Team1 with Alice/Bob/Carol and Team2 with Eve/Dave/Frank. The workflow also exposes CSV import, invitation-link member collection, team-size versus team-count generation, automatic versus manual team naming, and a criteria mode with availability, gender, and personality criteria shown. Final CSV and share-link delivery require entering an email address; the tested no-email flow only showed an on-page preview. It has no visible direct copy/download controls, no simple paste textarea, no repeat minimization, no multi-round planning, no saved roster UX without the Pandos backend, and no transparent quality diagnostics.
tools.pricing: Free tool flow allows up to 20 members without signup. More members require signup/contact. CSV/link delivery asks for an email address; the broader Pandos product has paid/business surfaces.
tools.overallComment: More structured than most basic splitters, but slow, server-heavy, and email-gated for exports/links.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The wizard is understandable, but it is much slower than paste-and-generate tools. Manual entry is one row at a time, and export/share requires an email address.
ratingComments.design: Professional SaaS-style wizard with clear steps and progress, but the interaction is clunky for quick ad hoc team splitting.
ratingComments.features: Notable workflow features: workspace creation, random or criteria generation, visible availability/gender/personality criteria options, manual entry, CSV import, invitation links, team-size or team-count generation, and automatic/manual team names. Missing direct paste, result editing, repeat planning, capacity controls, and quality diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Preview works. CSV file and result link are offered only after entering an email. No direct copy, CSV download, Excel, PDF/image, Zoom, or public share button was visible in live testing.
ratingComments.privacy: Weak-to-mixed. Member data and workspace state were sent to app.pandos.io during live testing, and the page loads Google Analytics, Microsoft Clarity, and Freshworks support. The privacy policy covers personal data, usage data, cookies, analytics, and transfer/storage in Canada.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup was required to generate and preview a small team set, but the tool has sign-in prompts, a 20-member free limit, and requires email for CSV/link delivery.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Random mode produced balanced team sizes. Criteria mode is visible, including availability/gender/personality, but it was not verified end-to-end and no solver diagnostics are shown.

pros: Generated and previewed teams successfully in live testing
Supports team-count and members-per-team generation modes
Criteria mode advertises availability, gender, and personality
cons: No simple paste textarea for quick rosters
Member data is sent to Pandos servers
No repeat avoidance or multi-round planning

tools.name: PickRandom.online Random Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Private cryptographic browser-side random teams with no account
tools.summary: PickRandom.online Random Team Generator is a focused browser-side team splitter. It accepts typed or pasted names, has an Add step for roster management, uses a slider for 1–10 teams, and generates balanced team cards. It prominently documents Fisher-Yates/Knuth shuffling seeded by the Web Crypto API, 100% in-browser processing, no server contact for names, no signup, zero data storage, and offline use after loading. It is strong on privacy and fairness transparency for a basic splitter, but output is weak: no visible copy/download/share/export controls, no custom team names, constraints, attributes, repeat avoidance, multi-round planning, or diagnostics beyond algorithm explanation.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No signup required.
tools.overallComment: A privacy-first basic splitter with good randomness documentation, but very limited outputs and no advanced rules.

ratingComments.privacy: Strong claim and implementation posture: in-browser Web Crypto/Fisher-Yates, no account, no storage, and no obvious ad-tech scripts in source inspection.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Balanced random teams using a documented CSPRNG shuffle, but no constraints or repeat minimization.

tools.name: Randly Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Clean no-account random groups with multiple generated draws
tools.summary: Randly Group Generator is a working no-account group generator with a polished interface. It is useful for quick alternate random arrangements, but it does not provide constraints, attribute balancing, repeat minimization across draws, saved rosters, diagnostics, or file export.

tools.name: Ruletaa Random Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Browser-local random groups with dedupe and CSV/image download
tools.summary: Ruletaa Random Team Generator is a responsive client-side random group tool. The page provides a participant textarea, group count, dedupe toggle, Start button, and live group containers. Documentation says it can download grouping results as image or CSV, stores participant data locally in the browser, and does not require login. Browser automation filled a tested roster and group count, though consent UI/script behavior prevented completing a custom generated result in the generic run; the live page and docs clearly expose the grouping workflow. It is stronger than bare randomizers because of dedupe and CSV/image export, but lacks custom team names, constraints, attributes, repeat avoidance, multi-round planning, and diagnostics.

tools.name: SpinnChoose Random Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Casual sports, gaming, and classroom team splits with list/bracket views
tools.summary: SpinnChoose Random Team Generator is a working random team and tournament-bracket maker for casual use. A retest of the underlying tool script generated three balanced teams from twelve pasted names, and a human browser check confirmed the public tool works well. It supports pasted names, custom team names, number-of-teams or team-size mode, list view, bracket view, and copy output. It is useful for quick sports, gaming, and classroom teams, but it has no constraints, attribute balancing, repeat avoidance, saved rosters, diagnostics, or file export. Automated testing also observed that the page depends on LiteSpeed-delivered JavaScript; if that loader fails, inline handlers can break.
tools.pricing: Free. No account is required.

The page describes dropping a roster into a wheel, setting Pick count to the desired team size, and spinning repeatedly to draw whole teams while removing picked names. It advertises cryptographically secure random selection, no ads, no signup, weighted entries such as Alice*2, per-name custom colors, saved rosters in localStorage, multiple wheels, and fullscreen projection. Automated testing on the team-picker landing page did not reach the wheel interaction itself, but the documented feature set is stronger than most wheel-based tools. It still lacks automatic full-team partitioning, constraints beyond weights, repeat avoidance, multi-round planning, and result diagnostics.

tools.name: Microsoft Excel
tools.bestFor: Spreadsheet users who want local files or organization-controlled rosters
tools.summary: Microsoft Excel can be used as a DIY random group generator with RAND/RANDBETWEEN formulas, random sorting, templates, or macros. It is powerful for rosters, exports, and offline/organization-controlled workflows, but like Google Sheets it is not a purpose-built group generator unless the user builds or downloads a template.
tools.pricing: Included in Microsoft 365 plans or standalone Office licenses; Excel for the web is available with a Microsoft account, and organization/education pricing varies.
tools.overallComment: Slightly better than Google Sheets for privacy/offline control, but a DIY spreadsheet workflow rather than a real group-generator product.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Not easy as a group generator. Excel users can build a random grouping sheet with formulas or templates, but this is setup work, not paste names and click generate.
ratingComments.design: Excellent spreadsheet UI for people who know Excel, but no purpose-built grouping interface unless you build one with a template, macro, or workbook design.
ratingComments.features: Flexible but DIY. Excel can handle arbitrary team counts, group sizes, saved rosters, notes, formulas, filters, imports, exports, macros, Power Query, and custom templates. Constraints, balancing, and repeat handling are only partial because users must design the logic themselves.
ratingComments.outputs: Excellent output surface: copy/paste, XLSX, CSV, PDF/print, local files, OneDrive/SharePoint sharing, versioning, and organization workflows. Stronger than most dedicated simple randomizers on export alone.
ratingComments.privacy: Better than many web tools because workbooks can stay local/offline or inside an organization-controlled Microsoft 365 tenant. Not automatically private: cloud storage, connected experiences, diagnostic/service data, links, macros, and sharing permissions all need care.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Desktop Excel requires a license or Microsoft 365 access, and Excel for the web requires a Microsoft account. Organizations may already have it, but it is not a no-account web tool.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Basic formula-based random groups are normal random-splitter quality. Better logic is possible with formulas/macros, but Excel has no built-in group solver, repeat minimizer, validation, or diagnostics.

pros: Can work offline or in controlled organization storage
Good for saved rosters, notes, and versioned workbooks
Extensible with formulas, templates, macros, and Power Query
cons: Microsoft account or license required for normal use
No built-in constraints or repeat optimizer
tools.reviewNote: Review as Excel used directly for grouping, not a specific third-party Excel template. Credit offline/local and organization-controlled workflows, but keep grouping scores below purpose-built generators because the logic is user-built.

tools.name: QuickWheelSpin Team Picker
tools.bestFor: Visual basic team picking with copy/share-image outputs
tools.summary: QuickWheelSpin Team Picker is a simple wheel-site team generator. Live testing used the built-in eight-name sample list, generated two balanced teams of four, verified a Copy Teams button, then opened the Share Results modal with copy-link, Instagram-story image, generic share-image, WhatsApp, Facebook, and X buttons. The UI supports entry add/edit/remove/clear, entry presets, number-of-teams mode, a split-method control that advertises split-by-team or players-per-team behavior, dark mode, language controls, and local browser storage for entries. The tested share-link creation fell back to an error URL because the Supabase share endpoint failed DNS resolution, so share links are only partially credited. It has no attributes, constraints, skill/gender balancing, repeat minimization, multi-round planning, file/spreadsheet export, saved scenarios, or diagnostics.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: A polished basic random team picker with good copy/social-image sharing, but no real balancing logic and a share-link failure in live testing.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The built-in sample names make the tool immediately testable, and generate/copy are straightforward. Bulk paste is claimed in the page copy, but the visible tested workflow centered on entry rows and add/edit/remove controls rather than a clear paste textarea.
ratingComments.design: Modern and pleasant, with a focused app panel, modal results, dark-mode/language controls, and clear entry management. Some icon-only controls require exploration.
ratingComments.features: Basic team generation with entry add/edit/remove/clear, presets, number-of-teams controls, a split-method control for team count versus players-per-team behavior, dark mode, and local stored entries. No file import/export, attributes, constraints, saved scenarios, repeat planning, or advanced balancing.
ratingComments.outputs: Better than many basic splitters: Copy Teams, Share Results, social share buttons, and image downloads for share/Instagram story are visible. Live share-link creation produced an error-fallback URL because the Supabase endpoint failed, and there is no CSV, Excel, PDF, or Zoom export.
ratingComments.privacy: Mixed. The privacy policy says wheel lists/settings are stored in browser local storage and shared-result data is temporarily stored on servers for about five minutes. The site uses Google Analytics, cookies/tracking language, and a Supabase endpoint for share links.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup, login, payment, or verification was needed.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Balanced random team sizes, but no skill balancing, gender/attribute balancing, constraints, repeat avoidance, or quality diagnostics.

tools.pricing: Free ad-supported web tool. No account is required for the generator; account buttons exist for broader Spinwheely features.
tools.overallComment: A good-looking simple random team splitter with unusually strong exports, but no bulk paste and no real assignment intelligence.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The UI is clear once loaded, but adding members one by one is slower than paste-based team generators. The first load also showed a transient React hydration error before the actual tool appeared.
ratingComments.design: Polished modern UI with clear setup/results tabs and visual team cards. It feels more like an app than an old utility page, though ads/account chrome add some clutter.
ratingComments.features: Decent simple-tool features: team count, naming style, max-members/equal-size setting, one-by-one member entry, results tab, clear controls, and exports. No bulk paste, imports, attributes, constraints, saved rosters, repeat planning, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Strong for a simple randomizer: export menu includes PDF, CSV, and text. No Zoom-specific export, shareable result links, spreadsheet import, or richer report/history workflow.
ratingComments.privacy: Mixed. The privacy policy says wheel content is local by default and the generator appeared browser-side in testing, but the site is ad-supported with Google/partner advertising, local storage, account/save/share paths, and consent-based ad tracking.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No login is needed for the random team generator itself, although login/signup buttons are present for broader Spinwheely features.

tools.summary: TeamShufflr is not a conventional paste-names-and-show-groups generator. It creates individualized team cards for workshops: set participant/card counts, define teams and team sizes, choose distinguishing features such as colors/names/icons, print cards, and reshuffle by using card features during activities. That can be useful for facilitation, but it is an indirect card workflow rather than a serious group assignment solver.
tools.pricing: Basic version is free. The site mentions custom branded cards by contacting them.
tools.overallComment: A clever physical-workshop card system, not a normal group generator. Useful niche, but it should not be rated like a solver.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: More setup than a normal random splitter because the product centers on creating cards and using them during an event.
ratingComments.design: Polished and friendly. The app and landing page are much better designed than most randomizer pages.
ratingComments.features: Niche but real: participant/card setup, team sizes, card features, names/colors/icons, printable cards, and repeated shuffling during workshops. No constraints, attributes, diagnostics, or repeat optimization.

ratingComments.privacy: German/GDPR-style privacy pages and cookie settings are better than many utilities, but the site uses Google Analytics/GTM cookies.
ratingComments.accountFriction: The simple card workflow can be used without an account.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Random card assignment can mix people during activities, but it does not optimize assignments or reduce repeats algorithmically.

pros: Can use names, colors, and icons

tools.name: Wheelpick Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Polished no-account browser-side random teams from a saved roster
tools.summary: Wheelpick Team Generator is a polished basic team splitter from a wheel-picker site. Live testing used the built-in ten-name sample list, generated three teams, and verified balanced team sizes of 4/3/3 with randomly named teams such as Team Coral, Team Amber, and Team Honey. The UI supports editable name rows, one-by-one add/remove, a paste-list importer, shuffle/clear controls, team-count increment/decrement, generate-again reshuffling, local saved rosters, confetti, and a remove-winner-after-spin setting inherited from the broader wheel picker. The page claims share-by-link for exact lists, but no explicit copy/share control was visible in the tested team-generator UI. It has no copy/export for team results, team-size mode, attributes, constraints, skill/gender balancing, repeat minimization, multi-round planning, file/spreadsheet export, or diagnostics.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: A very pleasant basic random splitter with good roster input/editing, but weak outputs and no advanced balancing logic.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Easy to try because it ships with a sample roster. Editing rows, adding names, importing a pasted list, changing team count, and generating again are straightforward.
ratingComments.design: One of the nicer basic splitters visually: clean, responsive, and readable with tasteful team cards and randomized team names.
ratingComments.features: Good roster ergonomics for a simple random splitter: editable entries, paste-list import, shuffle/clear, local remembered list, increment/decrement team count, and generate again. No team-size mode, custom constraints, attributes, saved scenarios, file import/export, or multi-round features.
ratingComments.outputs: Weak. Generated teams are shown on screen, but no dedicated copy, CSV, Excel, PDF/image, Zoom, or explicit team-result share control was visible in live testing. Share-by-link is claimed in marketing copy but not verified as a UI action.

ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup, login, payment, or verification was required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Balanced random team sizes using a claimed Fisher-Yates shuffle, but no skill/attribute balancing, constraints, repeat avoidance, or diagnostics.

No third-party tracking scripts observed in live testing
cons: No visible copy/export controls for generated teams
No repeat avoidance or multi-round planning
Share-by-link is claimed but no explicit team-result share action was visible

tools.name: Zoom breakout room assignment
tools.bestFor: Assigning live meeting participants directly into Zoom rooms
tools.summary: Zoom breakout rooms are not a general group generator, but they are a real in-meeting grouping workflow. Hosts can create up to 100 rooms, auto-assign participants evenly, assign manually, let participants choose rooms, pre-assign rooms with a CSV, save assignments for recurring meetings, and move people during the session. It is convenient inside Zoom and weak everywhere else.
tools.pricing: Breakout rooms are available in Zoom meetings, including free/basic contexts, but meeting duration, participant capacity, admin controls, and large-meeting needs depend on Zoom plan and account settings.
tools.overallComment: Very useful if the only destination is a live Zoom meeting. Not a general-purpose group generator and not a quality optimizer.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Easy enough for hosts already comfortable with Zoom, but awkward as a group generator: breakout settings must be enabled, pre-assignment lives in the web portal, CSV setup uses email addresses, and participants often need to be signed in correctly.
ratingComments.design: The in-meeting breakout UI is functional and integrated into the meeting experience. It is not a dedicated planning interface, but it is much better than exporting from a randomizer and then manually recreating rooms.
ratingComments.features: Moderate for Zoom-specific grouping: automatic even assignment, manual assignment, participant self-select, room renaming, moving/swapping, up to 100 rooms, CSV pre-assignment, saved assignments for recurring meetings, timers, broadcast, and activity status. Missing general group-generator features: constraints, balancing attributes, repeat optimization, multi-session planning outside recurring-room saves, and diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: The output is directly actionable inside Zoom, which is valuable. Outside Zoom it is weak: pre-assignment CSV import exists, but there is no normal roster export, spreadsheet/report output, or shareable assignment page for other tools.
ratingComments.privacy: Zoom has serious enterprise/security documentation, DPAs, and education compliance materials, but this is a hosted meeting platform processing participant identities, room names, telemetry/service data, chats/recordings depending on settings, and account/admin data. Better than ad-tech utilities, not private like a local tool.
ratingComments.accountFriction: High friction for a “group generator”: the host needs a Zoom account/meeting, breakout settings enabled, and pre-assigned participants need matching Zoom accounts/emails for assignments to apply reliably.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Automatic assignment creates evenly sized rooms, which is fine for live one-off breakouts. It does not balance attributes, satisfy constraints, reduce repeats across varied sessions, or explain quality.

pros: Automatic, manual, and self-select room assignment
Can save breakout assignments for recurring meetings
Timers, broadcast, and activity status help live facilitation
cons: Pre-assignment depends on participant Zoom account emails
No repeat optimization across arbitrary sessions
tools.reviewNote: Review only Zoom breakout-room assignment, not Zoom as a full meeting product. It should score well for in-meeting execution but not for general group-generation quality.

tools.name: Cojoro Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Standalone team generation with custom team names, leader selection, and multiple export formats if certificate warnings are acceptable
tools.summary: Cojoro Team Generator is a capable standalone random team page, but its current HTTPS certificate problem is a major trust and access issue. Retesting with certificate errors ignored produced working output: twelve names, three groups, generated three teams of four. The UI supports optional team names, presets, number of groups or members per team, random leader selection, saved/opened names, help, share/export menu, and page text/search results indicate text, CSV, Markdown, and PNG downloads. The feature set is good for a hobby tool, but the invalid certificate makes it hard to recommend for participant data.
tools.pricing: Free. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: Featureful for a simple tool, but an invalid HTTPS certificate sharply reduces trust.

There are no accounts, ads, analytics, consent banners, or observed server requests after loading. ratingComments.privacy: Strong for a hosted web app. No account, ads, analytics, consent banner, or generation API requests were observed; only a favicon 404 appeared in console. Input/config appear to persist via localStorage on the device. No privacy policy was found, so it is not a perfect score.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup, login, or payment is needed.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain balanced random groups. Good enough for one-off random teams, with no constraints, attribute balancing, repeat minimization, or quality diagnostics.

pros: No account, ads, analytics, or consent banner observed

tools.bestFor: Simple random teams with strong text/CSV/JSON exports if hosted processing is acceptable
tools.summary: Generate-Random.org Random Team Generator is a straightforward server-backed random team splitter.
