It supports number-of-teams and members-per-team modes, several input separators, duplicate/empty cleanup, Copy All, TXT export, CSV export, JSON export, and a visible API-style request. The grouping logic is still plain random splitting with no attributes, constraints, repeat reduction, saved rosters, or diagnostics.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No signup is required.
tools.overallComment: A capable basic random team splitter with unusually good exports, dragged down hard by hosted generation and a privacy story that conflicts with the observed ad/analytics consent stack.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Easy basic workflow: paste names, choose number of teams or members per team, generate. Separator controls and terminal-style labels add some clutter but are understandable.
ratingComments.design: Distinct terminal-style design and reasonably readable output. The page is long and SEO-heavy, but the tool area itself is clear.
ratingComments.features: Good for a basic splitter: by-team-count and by-size modes, multiple separators, duplicate/empty cleanup, CSPRNG/Fisher-Yates claims, Copy All, TXT/CSV/JSON export, and an API-style request. No constraints, attributes, team naming, saved rosters, multi-round planning, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Stronger than most simple randomizers: copy all plus TXT, CSV, and JSON export buttons. No Excel-native, Zoom-specific, image/PDF, share-link, or report-style output.
ratingComments.privacy: Bad. Despite a privacy policy claiming no tracking, no analytics, no third-party services, and no advertising, live testing showed a Google-managed consent dialog, Google Analytics, Funding Choices, AdSense/PageAd requests, and a 210-partner consent message. The entered names were sent to a server API in a GET query string.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account, signup, or payment is needed to generate teams.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain balanced random teams. Useful for one-off random splitting, but there is no constraint handling, attribute balancing, repeat minimization, or quality diagnostics.

pros: Copy All plus TXT, CSV, and JSON exports
Generated balanced group sizes in live testing
cons: No repeat avoidance or multi-round planning
No saved rosters or result history
Names are sent to a server API in the URL query
Observed ads/analytics contradict the privacy policy
Long SEO page around the tool

tools.name: Google Sheets
tools.bestFor: Spreadsheet users willing to build their own random grouping template
tools.summary: Google Sheets can be made into a random group generator with formulas, random sorting, templates, or Apps Script. It is flexible and excellent for storing rosters and exporting results, but it is not a purpose-built group generator: users have to build or copy the grouping logic themselves.
tools.pricing: Free with a Google account for consumer use; Google Workspace and Workspace for Education plans vary by organization.
tools.overallComment: Powerful as a platform, mediocre as a group generator. It can outperform many tiny randomizers if you know Sheets, but the actual group-generation workflow is DIY.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Not easy as a group generator. Entering a roster is easy, but random grouping requires formulas, sorting, a copied template, or Apps Script. Spreadsheet-comfortable users will be fine; everyone else gets setup work.
ratingComments.design: The spreadsheet interface is mature, readable, collaborative, and reliable. It is not tailored to group generation, so the UX depends entirely on the sheet/template you build.
ratingComments.features: Flexible but DIY. Sheets can handle arbitrary team counts, group sizes, saved rosters, notes, formulas, filters, imports, exports, and custom scripts. Attribute balancing, keep-apart rules, fixed placement, or repeats are only partial because users must design formulas/scripts themselves; none of it is a built-in group-generator workflow.
ratingComments.outputs: Excellent export and sharing surface: copy/paste, CSV, XLSX, PDF/print, share links, collaboration, version history, and integration with the broader Google ecosystem. Output is where Sheets is much stronger than most simple web randomizers.
ratingComments.privacy: Mixed. There is no ad-tech utility-page mess inside Sheets, and Workspace for Education has serious privacy terms. Rosters live in Google cloud under an account, sharing permissions can leak data if misconfigured, and consumer Google account data handling is not the same as a local/browser-only tool.
ratingComments.accountFriction: A Google account is required for the normal workflow, and organizational users may need Workspace permissions. Not a no-account web splitter.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Basic formula-based random groups are roughly normal random-splitter quality. Better results are possible with custom formulas/scripts, but there is no built-in solver, repeat minimization, constraint validation, or diagnostics.

pros: Good for saved rosters and notes
Can be extended with formulas, templates, and Apps Script
cons: No built-in constraints or repeat optimizer
Privacy depends on Google account/admin/sharing setup
tools.reviewNote: Review as Google Sheets used directly for grouping, not as a marketplace add-on or custom app. Credit platform flexibility and exports, but keep feature/result scores below purpose-built group generators because the grouping logic is user-built.

tools.name: GoOnlineTools Random Group Generator

tools.summary: GoOnlineTools Random Group Generator is a polished basic web splitter. Paste names, choose by groups or by members, generate, regenerate, and see simple statistics such as total groups, total members, average per group, and size range. The page advertises advanced options like custom team names, exclusions, representative selection, and export/share, but the visible tested workflow is much closer to a simple random splitter than a serious constraint tool. Privacy is the main weakness because the page carries ads, Funding Choices, Google sign-in scripts, Microsoft Clarity, and other tracking/storage surface.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required for generation; sign-in exists for the broader GoOnlineTools site.
tools.overallComment: A decent modern simple splitter, but the privacy/ad-tech load is bad and the advertised advanced controls are not enough to make it a solver.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Easy paste-and-generate flow with by-groups/by-members modes and a clear regenerate button.
ratingComments.design: Modern and readable, though ads and surrounding tool-site chrome add clutter.
ratingComments.features: Better than the bare minimum: by groups/by members, regenerate, statistics, and advertised advanced options. No proven hard constraints, repeat optimization, or real diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Result action icons and page copy indicate copy/download/share, but output formats are not as clear or strong as CSV/PDF-focused tools.
ratingComments.privacy: Poor. The page runs ads/Funding Choices, Google account/sign-in scripts, Microsoft Clarity, and stores ad/recent/favorite/color-mode data. The policy also allows ad cookies and IP collection.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Generation works without sign-in. Sign-in is visible for site features such as favorites.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Slightly above bare random because by-members mode and stats help, but assignments are random-only unless the advanced options prove more substantial.

tools.name: Gynzy Group Maker

tools.summary: Gynzy Group Maker is a simple interactive-whiteboard group randomizer. The public library page says it randomizes groups of students from a list and opens without an account. In the board tool, you enter student names on separate lines, choose the number of students per group, save, and get a whiteboard-style randomized layout with a shuffle button. It looks good for classroom display, but it is a basic random splitter with no constraints, balancing, exports, or repeat handling.
tools.pricing: The Group Maker opens with “No account needed” from the Gynzy library page. Gynzy itself is a broader paid/education whiteboard platform, but this specific tool can be opened without signing in.
tools.overallComment: A nice presentation-oriented classroom randomizer, not a serious group planner. It is much better as a whiteboard reveal tool than as a constraint-aware generator.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Once the tool is open, the workflow is simple: paste names, set students per group, save, shuffle. The Cookiebot banner and onboarding screens add friction before the actual tool appears.
ratingComments.design: Good interactive-whiteboard design. The output is large, visual, and classroom-friendly, which is the main reason to use it.
ratingComments.features: Basic group-generation depth: names, number of students per group, random layout, and reshuffle. No constraints, attribute balancing, imports, repeat handling, diagnostics, or group-count mode was visible.
ratingComments.outputs: The output is useful for live display on a Gynzy board, but there is no obvious CSV, copy, PDF/image, share, or Zoom-friendly export workflow for the generated groups.
ratingComments.privacy: Poor for sensitive roster use. The site exposes a large Cookiebot surface with statistics/marketing categories and providers such as Google, RudderStack, Segment, Typeform, Vimeo, Intercom, Stripe, and Sprig. Necessary-only cookies can be selected, but the surrounding tracking stack is heavy.
ratingComments.accountFriction: The specific Group Maker tool opened without signing in.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Result quality is basic random splitting. Fine for quick classroom grouping, but it does not improve assignment quality beyond random groups of a chosen size.

pros: No account required for the tool
cons: Heavy cookie/tracking surface around the site
Onboarding and cookie screens add friction
tools.reviewNote: Review the Group Maker library item and board tool, not the whole Gynzy whiteboard platform. It is a presentation-friendly randomizer with no-account access, but privacy and feature depth should stay conservative.

It is just a spreadsheet workaround, not a dedicated optimizer.

ratingComments.privacy: Roster data can stay in your own spreadsheet after download, which is good. The website page itself runs ads and Google tags.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required for the template page. Google Sheets use may involve a Google account.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Slightly above plain web randomizers because spreadsheet sorting gives manual control, but random-only and explicitly not social-golfer solving.

tools.name: ClassDojo Group Maker
tools.bestFor: Teachers already using ClassDojo who want quick classroom groups
tools.summary: ClassDojo Group Maker is not really a standalone web group generator. It is a Toolkit feature inside the ClassDojo app for teachers with a ClassDojo class roster. It can randomly generate student groups of any size and supports sets of students who should not be grouped together. For existing ClassDojo classrooms that is handy; for everyone else it is an account/app workflow around a basic random classroom splitter.
tools.pricing: ClassDojo Toolkit is advertised as free and included in the ClassDojo app. ClassDojo also has optional paid/premium family features, but the Group Maker workflow itself is positioned as free.
tools.overallComment: Useful if your class already lives in ClassDojo, but weak as a general-purpose group generator. The keep-apart sets are real value; the app/account wall and limited export surface are the problem.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: For an existing ClassDojo teacher with a roster, group generation is probably very easy. For anyone else, it starts with downloading/signing into ClassDojo and setting up a class, which is a lot of friction for one random split.
ratingComments.design: ClassDojo is polished, classroom-friendly, and much nicer than most randomizer pages. The public Group Maker page is clear, but it mostly advertises the app rather than exposing the tool directly.
ratingComments.features: Better than the weakest random splitters because it uses a saved class roster and supports keep-apart sets. Limited: random groups only, group size choices, no attribute balancing, no multi-session repeat minimization, no solver diagnostics, and no serious planning controls.
ratingComments.outputs: Very weak for export. The visible product positioning is in-app classroom display/use, not CSV, Excel, Zoom breakout export, share links, PDFs, or reusable result reports.
ratingComments.privacy: ClassDojo has a much more serious student-privacy posture than ad-heavy utility sites: COPPA/FERPA/GDPR language, iKeepSafe certifications, Student Privacy Pledge, no selling/renting, and no student behaviorally targeted advertising. It is a hosted classroom platform collecting rosters, account data, device/usage data, and service-provider analytics, so it is not comparable to local/no-account tools.
ratingComments.accountFriction: High. The Group Maker is part of the ClassDojo app, not a no-login webpage. You need the ClassDojo ecosystem and likely a teacher account/class roster for real use.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Slightly above a plain random splitter because keep-apart sets can prevent bad pairings. It does not optimize balance, preferences, repeated encounters, or multi-session coverage.

pros: Keep-apart sets for students who should not be grouped together
Strong education privacy posture compared with ad-heavy utilities
cons: Mostly useful only for existing ClassDojo classrooms
tools.reviewNote: Scope this to the Toolkit Group Maker, not the full ClassDojo classroom platform. Do not let ClassDojo polish or broader classroom features inflate the group-generator score. Unknown review-builder feature states were resolved conservatively because the public Group Maker positioning does not show custom team names, clipboard export, or saved result history.

tools.name: CodeBeautify Random Team Generator

The live page displayed five generated team cards; a consent-management iframe blocked some click automation during custom roster testing, but the form and output controls were visible. It is useful for quick teams, but it is ad/tracker heavy and lacks team-size planning beyond basic count/member controls, custom team names, constraints, repeat avoidance, multi-round planning, share links, or quality diagnostics.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. Login/favorites are optional; ads and consent management are present.

ratingComments.outputs: Per-team copy links and a Download button are visible. No CSV/Excel/Zoom/share workflow was verified.

ratingComments.accountFriction: No account needed for the generator; login is only for site features such as favorites.

tools.pricing: Free with ads. Premium removes ads and adds account features such as saved lists/My Page; the premium page advertises one-off access options and group accounts, with cookies required for premium login.
tools.overallComment: Good classroom showmanship around a basic random splitter. The exports and fullscreen presentation are useful, but the tool has no real grouping intelligence and the ad/tracking/privacy story is weak.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The basic workflow is not hard: paste names, choose a number, generate. The old animated UI, settings cog, iframed app, and many themed variants make it less clean than the best simple splitters.
ratingComments.design: Fun for classrooms, but busy and dated. The site is full of navigation, premium prompts, ads, animations, sounds, and themed variants. That is presentation value, not a clean modern group-generator UI.
ratingComments.features: Decent for a basic random splitter: number-of-groups mode, max-group-size mode, animated reveals, fullscreen, sound/transition toggles, jump-to-group, copy, CSV export, and premium saved lists. No constraints, balancing, repeats, attributes, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Better than many simple randomizers. It can copy one group or all groups, download a CSV, show all groups together, use fullscreen presentation, and save lists/results through premium workflows. No Zoom-specific export, spreadsheet import, PDF/image export, or serious report format.
ratingComments.privacy: Poor. The free site is ad-supported and uses Google Analytics/Google Tag Manager, Google AdSense-style advertising, Publift/Fuse ad scripts, cookies, Cloudflare, and premium account storage. The user-data terms explicitly warn not to upload personal/sensitive data and to treat uploaded data as non-sensitive/non-personal.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Generating groups does not require an account. Premium is only needed for saved lists, ad-free use, and account convenience features.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random grouping with even-ish sizes. Animations, themes, and stronger randomness presentation do not improve assignment quality.

pros: No account required for basic use
Premium saved lists for repeated rosters
cons: No constraints, balancing, or repeat optimization
User-data terms warn against personal/sensitive data
tools.reviewNote: Do not over-credit the many themed generators; they are mostly skins around the same random splitter. The CSV/copy/fullscreen output surface is real and should be credited.

tools.name: RandomTools.app Random Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Simple random teams with copy output and recent-draw history
tools.summary: RandomTools.app Random Team Generator is a clean basic splitter. It accepts multiline or comma-separated names, supports a number-of-teams setting, optional target team size, balanced vs uneven mode, copy-list output, and a recent team-draw history for comparing the last eight assignments. It generated balanced teams correctly when the target team-size override was left blank. It has no constraints, attributes, repeat optimization, roster import/export, PDF/CSV export, or diagnostics.
tools.pricing: Free. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: A good lightweight randomizer thanks to copy output and history, but it remains basic random splitting.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Simple pasted-name workflow. Users need to notice that target team size overrides number of teams.
ratingComments.design: Clear and compact with useful summary stats and recent-draw history.
ratingComments.features: Basic team count, optional team size, balanced/uneven mode, copy output, and recent-history log. No constraints, attributes, repeats, imports, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Copy List is available. There is no CSV, PDF/image, share link, or specialized export.
ratingComments.privacy: The page says everything runs locally. Google Tag Manager/Analytics and Google fonts loaded, so it is not fully tracker-free.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup or login is needed.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Balanced random distribution only.

tools.reviewNote: When both team count and target team size are filled, team size takes priority and can produce extras/benched participants.

tools.name: Spin Numbers Make Random Teams
tools.bestFor: Quick local random team splits with copy-only output
tools.summary: Spin Numbers Make Random Teams is a clean basic splitter: paste one name per line, choose 2–10 teams, optionally shuffle, and generate balanced groups with names like Team Alpha, Team Beta, and Team Gamma. Live testing with twelve names and three teams produced three teams of four and a Copy Results button. It runs the grouping in the browser and does not appear to upload entered names, but it has no group-size mode, custom team names, imports, exports, constraints, attributes, repeat handling, saved rosters, or diagnostics.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: A pleasant copy-only random splitter. Fine for quick one-off teams, but feature depth and output options are thin, and the page has a heavy ad/consent setup.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Very easy: paste names, choose team count, optionally toggle shuffle, and generate. The 2–10 team limit and number-of-teams-only setup keep it simple but limited.
ratingComments.design: Clean, modern, and more polished than many throwaway randomizer pages. The surrounding WordPress/article layout and ad infrastructure add some noise.
ratingComments.features: Basic random splitting only: pasted names, team count from 2 to 10, shuffle toggle, regenerate, copy results, and browser-side processing. No group-size mode, custom names, constraints, attributes, imports, saved rosters, repeat planning, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Copy Results is useful, but there are no CSV/spreadsheet, TXT, JSON, PDF/image, Zoom, print, or result-share exports. Social buttons share the page, not the generated teams.
ratingComments.privacy: Mixed. The tool claims names stay in the browser and live testing did not show names being sent to a generation API. The page still uses Google Analytics, Google AdSense, Funding Choices, and a consent dialog saying data may be shared with 210 partners including precise geolocation processing.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup, login, or payment is needed.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain balanced random teams. Good enough for a one-off split, with no constraints, balancing dimensions, repeat minimization, or quality diagnostics.

pros: Generated balanced group sizes in live testing
cons: No CSV/spreadsheet or other real exports
No constraints, attributes, or repeat avoidance
Ad/consent stack says data may be shared with 210 partners

tools.name: Suijidaquan Random Grouping Generator
tools.bestFor: Multilingual random grouping page with share and configurable randomization
tools.summary: Suijidaquan Random Grouping Generator is a simple random grouping tool. It provides a textarea, number input, Random button, Modify config, and Share controls. The reviewed page used sample animal names and did not expose rich exports, but it fits as a basic multilingual random grouping utility.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A basic random grouping tool with share/config controls.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Reviewed with live page controls and an eight-name sample where possible.
ratingComments.design: Reflects the observed page layout, clarity, and surrounding clutter.
ratingComments.features: Textarea, numeric grouping setting, modify config, and share.
ratingComments.outputs: Share is present; no CSV/PDF export observed.
ratingComments.privacy: Cloudflare and Google Tag Manager observed; no account required.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required for the reviewed basic flow unless noted.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Random assignment only; no full multi-session optimizer.

tools.reviewNote: Coverage entry for a Chinese/random-tools site.

tools.name: Super Teacher Tools Classroom Seating / Groups
tools.bestFor: Teachers who want saved class rosters plus simple random classroom groups
tools.summary: Super Teacher Tools Instant Classroom / Group Maker is an old-school but working classroom roster system. You create an Instant Classroom account with a group name, email, display name, and password, then create or import a class list of up to 100 students. The Group Maker can split a saved roster by students per group or by number of groups, with options from 2 to 10 and a remainder-distribution workflow. It also sits beside seating-chart and random-name tools. For group generation specifically, it uses basic random splitting: no constraints, no attribute balancing, no repeat avoidance, no diagnostics, and no clean export format.
tools.pricing: Free. The product page says “100% Free forever,” but actual custom class use requires creating a Super Teacher Tools / Instant Classroom group account.
tools.overallComment: A functional saved-roster classroom randomizer with useful group-size/group-count controls, held back by account friction, a dated interface, ad/consent load, and no optimization beyond random grouping.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: After setup, generating groups is straightforward: choose students per group or number of groups and click Create Groups. Getting there requires account creation, password login, old-style roster forms, and sometimes hidden/awkward bulk-upload controls.
ratingComments.design: Very dated classroom-web layout with image-button navigation and ad blocks. It works, but it feels much older and less polished than modern tools.
ratingComments.features: Better than a one-shot splitter because it saves class rosters and supports both group-size and group-count generation plus remainder distribution. It does not support constraints, attribute balancing, repeat minimization, multiple sessions, pinned placements, or solver diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Results are shown on a web page as Group #1, Group #2, etc. There is no obvious CSV, Excel, copy-to-clipboard, PDF/image, Zoom breakout, or structured export workflow.
ratingComments.privacy: Weak for student-roster entry. Testing showed Google advertising/analytics/consent infrastructure, DoubleClick, AddThis on the landing flow, and a Funding Choices consent dialog mentioning data sharing with 210 partners. Rosters are stored server-side in an account-like classroom group.
ratingComments.accountFriction: The demo can be viewed freely, but real class roster use requires creating a named group account with email, display name, and password, then logging in.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Good enough random classroom splitting, including uneven remainder handling. It does not optimize group quality or avoid repeated pairings.

pros: Saved class rosters up to 100 students
Can create groups by students per group or by number of groups
Remainder distribution option for uneven groups
Adjacent seating-chart and random-name tools for the same roster
cons: Requires account-style setup for real class rosters
No constraints, balancing, or repeat avoidance
No clean export/copy workflow for generated groups
Ad and consent stack is heavy for student roster data
Roster setup is clunky despite bulk/import support
tools.reviewNote: Live-tested the Instant Classroom demo and a temporary dummy class. Keep score focused on Group Maker capability, not the adjacent seating-chart or random-name-picker tools. Do not rate it as no-account: real custom roster use requires sign-up/login even though the demo is public.

tools.name: Fotify Speed Dating Event Planner
tools.bestFor: Quick speed-dating event timing plans
tools.summary: Fotify’s public planner is freely usable and generated an event plan in-browser during live testing. With 12 attendees and five-minute rounds, it produced a minute-by-minute plan with doors-open timing, host welcome, open mingling, five structured speed rounds, breaks, wrap-up, and event-end timing. That is useful event planning output, but it does not assign named participants to pairs or tables; the page upsells Fotify’s paid Match & Connect product for the actual matchmaking layer.
tools.pricing: The planner page is free and requires no account. Fotify advertises Match & Connect at $19.99 per event for the matchmaking app layer.
tools.overallComment: Useful as a free timing planner for speed-dating/networking events, but weak as a group generator because it does not output named pairings.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The planner is extremely easy: attendee count, format, start time, round length, buffer, generate.
ratingComments.design: Clean modern page with understandable generated schedule blocks and explanatory copy.
ratingComments.features: It handles attendee count, timing, classic/hybrid format, and minute-by-minute event planning. It does not generate named pairs, tables, constraints, or repeat-aware assignments.
ratingComments.outputs: The output is readable on-page, but no strong CSV/PDF/export workflow was observed for the free planner.
ratingComments.privacy: The free planner can be used without participant names. Cookie/analytics and the paid hosted app are separate considerations.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup is needed for the planner.
ratingComments.resultQuality: It calculates a plausible event timeline, not actual participant assignments. For group generation, that is a serious limitation.

pros: Good for speed-dating and speed-networking timing
Does not require participant personal data
cons: Actual matchmaking layer is a paid Fotify product
tools.reviewNote: Live test generated a 12-attendee speed-dating plan with five rotation rounds and a minute-by-minute schedule; no named pair/table assignments were produced.

tools.name: Games4esl Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Teachers who want quick random classroom groups and a printable PDF
tools.summary: Games4esl Random Group Generator is a classroom-focused random splitter. Paste names, choose a group size from 2 to 10, generate groups, and download a PDF. It also describes a leftover-handling choice for uneven splits. The actual grouping uses basic random assignment, but PDF output gives it a practical classroom advantage over many bare page-only splitters.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A good teacher-friendly basic splitter with PDF output. The ad/consent stack is the big downside.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Easy classroom flow: paste names, choose group size, generate. The cookie/consent overlay gets in the way.
ratingComments.design: Readable and teacher-focused, with less chaos than many old randomizer pages. Embedded in a busy content site.
ratingComments.features: Group-size mode, 2–10 size choices, and uneven-leftover handling are useful basics. No constraints, balancing, saved lists, or repeat planning.
ratingComments.outputs: Better than most simple splitters because it has a PDF download. No CSV/spreadsheet export or share link.
ratingComments.privacy: Poor. The page loads Google ads/AdSense, Google Analytics, Ezoic/gatekeeper consent scripts, and Cloudflare analytics, and the policy names Google, Amazon, and Ezoic advertising partners.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random classroom grouping. Fine for one-off teams, not for fairness beyond group sizes.

tools.reviewNote: Score the teacher tool itself, not Games4esl’s broader ESL resource library.

tools.name: Juicy Tools Group Maker
tools.bestFor: Teacher suite group maker with free-trial account flow
tools.summary: Juicy Tools Group Maker is a teacher-focused product page for a student team generator. It is part of the Juicy Tools classroom suite and promotes random group creation for lab partners, discussion groups, and classroom teams. The reviewed flow emphasized a 14-day free trial and sign-in rather than an immediately usable public widget, so it is included as a productized classroom app with account friction.
tools.pricing: 14-day free trial; sign-in required for full tool access.
tools.overallComment: Relevant classroom app, but gated behind suite/trial flow.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Reviewed with live page controls and an eight-name sample where possible.
ratingComments.design: Reflects the observed page layout, clarity, and surrounding clutter.
ratingComments.features: Teacher group maker in a classroom suite; full controls were not accessible without starting trial/sign-in.
ratingComments.outputs: No export controls verified on the public page.
ratingComments.privacy: Session cookie, Google/YouTube/ads-related hosts observed; account/trial required for use.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required for the reviewed basic flow unless noted.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Random assignment only; no full multi-session optimizer.

tools.reviewNote: Include as productized classroom app coverage.

tools.name: RakkoTools Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Basic random groups with CSV export
tools.summary: RakkoTools is a better-than-average basic random group generator. It can divide by number of teams or members per team, select organizers/representatives, and export CSV. That makes it more useful than the pure toy randomizers, but it is random-only and the page has a bad consent/ad-tech environment.
tools.pricing: Free, ad-supported web tool. No account or payment is needed for the basic generator; pricing is not the issue, the weak privacy/ad-tech environment is.
tools.overallComment: A better-than-average basic randomizer because it has CSV export, two split modes, and representatives. Not a real group-planning tool, and the privacy/ad-tech setup is bad.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The form is simple, but the page is cluttered and consent/ad friction prevents a clean 5/5.
ratingComments.design: Cluttered Rakko network utility page, but the actual tool area is usable.
ratingComments.features: Better than the weakest randomizers: number-of-teams mode, members-per-team mode, representative selection, and CSV export. No constraints, balancing attributes, repeat avoidance, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: CSV export is real and worked in testing. No spreadsheet import, Zoom export, shareable result link, or richer result workflow.
ratingComments.privacy: Bad privacy setup. The consent banner mentions personal data sharing with 210 partners, precise geolocation, ad personalization, and device storage. The privacy policy is generic and includes marketing/newsletter use.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Mostly random-only, but representative selection adds a tiny bit of assignment usefulness. There is no real balancing, constraints, repeat reduction, or quality explanation.

pros: Can divide by teams or members per team
tools.reviewNote: It does not have the same obvious search dominance as ClassTools or RandomLists, but it is mostly a generic utility page. Its CSV export and representative selection make it stronger than the weakest random splitters.

tools.name: RandomHub Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Minimal no-account random group splits with copy output
tools.summary: RandomHub Random Group Generator is a compact cyber-styled random team splitter. Paste one name per line, set the number of groups, generate, see live member/group statistics, and copy all results. It is simple and quick, but narrower than many peers: no group-size mode, no import/export files, no PDF/image, no constraints, no attribute balancing, no saved rosters, and no repeat planning.
tools.pricing: Free. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: A clean minimal splitter with copy output, but feature depth and exports are thin.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The workflow is obvious and fast: paste names, set group count, generate. Keyboard shortcut support helps.
ratingComments.design: Distinct, compact cyber-style interface with clear statistics and result cards.
ratingComments.features: Only the essentials: pasted names, number-of-groups mode, live stats, regenerate, and copy all. No group-size mode, constraints, imports, saved lists, repeat handling, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Copy-all output is useful. There is no CSV, TXT, PDF/image, share link, or spreadsheet workflow.
ratingComments.privacy: No account is required and the tested page did not show a large ad/consent stack. It is a hosted web tool, so it is not equivalent to an offline/local app.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup or login is needed.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random balanced groups. There is no result-quality logic beyond even distribution.

tools.reviewNote: Treat as a minimal generic randomizer. Its UI is nicer than its feature set.

tools.name: RandomTools.io Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Ad-supported random team splitter with copy, print, and Excel export claims
tools.summary: RandomTools.io Team Generator is a basic comma-separated random team splitter. It can split names by number of teams, optionally split by spaces and remove duplicates, then show Team1/Team2-style results. The page claims copy, print, and Excel export for results, but the workflow is surrounded by a heavy consent/ad overlay.
tools.pricing: Free ad-supported web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: It works, but it is a very ordinary random splitter buried under ad-tech clutter.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The core form is simple, but the consent/ad overlay gets in the way.
ratingComments.design: Generic utility page with ads and clutter.
ratingComments.features: Team count, split-by-space, and duplicate filtering are basic only.
ratingComments.outputs: The page advertises copy, print, and Excel export, which is better than display-only tools.
ratingComments.privacy: Poor. The page uses a large ad-consent stack and advertising scripts.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Even random splitting only, no constraints or balancing.

tools.name: SmartFreeTools Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Random team draw page with copy/Excel/print/PDF features described
tools.summary: SmartFreeTools Team Generator is an online group draw tool for splitting pasted participants into a chosen number of teams. The page describes copying results, exporting to Excel, printing, and downloading as PDF. During review the generator area showed a generating state rather than final teams, so it is listed as a relevant but lower-confidence tool.
tools.pricing: Free ad-supported web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: Relevant, but live output was not fully verified during review.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Reviewed with a pasted eight-name sample where possible; the main controls were understandable.
ratingComments.design: Usability reflects the observed page layout and surrounding clutter.
ratingComments.features: Participant list and number-of-groups workflow; output actions are described.
ratingComments.outputs: Copy, Excel, print, and PDF are described, but final output was not fully verified.
ratingComments.privacy: Cookie consent and analytics scripts were observed.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account was required for the reviewed basic flow unless otherwise noted.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Random grouping only; no full optimizer.

cons: Live run remained in generating state

tools.name: SpinTheWheel.io Team Draw Generator
tools.bestFor: Randomly pairing teams from two pots, not forming teams from people
tools.summary: SpinTheWheel.io Team Draw Generator is a German-language two-wheel matchup draw. It is not a normal participant-to-team splitter: it starts with two pots of existing teams, spins both wheels, removes selected entries, and records the matchup in a results tab. Live testing accepted the privacy dialog, spun both wheels with “Alles drehen”, produced a Team E vs Team X matchup, removed those teams from the wheels, and showed the result count/results tab. It supports editable wheel entries, separate wheels, all-at-once or individual spinning, save/share/design controls, result history, sorting/clearing results, and theme/background customization. It does not provide real group construction, team-size controls, attribute balancing, constraints, repeat-minimization, CSV/Excel export, or solver diagnostics.
tools.pricing: Free ad-supported web tool. No account was required to spin and record a matchup; saving custom wheels may require account/login behavior on the wider site.
tools.overallComment: Useful for transparent tournament-style team draws, but it is a matchup picker rather than a full group/team generator.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The sample wheels make it immediately usable, and the all-spin button worked. The cookie-consent wall, icon-heavy controls, German-only reviewed page, and two-wheel mental model make it less straightforward for ordinary team splitting.
ratingComments.design: Polished visual wheel experience with configurable design/theme controls, full-screen-style UI controls, and animated draw behavior. Ads and dense icon buttons add clutter.
ratingComments.features: Strong for its narrow purpose: two separate team pots, individual or all-at-once spins, selected-team removal, editable entries, result tab, sorting/clearing results, save/share, and design customization. Missing actual participant grouping, team-size/team-count generation, imports/exports, constraints, attributes, repeat planning, and diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Results are recorded in a results tab and can be sorted or cleared. No dedicated copy, CSV, spreadsheet, PDF/image, Zoom, or structured export was visible in live testing.
ratingComments.privacy: Weak. The consent dialog described 1728 partners, cookies/device identifiers, personal data, personalized advertising, content measurement, audience insights, precise geolocation, and device scanning. The site also loads Google Tag Manager/Analytics and advertising scripts.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup or login was needed for the tested spin/result flow.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Fair random matchup selection from two pots, but no optimization or balancing beyond equal random chance. It does not solve the harder problem of forming balanced groups from participants.

pros: Two-pot matchup draw works in live testing
Selected teams are removed after a draw
Can spin wheels individually or together
No account required for basic spinning
cons: No team-size or number-of-teams splitting mode
No constraints, attributes, or balancing solver
No repeat minimization or multi-round schedule planning
Very broad ad/tracking consent with many partners

tools.name: Teacher Toolset Random Group Maker

tools.summary: Teacher Toolset Random Group Maker is a simple classroom randomizer. It lets teachers add students individually, use Bulk Add or Import, choose by group count or group size, start grouping, rerun, and export results as text. Live testing with individually added students produced balanced groups. It is adequate for quick classroom groups, but it has no constraints, attributes, repeat handling, saved multi-class workflow, diagnostics, or strong privacy posture because the page uses Google ads and a 210-partner consent system.
tools.pricing: Free. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: A usable classroom splitter with export, but limited features and poor consent/privacy posture.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Adding students individually and grouping works. Bulk Add exists, but the tested route was not as smooth as paste-first tools.
ratingComments.design: Teacher-focused page with clear result cards and educational use-case copy.
ratingComments.features: By-count/by-size grouping, add/bulk/import, current list, regroup, and export. No constraints, attributes, repeats, or saved class manager.
ratingComments.outputs: Export is available after results. No CSV/PDF/share output was verified.
ratingComments.privacy: Bad for student names: Google ads/consent, Tag Manager/Analytics, click tracking, and a 210-partner consent notice loaded.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup or login is needed.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random balanced groups.

tools.name: TeachQuill Classroom Group Generator
tools.bestFor: AI-assisted classroom grouping drafts from natural-language constraints
tools.summary: TeachQuill Classroom Group Generator is an AI-style classroom grouping form, not a normal deterministic group generator. The page accepts a quick natural-language description or a guided form with class size, names/notes, objectives, timing, materials, and constraints. In testing, attempting to generate groups opened a sign-in/create-account modal, so the actual output quality was not verifiable without an account.
tools.pricing: Account required to generate in the tested workflow. Pricing is part of TeachQuill’s broader AI educator platform; the page pushes “Get started free” and login rather than a no-account group-generator workflow.
tools.overallComment: Interesting as an AI grouping draft tool, but account friction and unverified output keep it well below purpose-built group generators. Useful if you already want TeachQuill’s broader AI platform; not a good no-account group maker.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The prompt and guided form are easy to understand, but the workflow stops at account creation before showing generated groups.
ratingComments.design: Polished modern educator-platform page with good examples and a clean form.
ratingComments.features: Natural-language constraints and guided fields are useful, but the group-generator-specific controls are mostly prompt-based and output was not verifiable without signing in.
ratingComments.outputs: No useful output/export surface was verified because generation led to login. The page promises professional output, but not concrete CSV/copy/share controls in the free visible workflow.
ratingComments.privacy: The policy is broad and generic, including account data, user content, cookies, automatic collection, third-party providers, marketing contact, and tracking that may count as sale/sharing under California law. Not ideal for student rosters.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Generation required sign-in or account creation in the tested workflow.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Could be useful for drafts when a teacher writes good instructions, but the actual grouping behavior was not verifiable without an account and should not be credited like a proven constraint solver.

pros: Guided form for class size, names, timing, and constraints
Can express unusual grouping instructions in natural language
cons: Account required to generate in tested workflow
Prompt-based behavior is harder to audit than a deterministic grouping UI
Broad privacy policy and tracking/sale-sharing language
tools.reviewNote: Review the classroom group generator page, not TeachQuill’s whole AI educator platform. Generation prompted sign-in during testing, so keep output/result claims conservative.

tools.pricing: Free. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: A working casual splitter, but weaker than cleaner tools with exports or privacy-first design.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The basic paste-and-pick workflow works.
ratingComments.design: Readable form and result cards, mixed into a broad content/ratings page.

tools.name: Versakit Group Generator

Paste one name per line, choose number of groups or people per group, choose random/alphabetical/reverse sorting inside groups, generate evenly sized groups, then copy all groups or download a text file. It runs client-side with no account, but it has no constraints, balancing, leaders, repeats, or spreadsheet-oriented export.
tools.pricing: Free. No account or payment required.
tools.overallComment: A clean and privacy-friendly basic splitter. Better than many junk randomizers as a utility page, but not a serious group generator.

ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random grouping with even-ish sizes. Fine for one-off random teams, but no assignment quality beyond random distribution.

cons: No balancing, constraints, or repeat optimization

Generic toolkit page, not a dedicated planning workflow

It supports editing names, changing team count, copying all results, saving session data, changing the page title, and multiple display formats according to the page text. It is useful for quick casual splits, but it has ads, no constraints, no attributes, no repeat avoidance, no diagnostics, and no modern export workflow beyond copy/display.
tools.pricing: Free. No account is required.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The tool works, but localization and an older interface make the workflow less obvious.
ratingComments.design: Functional but dated, with ad slots around the tool.
ratingComments.features: Name editing, team count, copy all, saved session data, and display formats are useful basics.
ratingComments.outputs: Copy-all/display output is available; no CSV/PDF/image export was verified.

ratingComments.resultQuality: Balanced random teams only.

tools.name: Random Choice Generator Random Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Very simple browser team splitting with shareable name-list URLs
tools.summary: Random Choice Generator Random Team Generator is a bare-bones random team splitter. Live testing used a URL prefilled with Simpsons names, selected three teams, clicked Go, and produced three size-balanced random teams. The UI supports a one-per-line paste textarea, quick buttons for 2–11 teams, a custom team-count number field, instant regeneration by pressing Go again, and a Copy link feature that stores the input names in the URL for sharing/reuse. It does not provide team-size mode, editable team names, copy/export for generated teams, saved rosters beyond the URL, attributes, constraints, repeat handling, diagnostics, or multi-round planning.
tools.pricing: Free ad-supported web tool. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: Functional for quick random teams, but extremely minimal and dragged down by weak outputs plus heavy ad/tracking privacy concerns.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Fast and simple: paste names, choose a team count, click Go. Prefilled URL parameters worked. The cookie-consent wall and ad-heavy page add friction.
ratingComments.design: Plain but understandable WordPress-style page. The tool area is clear, but surrounding generator lists, tip jar content, ads, and cookie UI make it feel cluttered.
ratingComments.features: Only the basics are present: name textarea, fixed/custom number-of-teams controls, random split, and a reusable/shareable input link. No team-size mode, team names, constraints, attributes, repeat handling, file import, or solver controls.
ratingComments.outputs: Teams are displayed on the page, and the input list can be shared via URL. No dedicated copy/export/share for the generated team result was visible.
ratingComments.privacy: Very weak. The cookie banner cited 315 partners and purposes including precise geolocation/device scanning, personalized ads/content, measurement, and audience research. The privacy policy/Mediavine notice describes broad ad tracking, sharing/selling for interest-based advertising, and partner processing; live network logs showed many ad/identity-sync calls. The share URL also places participant names directly in the page URL, which can leak through analytics/referrers.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup, login, payment, or verification was needed to use the generator.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Basic random balanced teams by count. No balancing by attributes, constraints, repeats, or quality diagnostics.

pros: Generated teams successfully in live testing
Quick buttons for common team counts
Shareable/reusable URL for the input list
cons: No copy/export for generated team results
No repeat avoidance or multi-round planning
Participant names in share URLs can leak via analytics/referrers
Very heavy ad/tracking consent and partner ecosystem

It generated balanced teams correctly in the browser and stores only theme state locally. The tradeoff is that there is almost nothing else: no export, no copy button, no constraints, no labels, and no repeat planning.

tools.pricing: Free static web tool. No account, payment, or sign-up.
tools.overallComment: A very basic random splitter, but privacy-friendly, no-account, and CSV-capable enough to rank above the weakest utility pages.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Simple enough: paste names, choose people per group or number of groups, click generate. The toggle is a little clunky, but the workflow is obvious.
ratingComments.design: Very plain Bootstrap/static-page design. It is readable, but it feels like a quick internal utility rather than a polished product.
ratingComments.features: Low feature depth, but not empty: paste input, number-of-groups mode, people-per-group mode, and CSV download. No constraints, balancing, saved rosters, repeat handling, team names, leaders, diagnostics, or editing controls.

ratingComments.privacy: Strong. The page is static, uses browser-side JavaScript, has no visible analytics/ad scripts, no account, and no server submission for names. It does load jQuery and Bootstrap from CDNs, but roster data appears to stay in the browser.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account or sign-up. Just open the page.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Standard random split quality only. It distributes names into groups, but there is no balancing beyond rough group sizes, no constraints, no repeat reduction, and no diagnostics.

pros: Choose group count or people per group
cons: No saved rosters or result history
tools.reviewNote: Simple static University-hosted tool. It is not competitive on features, but privacy plus CSV export make it better than the weakest bare splitters.

tools.name: Wheelzify Random Team Generator Wheel
tools.bestFor: Presentation-style wheel-based team picking rather than one-click team splitting
tools.summary: Wheelzify Random Team Generator Wheel is a landing/workflow page for using Wheelzify as a team picker. Browser inspection found a polished Next.js wheel site with sign-in, saved wheel sets, spin history, language/theme controls, Google Analytics, Microsoft Clarity, service-worker/localStorage usage, and documentation for Party Mode, saved rosters, color coding, and fullscreen presentation. It is more of a wheel experience than a direct batch team generator; automated live testing did not reach a one-click generated team result from the landing page.
tools.pricing: Free to start. Sign-in is promoted for saved rosters/wheel sets and other site features.
tools.overallComment: Good presentation-wheel wrapper for team picking, but weak as a direct team generator and not privacy-light.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The team page explains a manual spin/remove/repeat process rather than a direct paste-and-generate flow.
ratingComments.design: Polished modern wheel UI and documentation.
ratingComments.features: Wheelzify advertises party mode, saved rosters, colors, fullscreen, and spin history, but team generation is manual/repeated wheel picking.
ratingComments.outputs: No direct team result export was verified from the team page.
ratingComments.privacy: Google Analytics, Microsoft Clarity, service worker/localStorage, cookies, and sign-in surfaces are present.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Basic browsing does not require an account, but saved rosters are tied to sign-in.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Visible random wheel picking; no automatic balanced teams, constraints, or diagnostics were confirmed.

tools.name: Flippity Random Name Picker
tools.bestFor: Spreadsheet-backed classroom grouping and quick random teams
tools.summary: Flippity Random Name Picker is a classroom utility that can create groups of 2–5 or 2–5 teams from names entered directly or from a Google Sheets template. It is more group-capable than Wheel of Names, but mostly a simple random classroom tool rather than a serious optimizer.
tools.pricing: Free, ad-supported Flippity tool. Quick-entry use is free without a Flippity account; the spreadsheet-template workflow requires a Google account and Google Sheets setup.
tools.overallComment: A useful classroom randomizer, but feature depth is thin for group generation because group/team sizes are limited to preset tabs.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The quick-entry path is easy enough: paste or type names, generate, then use the Groups/Teams tabs. The Google Sheets template path is powerful for teachers but clunky compared with a purpose-built group generator.
ratingComments.design: Functional but dated. The tabs for spinner, lineup, groups, teams, chart, timer, and fullscreen are useful, but the visual design feels old-school classroom-web rather than polished.
ratingComments.features: Low for group generation. It directly offers groups of 2–5 and teams of 2–5, which is better than a pure picker workaround, but the limits are glaring: no arbitrary number of teams, no arbitrary group size beyond the preset tabs, no custom capacities, no constraints, no balancing, no multi-session planning, and no repeat optimization.
ratingComments.outputs: Useful classroom display modes and bookmark/share links, but weak export surface. It does not provide proper CSV group assignment export, Zoom export, result diagnostics, or schedule-style output.
ratingComments.privacy: Better than many ad-heavy randomizers because Flippity says it has no user accounts, does not store tool data on Flippity servers, and reads public Google Sheet data in the browser. It uses Google Analytics and Google AdSense, and Google-Sheets-based rosters are effectively published/accessible to anyone with the long link. Not ideal for sensitive student names.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No Flippity account is needed and quick-entry works directly. The spreadsheet workflow requires a Google account and template copying, which is friction if you just want groups now.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Basic random grouping with hard preset limits. It can make simple classroom groups, but inability to choose arbitrary team counts/group sizes plus no balancing or constraints keeps quality low.

pros: Quick-entry option without a Flippity account
No Flippity server storage of activity data
cons: Google Sheets template setup is clunky
No arbitrary number of teams or group size
AdSense and Google Analytics are present
Published spreadsheet links are not truly private
tools.reviewNote: Good teacher utility, but review strictly as a group generator: direct Groups/Teams tabs are real, yet limited to fixed presets rather than arbitrary group counts/sizes.

tools.name: OpenEduCat AI Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Institutions already evaluating OpenEduCat, not quick public grouping
tools.summary: OpenEduCat AI Group Generator is presented as a feature inside the broader OpenEduCat education platform, not as a public standalone generator. The page describes roster import or pasted names, grouping by skill/learning style/interest, cooperative roles, group sizes from 2 to 5, hard and soft constraints, no-repeat history, seating arrangement, rationale, and printable/digital role cards. Those are strong claims for group-generation depth, but the page exposes no usable generator form: the calls to action lead to a demo/free-trial flow. Treat it as a gated institutional product claim, not a quick tool you can use immediately.
tools.pricing: Requires OpenEduCat access/trial. The site advertises a 15-day free trial and modular pricing from $69/year for modules, with cloud or on-premise deployment depending on the plan.
tools.overallComment: Potentially powerful on paper, but not independently usable from the public page. The rating stays conservative because the generator is gated behind OpenEduCat access and the claimed AI grouping workflow is not directly verifiable as a standalone tool.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: There is no public generator workflow on the page. A teacher looking for immediate groups has to enter a demo/trial/account path first.
ratingComments.design: The marketing page is polished and readable, with clear explanations of the claimed workflow.
ratingComments.features: The advertised feature set is unusually deep: roster import, criteria-based grouping, constraints, no-repeat history, roles, seating arrangement, and rationale. Because it is gated and not directly testable as a standalone tool, these capabilities should be treated cautiously.
ratingComments.outputs: The page claims printable role cards, digital assignments, seating views, and rationale export, but it does not expose a public result or export workflow.
ratingComments.privacy: OpenEduCat has a formal privacy policy and institutional cloud/on-premise positioning, but this is an account-based platform for student data. The public page loaded analytics/chat/product-tracking infrastructure including Google Tag Manager/Analytics/DoubleClick, PostHog, Cloudflare Insights, and Tawk.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No public generator is available without entering the OpenEduCat demo/trial/account flow.
ratingComments.resultQuality: If the advertised constraints, history, and balancing work as described, result quality could be better than random splitters. The public page does not provide enough access to verify that quality.

pros: Claims cooperative role cards and rationale
cons: Advertised AI workflow is not directly verifiable from the page
Likely too heavy for quick one-off grouping
tools.reviewNote: Scope this to the OpenEduCat AI Group Generator page. Do not credit the whole OpenEduCat ERP/LMS platform beyond the grouping claims made for this specific feature. Cap the score until the actual in-product generator can be tested.

ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required was visible.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Random group generation worked like a normal basic splitter. The AI smart grouper is the weak point: it generated groups but ignored the instructions given to it.

cons: No clear privacy policy for AI processing
No verified real constraints or balancing
tools.reviewNote: Do not treat the AI prompt box as verified constraint support. Random matching works; AI matching is interesting but instruction-following was weak.

tools.name: Homeschool of 1 Random Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Embedded classroom/homeschool team splitter inside an ad-heavy article
tools.summary: Homeschool of 1 Random Team Generator is a real embedded team generator inside a long homeschool article. Targeted browser testing confirmed that the underlying tool can generate groups from pasted student names, with group count, label, reroll, copy results, and reset controls. The normal page experience is heavily affected by consent/ad overlays and article clutter, and automated clicks were flaky until targeting the generator controls directly. It is useful if you are already on the article, but weak as a standalone group-generation product.
tools.pricing: Free, ad-supported web tool. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: A working embedded classroom splitter buried in a cluttered, ad-heavy article page.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The embedded form is understandable, but consent/ad overlays and page clutter interfere.
ratingComments.design: The generator itself is small and simple; the surrounding article page dominates the experience.
ratingComments.features: Group count, label, reroll, copy, and reset are useful basic features.
ratingComments.outputs: Copy Results is available; no file export was verified.
ratingComments.privacy: Raptive/AdThrive, consent manager, Clarity, Google analytics/tag manager, Slickstream, and related hosts were observed.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup or login is needed.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Basic random balanced groups only.

tools.reviewNote: Direct #tg-generate click produced group HTML; general headless click flow was flaky and screenshots showed consent overlay. Heavy third-party ad/analytics footprint observed.

tools.name: MiniWebTool Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Fast basic web groups with arbitrary group size or team count
tools.summary: MiniWebTool Random Team Generator is a simple browser-based group splitter. It can create groups either by members per group or by number of groups, saves the entered list locally, and has one-click copy. That makes it a real basic group generator, but not a serious optimizer.
tools.pricing: Free, ad-supported web tool with an anti-adblock/premium upsell popup. Premium is positioned as ad-free and faster; the related developer API starts free with credits and paid tiers.
tools.overallComment: A decent simple splitter, but the ad-supported/anti-adblock page environment drags it down. It is only in the basic-random-generator tier because it has no real grouping rules.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Very easy: paste one name per line, choose group size or number of groups, generate, copy. This deserves the same easy-workflow score as other obvious basic splitters.
ratingComments.design: The generated group cards are readable, but the page is a generic ad-supported utility page and the anti-adblock premium popup is intrusive. Not terrible, but clearly worse than the clean basic tools.
ratingComments.features: Basic group-generation feature depth. It supports arbitrary members-per-group or number-of-groups modes, balanced size distribution, local saved input, copy buttons, and an API. That is better than preset-only tools, but only earns about a 2 because there are no constraints, attributes, custom per-group capacities, multi-session planning, repeat optimization, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Copy buttons are useful, but this is a basic output surface. The consumer page does not provide CSV, Excel, Zoom export, share links, printable/PDF output, or persistent result history.
ratingComments.privacy: The tool page says names are stored only in browser local storage, which is good for roster privacy. The surrounding site is much less clean: ads, Google Analytics/Tag Manager, DoubleClick/AdSense-style advertising, Cloudflare analytics, and an anti-adblock popup pushing allowlisting or Premium.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account is needed for the web tool, which is good. The anti-adblock popup adds real access friction by asking users to allowlist the site or upgrade to Premium before continuing.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Standard one-off random grouping with even-ish group sizes. Consistent with the other plain random splitters: useful for a quick split, but no balancing, constraints, repeat reduction, or quality diagnostics.

pros: Supports group size or number of groups
cons: No CSV/Excel/Zoom export on consumer page
tools.reviewNote: Consistency anchor: treat MiniWebTool as a plain random splitter with arbitrary group size/count. The anti-adblock popup should count against design/privacy/pricing, not feature depth.

tools.name: Online Stopwatch Random Magic Hat Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Classroom display novelty when animated sorting matters more than features
tools.summary: Online Stopwatch Random Magic Hat Group Generator is a themed animation wrapper around a basic random group generator. The appeal is presentation: names get placed into groups by a magic-hat animation, with fullscreen and premium/ad-free hooks. As a group generator, it is thin: enter names, choose group count, watch the animation. The surrounding Online Stopwatch page is extremely ad-heavy and tracker-heavy.
tools.pricing: Free with ads. Premium/ad-free and saved-name convenience are promoted by the site.
tools.overallComment: Fun classroom theater, weak group-generation product. The ad-tech load is severe.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The concept is simple, but the generator is embedded in a cluttered, ad-heavy page and animation adds friction if you just need results.
ratingComments.design: The magic-hat theme is fun for a classroom screen, but the surrounding page is messy and dated.
ratingComments.features: Basic animated random grouping only. Premium can save names, but there are no constraints, balancing, imports, exports, repeats, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Mostly visual/on-screen. Fullscreen helps presentation; there is no serious copy/CSV/export workflow.
ratingComments.privacy: Terrible for student rosters. The page loads a large ad stack with Google ads, Amazon ads, prebid, Funding Choices, and multiple tracking/storage identifiers.
ratingComments.accountFriction: The basic generator is free, but saved names/ad-free convenience pushes toward premium.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random grouping. The animation does not improve assignment quality.

pros: No account needed for basic use
tools.reviewNote: Treat this as an animated classroom randomizer, not as a planning tool.

tools.name: PickJa Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Quick no-account random groups with group-count or members-per-group mode
tools.summary: PickJa Random Group Generator is a clean, basic splitter on PickJa’s randomizer site. Paste one name per line, choose either number of groups or members per group, and it displays balanced random groups with member counts. The tool itself is easy and useful for one-off grouping, but there are no copy/export controls, no constraints, no saved rosters, no repeat planning, and the page is wrapped in heavy advertising/consent infrastructure.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required for the generator; sign-in exists for the broader PickJa site.
tools.overallComment: A pleasant basic splitter, but shallow. It gains points for dual mode and readable output, then loses them on missing exports and severe ad-tech privacy posture.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Straightforward one-name-per-line paste flow with group-count and members-per-group modes. The consent banner is intrusive, but the core tool is easy once reachable.
ratingComments.design: Modern, readable, and cleaner than many randomizer pages. The surrounding navigation, ads/consent layer, and long SEO content keep it from feeling polished.
ratingComments.features: Basic splitter feature depth: paste names, choose number of groups or members per group, preview counts, and regenerate. No constraints, balancing attributes, imports, saved rosters, repeats, or diagnostics beyond counts.
ratingComments.outputs: Page display only. No copy button, CSV/spreadsheet export, print/PDF/image export, share link, or saved result history.
ratingComments.privacy: Poor. The tool page loads Google ad/analytics infrastructure and shows a consent prompt referencing 210 partners, device storage/access, personalized ads/content measurement, and precise geolocation. The policy also covers account/share data and Google Analytics. Not appropriate for sensitive rosters.
ratingComments.accountFriction: The generator works without an account. Sign-in is present for the broader PickJa site but not required for this workflow.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Normal random split quality. It balances group sizes evenly, but has no constraints, repeat minimization, seeded reproducibility, or quality diagnostics.

cons: No saved roster or result history
Heavy ad-tech consent banner and tracking environment
tools.reviewNote: Review the specific random group generator page, not PickJa’s broader randomizer/list product.

tools.name: QuizBreaker Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Not recommended; basic space-separated random teams on a QuizBreaker marketing page
tools.summary: QuizBreaker Team Generator is a very basic random splitter embedded in a QuizBreaker product-marketing page. Live testing found an important mismatch: the instructions say to put each name on a new line, but newline-separated names were treated as one member; space-separated names did generate three balanced teams from twelve sample names. It has a number-of-teams input and a Copy button, but no visible team-size mode despite the page copy, no custom team names, no exports beyond copy, no constraints, no attributes, no saved rosters, no repeat handling, and no diagnostics.
tools.pricing: Free public tool. No account is required for the generator; the surrounding QuizBreaker product promotes signup and paid/product workflows.
tools.overallComment: A weak random splitter. It technically works with space-separated names, but the documented newline workflow failed in live testing and the feature set is very shallow.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The intended UI is simple, but the documented one-name-per-line workflow failed in live testing because the script splits on spaces. Users can work around it with space-separated names, which is awkward for real rosters.
ratingComments.design: Modern and readable, but the actual generator is small and surrounded by a long QuizBreaker signup/product marketing page.
ratingComments.features: Very shallow: number-of-teams input, member textarea, random generation, and copy. No working newline roster paste, no team-size mode visible, no custom names, no constraints, attributes, imports, saved rosters, repeat planning, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Copy is useful. There are no CSV/TXT/JSON exports, spreadsheet output, print/PDF/image output, share links, Zoom export, or saved result history.
ratingComments.privacy: Mixed-to-poor. The generator appears browser-side and no generation API request was observed, but the page uses Google Tag Manager/Google Analytics, and the privacy policy discusses cookies, Google AdSense advertising, remarketing, and third-party behavioral tracking.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account is needed for the free generator itself, although the surrounding page heavily promotes QuizBreaker signup.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random teams, and the newline input bug can collapse a roster into one item. There are no constraints, balancing dimensions, repeat minimization, or quality diagnostics.

pros: No account required for the generator
Appears to run generation in the browser
cons: Documented newline input failed in live testing
No visible team-size mode despite page copy
No CSV/spreadsheet or other exports beyond copy
No repeat avoidance or multi-round planning
Surrounded by QuizBreaker product marketing and analytics/remarketing policy

tools.name: Random Group Generator Chrome Extension
tools.bestFor: Chrome users who want a tiny local random-group popup
tools.summary: Random Group Generator is a Chrome extension, not a normal web app. The listing describes a simple popup workflow: enter one name per line, choose the number of groups, click Generate, view labeled groups, and clear the saved list. It persists the entered names in browser local storage and the Chrome Web Store disclosure says the developer does not collect or use user data. I could inspect the listing with Playwright, but did not install/run the extension itself in the browser session.
tools.pricing: Free Chrome extension. No account advertised, but it requires installing a browser extension.
tools.overallComment: A basic random splitter packaged as a Chrome extension. The local-data posture is good, but the grouping feature set is very thin and installation is unnecessary friction for most users.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The described workflow is simple, but needing to install a Chrome extension just to split groups is extra friction compared with a web page.
ratingComments.design: Only the Chrome Web Store listing and screenshots were available here. It appears to be a small functional popup, not a polished planning interface.
ratingComments.features: Very basic: one-name-per-line input, number of groups, random generation, clear, and local-storage persistence. No group-size mode, constraints, balancing, imports, exports, repeats, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Weak. The listing mentions on-screen labeled groups only. No copy, CSV, print, share, or spreadsheet export is advertised.
ratingComments.privacy: Strong on paper for a simple tool: the Chrome Web Store disclosure says the developer does not collect or use data, and names are saved locally in browser storage. This is a small extension from a non-trader developer, so trust depends on installing extension code.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account advertised, but extension installation is a real barrier compared with no-install web tools.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random grouping only. Local storage and extension packaging do not improve assignment quality.

pros: Names stored locally in browser storage
Chrome Web Store disclosure says no data collection
tools.reviewNote: Playwright inspected the Chrome Web Store listing after rejecting Google consent. Extension itself was not installed/tested; review should stay scoped to advertised/listing behavior.

ratingComments.privacy: Names persist in local storage; no broader collection evidence in listing.

ratingComments.resultQuality: Basic random groups only.

tools.name: Spin the Crocodile Team Picker

tools.name: UltimateSolver Random Groups
tools.bestFor: Privacy-conscious basic random groups with cryptographic server randomness
tools.summary: UltimateSolver Random Groups is a basic random group generator. It lets users enter names, choose number of groups or people per group, optionally avoid incomplete groups, save name lists in cookies, use fullscreen/animation display, and print/copy the result. The randomization is server-side using PHP random_int(), so names are sent to the server even though the site says input/output data is not stored or shared.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account or payment required.
tools.overallComment: A decent basic splitter with unusually explicit randomness documentation and privacy claims, but only basic random grouping and server-side processing.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Straightforward once you find the controls: enter names, choose number of groups or people per group, generate. The page has many surrounding random-generator options, so it is less clean than the best simple splitters.
ratingComments.design: Functional but dated and busy. It feels like an old calculator/randomizer site, not a focused classroom/grouping product.
ratingComments.features: Low feature depth. It supports number-of-groups, people-per-group, no-incomplete-groups options, sorting/display options, saved name lists in cookies, fullscreen, and a puzzle/expert-group mode. No balancing, constraints, multi-session planning, repeats, attributes, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Basic output only: on-page result, copy-style output, print-friendly behavior, fullscreen display, and cookie-saved name lists. No CSV/Excel export, share links, Zoom export, or result history.
ratingComments.privacy: Mixed. The page says SSL is used and input/output data is not stored or shared, and there is a clear GDPR privacy policy. The generator posts names to a server endpoint for random_int() grouping, stores saved name lists in cookies if used, and the broader site privacy policy includes Google Analytics, AdSense, YouTube, VG WORT pixels, and affiliate tracking.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account or payment required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Standard one-off random grouping. Cryptographically secure random selection is nice, but it does not improve group assignment quality beyond random even-ish splitting.

pros: Number of groups or people per group
Claims not to store/share input or output
Can save name lists in cookies
cons: Server-side processing sends names to endpoint
tools.reviewNote: Review the random-groups page specifically. Do not over-credit cryptographic randomness; for group generation quality, it is just a random splitter.

tools.name: HeySpinner

It is good for a public random reveal, but it is not a direct group generator: there are no automatic balanced team cards, team-count/team-size generation, exports for team results, constraints, repeat avoidance, multi-round planning, or diagnostics.
tools.pricing: Free wheel tool. Account is required for saved wheels and share links; local unsaved use works without login.
