tools.reviewNote: Tested after clearing the consent overlay; the overlay otherwise intercepted clicks on the generator.

tools.name: My2lbox Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Tiny random or level-weighted groups when privacy/ad-tech tradeoffs are acceptable
tools.summary: My2lbox Random Group Generator is a small form-based group maker. It can generate random groups or weighted groups by participant level, but the workflow is clunky: participants are entered one-by-one in separate fields, extra rows must be added manually, and results are just displayed on the page. The weighted-by-level option is a real grouping feature, but the tool has almost no export or workflow support.
tools.pricing: Free. No account required.
tools.overallComment: Interesting because it has level-weighted grouping, but the UX, output surface, and privacy/ad-tech situation are weak.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Not terrible, but clunky. Instead of pasting a normal list, you enter names into separate fields and manually add more participant rows. Weighted mode adds another level-selection step.
ratingComments.design: Old Bootstrap-style page with ads and awkward layout. The cookie/ad consent layer is intrusive and parts of the surrounding page/content are in French even on the English page.
ratingComments.features: Above the weakest random splitters because it supports random or weighted grouping by participant level. That is basically it: number of groups, participant names, optional levels. No group-size mode, constraints, leaders, imports, exports, repeats, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Very poor. Results are displayed on the page only. No copy button, CSV/text download, print view, share link, saved history, or spreadsheet-friendly output was visible in testing.
ratingComments.privacy: Poor. The legal page says data submitted through tools is not tracked, stored, or archived after processing, which is good. The page shows a Google-managed consent dialog mentioning 210 partners, personalized ads/content, audience measurement, device storage/access, and possible precise geolocation, plus multiple Google AdSense/Tag scripts.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Better than a plain random splitter when weighted-by-level mode is used, because it can spread participant levels. A simple heuristic/form tool with no broader constraints or repeat optimization.

pros: Legal page says tool-submitted data is not retained
Useful for quick level-balanced classroom groups
cons: No paste-list textarea; names are entered field by field
tools.reviewNote: Playwright test confirmed random grouping from individual participant fields and page-only results. Weighted-by-level is advertised and exposed in the form, but output/export remains weak.

tools.name: Random Teams Generator Android App
tools.bestFor: Simple Android random teams with generated names and flags
tools.summary: Random Teams Generator is a simple Android app for entering names, choosing how many teams to generate, and creating random teams. It adds randomly generated team names and flags, but the listing makes clear that it does nothing special beyond simplifying team creation.
tools.pricing: Android app on Google Play. Pricing was not evaluated from the fetched listing.
tools.overallComment: A very basic mobile random team generator. Relevant for coverage, but weaker than most browser tools.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Simple mobile flow after installation.
ratingComments.design: Only store listing evidence was reviewed.
ratingComments.features: Basic team generation with random names/flags.
ratingComments.outputs: No meaningful export workflow described.
ratingComments.privacy: Data behavior was not evaluated beyond the fetched listing.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Requires Android app installation.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random teams only.

pros: Enter names and choose team count
tools.reviewNote: Reviewed from Google Play listing.

tools.name: RandomTeams.net
tools.bestFor: Very simple team-size splitting with generated funny team names
tools.summary: RandomTeams.net is a minimal random team generator. Live testing entered twelve comma-separated names, set team size four, and generated three groups with automatically generated silly team names. It is fast and no-account, but limited: it groups by team size rather than rich team-count settings, has no export/copy controls verified, no constraints, no attributes, no repeat handling, no saved rosters, and no diagnostics. The page also has ads and consent scripts.
tools.pricing: Free. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: Works for a quick team-size split, but it is one of the thinner simple randomizers.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Comma-separated input and team-size setting worked.
ratingComments.design: Very plain page with ad slots and novelty team names.
ratingComments.features: Team-size splitting and automatic silly team names are the main features.
ratingComments.outputs: No copy/download/export control was verified.
ratingComments.privacy: Google ads/consent and fonts loaded.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup or login is needed.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Balanced random teams by team size only.

tools.reviewNote: Live test generated three four-person teams with novelty names.

tools.name: Repixify Random Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Users who specifically want leader markers and custom team names and do not care about ad-tech
tools.summary: Repixify Random Team Generator promises more than a bare splitter: number-of-teams or participants-per-team modes, custom team names, a $ prefix to mark leaders, and a Copy All Teams button. On the actual page, the form is more brittle than it should be and can get stuck in the configuration state instead of showing results. Even if the promised controls work reliably for some users, this is a random splitter with no hard constraints, no balancing logic beyond team sizes/leaders, and no serious export surface.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: Interesting leader/team-name ideas, but the tool is clunky and the privacy footprint is ugly. Not a strong recommendation.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The visible flow is more fragile than a simple random team generator should be. The number/team-name controls add friction instead of clarity.
ratingComments.design: Modern enough visually, but surrounded by a generic tool-site layout, cookie prompt, and lots of related-tool clutter.
ratingComments.features: Leader markers, custom team names, and two split modes are real features. There are no constraints, balancing categories, imports, history, or repeat optimization.
ratingComments.outputs: The page describes a Copy All Teams button, but no CSV, PDF, image, or spreadsheet export. Output depth is low.
ratingComments.privacy: Very poor. The page uses a CMP/ad stack with Mediavine/Journey-style scripts, consent storage, cookies, and a policy that explicitly discusses Google ad cookies and third-party ad servers.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required for the generator.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Random-only. Leader markers may distribute marked leaders, but there is no evidence of broader assignment quality.

tools.reviewNote: Do not credit Repixify’s general AI/text-tool catalog; only the random team generator matters here.

tools.name: 1minusbeta Name Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Ultra-minimal name grouping
tools.summary: 1minusbeta Name Group Generator is an extremely minimal working group splitter. Live testing confirmed pasted names, max-names-per-group, number-of-groups input, and generated group text. The whole page is essentially just the form and output. That makes it fast and privacy-light, but it has no polish, no copy/export controls, no constraints, no saved lists, no repeat handling, and no diagnostics.
tools.pricing: Free. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: It works, but it is a bare form with almost no product surface beyond random grouping.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Simple enough, though the two numeric fields can be ambiguous.
ratingComments.design: Almost no visual design.
ratingComments.features: Basic group count/max names per group only.
ratingComments.outputs: Plain text output; no copy or export control was verified.
ratingComments.privacy: Only 1minusbeta.com was observed.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup or login is needed.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Basic random groups only.

tools.reviewNote: Live test generated three groups from twelve pasted names. Observed only first-party host requests.

tools.name: BestRandoms Random Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Very basic ad-supported random group generation
tools.summary: BestRandoms Random Team Generator is an older utility page for splitting items into groups. The page exposes an Items textarea, Groups field, and Generate button, and live browsing showed generated Team 1 through Team 4 cards with quantities. It appears to support average/random grouping from a pasted list, but there are no visible copy/export/share controls, team-size mode, custom names, constraints, attributes, repeat avoidance, multi-round planning, or diagnostics. The site is heavily ad/content driven, loading Outbrain, Google ads, Snigel/btloader advertising scripts, and Google Analytics.
tools.pricing: Free ad-supported web tool.
tools.overallComment: Covers the bare minimum but is old, ad-heavy, and has almost no output features.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Basic Items + Groups + Generate flow, but the page is cluttered.
ratingComments.design: Dated content-site layout with lots of unrelated recommendations.
ratingComments.features: Only basic group count splitting was visible.
ratingComments.outputs: On-page team cards only; no copy/export/share controls were observed.
ratingComments.privacy: Loads multiple ad networks/content recommendation scripts and Google Analytics.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Basic random grouping with team quantities.

tools.name: Breno Design Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Small rosters where you want to click-select names and make quick visual groups
tools.summary: Breno Design Group Generator is a small visual tool on a designer portfolio site. You add members one at a time, select names with checkboxes, choose the number of groups with a slider, and generate groups. It also has Shuffle, Deselect All, and Select Random Member controls. It is nicer-looking than many bare utilities, but it is inefficient for real rosters and has no paste/import/export workflow.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A cute but shallow random grouping widget. The one-at-a-time input makes it worse than many uglier but faster splitters.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Adding names one by one and selecting checkboxes is tolerable for tiny lists and bad for classrooms or workshops. Paste support is the missing basic feature.
ratingComments.design: Visually cleaner and more playful than most randomizer pages, as expected from a portfolio site.
ratingComments.features: Basic grouping only: add members, select/deselect, shuffle, select random member, choose number of groups, generate. No constraints, attributes, imports, exports, saved rosters, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Page display only. No copy button, CSV, print, image/PDF export, share link, or saved result history.
ratingComments.privacy: No account required, but there is no clear privacy page for the tool and the site loads Google Tag Manager, Microsoft Clarity, reCAPTCHA, Webflow, and other third-party scripts.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random grouping. The select/shuffle UI does not improve assignment quality.

cons: Names must be added one at a time
Google Tag Manager, Microsoft Clarity, and reCAPTCHA scripts
tools.reviewNote: Review the specific design-tool widget, not Breno Garret’s broader design services portfolio.

tools.name: Calculate.co.nz Random Team Generator
tools.bestFor: One-off random teams when page-only output is enough
tools.summary: Calculate.co.nz Random Team Generator is a plain utility on a New Zealand calculator site. Paste names, enter the number of teams, and it outputs Team 1, Team 2, and so on. It says it can handle up to 10,000 names, but it also sorts names alphabetically inside teams after assignment, which makes the result look less like a shuffled roster. There are no export controls, no group-size mode, and no real group-planning features.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: Usable for a quick split, but very basic and output-poor. It belongs near the bottom of the simple random splitter tier.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Simple enough: paste names, enter number of teams, generate. The surrounding calculator-site navigation is more clutter than the tool needs.
ratingComments.design: Old-fashioned calculator-page design. Functional, but not especially pleasant or focused.
ratingComments.features: Just number of teams and a textarea. No group-size mode, constraints, attributes, leaders, saved rosters, repeats, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: On-page results only. No copy button, CSV, spreadsheet export, print view, share link, or saved result history.
ratingComments.privacy: No account required, but the page loads Google Tag Manager/Analytics and other third-party scripts. There is no strong roster-specific privacy posture on the tool page.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random assignment, then names are sorted alphabetically inside each team. That is fine for a trivial split, but it adds no assignment-quality value.

pros: Can handle large pasted lists according to the page
tools.reviewNote: This is a small calculator-site utility, not a dedicated group assignment product.

tools.name: GoSpinWheel Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Live wheel-based team assignment when presentation matters more than balanced rosters
tools.summary: GoSpinWheel Random Group Generator is a polished wheel page for assigning one person at a time to a random team. It is fun for a live classroom or game-night reveal, with editable team labels, weights, colors, result history, and share-card output. It is not a real roster-to-groups generator: the advertised workflow is to spin for each person, so balanced sizes, constraints, repeat avoidance, imports, and proper group-roster outputs are all missing or manual workarounds.
tools.pricing: Free ad-supported web wheel. Core spinning and editing work without an account; sign-in or paid plans are tied to public share links, cloud library, and ad-free use.
tools.overallComment: A good-looking spinner, but a weak group generator. It is better understood as a presentation-oriented random team assignment workaround than as a tool that generates balanced groups.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Editing team labels and spinning the wheel is straightforward. As a group generator, the workflow is clunky because participants are not pasted into a roster and split at once; the user has to call each person and spin repeatedly, or build a multi-wheel workaround.
ratingComments.design: Modern, colorful, and engaging for live presentation. The wheel UI, sounds, themes, and result modal look much better than most old utility pages, but ads, consent UI, sign-in prompts, and generic wheel-site controls add clutter.
ratingComments.features: Strong spinner features do not translate into strong group-generation features. It has editable entries, weights, colors, visual themes, result history, local saves, share-card output, and general wheel import/export, but no real team-size controls, constraints, roster import, attribute balancing, repeat planning, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Useful for sharing a single spin result: copy/download a winner image, social share buttons, and a results tab. Weak for actual groups because it does not produce a complete team roster, CSV/spreadsheet export, Zoom breakout output, or printable group assignment report.
ratingComments.privacy: Poor for sensitive rosters. The page uses a Google-managed consent/ad stack, AdSense/Funding Choices, Google Analytics, and ads for anonymous/free users. The privacy policy says anonymous visitors can save locally, but public share links and cloud library entries store wheel payloads, and share links are not private storage.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account is needed to spin or edit the wheel. Sign-in is required for public share links, and paid plans are used for cloud library/ad-free workflows.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Random team assignment only, one spin at a time. There is no guarantee of balanced group sizes, no constraints, no attribute balancing, no repeat minimization, and no quality diagnostics.

pros: Editable team names, colors, and weights
No account required for core spinning
Copy/download/share card for a spin result
Local wheel saving and general wheel import/export
cons: Does not generate full group rosters
No constraints, attributes, or repeat avoidance
No CSV/spreadsheet or Zoom group export

tools.name: PinCalculator Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Tiny no-account random groups when one-by-one name entry is acceptable
tools.summary: PinCalculator Random Group Generator is a very small random grouping page. Users add names one at a time, enter a group size, and generate simple random groups. It works, but there is no paste-list workflow, no copy or export button, no constraints, no balancing beyond group size, and no saved roster handling.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A working but extremely basic random group generator. The one-by-one name entry makes it weaker than most paste-and-generate tools.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The workflow is simple, but adding every name individually is annoying for real rosters.
ratingComments.design: Clean enough generic calculator-site layout, with lots of surrounding tool links.
ratingComments.features: Almost no group-generation depth: add names, choose group size, generate.
ratingComments.outputs: Display-only results. No copy, print, share, CSV, or download controls.
ratingComments.privacy: No account and only light observed analytics, but the privacy policy is generic and the page uses Google Analytics.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Random groups by size only, with no balancing, constraints, or diagnostics.

tools.reviewNote: Useful for maximum coverage, but weak compared with ordinary textarea-based randomizers.

tools.name: RandomCount Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Tiny groups where adding names one at a time is acceptable
tools.summary: RandomCount Team Generator is a basic member-per-team splitter. Add each member one at a time, set members per team, and generate. It eventually produces simple Team 1/Team 2/Team 3 results, but there is no paste-a-roster textarea, no copy/export, no group-count mode, no constraints, no saved roster, and no meaningful diagnostics.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A working but weak random splitter. The one-at-a-time name entry alone makes it worse than most basic paste-and-generate tools.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Adding members one by one is tedious compared with pasting a class list. The rest is simple enough.
ratingComments.design: Plain Bootstrap-style utility page. Usable, but not polished.
ratingComments.features: Only add/remove members and members-per-team random splitting. No paste import, group-count mode, constraints, balancing, saved lists, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Results display on the page only. No copy, CSV, PDF, share, or print-focused output.
ratingComments.privacy: The footer privacy policy is easy to find and it discloses Google Analytics/AdSense, location-derived information, generated-data language, and legal/service-provider sharing. The tool page also set Google Analytics cookies. Lighter than many ad-tech pages, but not ideal for roster data.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random grouping with even team size. Fine for trivial one-off use only.

pros: Not overloaded with a huge consent stack
cons: Vague privacy policy language around location and generated data
tools.reviewNote: Treat the repeated “balanced” language as basic equal-size splitting, not real balancing. Privacy policy lives at https://randomcount.in/privacypolicy.

tools.name: Terrific Tools Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: People who want a small browser tool with individual member entry and simple group-size controls
tools.summary: Terrific Tools Random Group Generator is not a serious group-planning tool. It has a group title, members-per-group setting, dummy-name option, member rows, balance/remainder toggles, and an advertised copy-to-clipboard output. The problem is the workflow is clunkier than most simple splitters because it uses individual member fields instead of a normal paste-a-list textarea, and the page is wrapped in promotional banners, comments, account buttons, and a huge directory of unrelated random tools.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required for generation; account prompts are for comments, ratings, bookmarks, and the broader site.
tools.overallComment: A weak random group generator with a few basic controls but worse input ergonomics than many simpler tools. The surrounding site noise and ad/privacy posture drag it down further.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The tool is usable, but individual member rows are a bad fit for a class or workshop roster. A simple textarea would be faster.
ratingComments.design: The actual form is readable, but the repeated launch banner, account/comment widgets, and enormous unrelated-tool grid make the page feel bloated.
ratingComments.features: Slightly above the weakest splitters: group title, members-per-group, dummy names, balance/remainder toggles, and dynamic members. No constraints, attributes, multi-round planning, saved rosters, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: The page advertises copying groups to the clipboard, but there is no spreadsheet, CSV, image, PDF, Zoom, or share workflow visible in the basic form.
ratingComments.privacy: The site says tools process data in the browser, which is good for names. The privacy policy also discloses Google Analytics and Mediavine interest-based advertising, and the page sets CMP/ad-consent storage.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Generation itself is free and no-login. The account UI is mainly for ratings, comments, and bookmarks.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Basic random grouping only. Balance/remainder toggles help group sizes, not assignment quality.

cons: Clunky member entry for real rosters
Google Analytics and Mediavine ad/privacy footprint
tools.reviewNote: Review the specific random group generator, not the broader terrific.tools directory or desktop converter product.

tools.name: MadeInText Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Very simple one-off random groups when privacy does not matter
tools.summary: MadeInText Random Group Generator is a tiny free web tool: paste names or items, enter the number of groups, and generate. It does produce random groups, but that is the whole story. There is no group-size mode, no copy/export button for results, no constraints, no balancing, no saved roster, and no diagnostics.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A working bare splitter dragged down by a heavy ad/consent stack and almost no features.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Paste names, enter number of groups, generate. It is extremely simple.
ratingComments.design: Plain content-site layout with lots of surrounding navigation and tool links. Functional but not pleasant.
ratingComments.features: Only number-of-groups random splitting. No group-size mode, constraints, balancing, saved lists, imports, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Results are just printed on the page. The Copy Link button shares the page, not the generated groups.
ratingComments.privacy: Terrible for classroom/student names. The page uses Mediavine/rapid-edge style ad scripts, consent storage, Google Analytics, and a policy describing AdSense, Mediavine, IP address, device, browser, email hash, and interest-based advertising data.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random assignment. Fine for trivial splits, nothing more.

tools.reviewNote: Treat it as a basic text-tool-site widget, not a serious classroom group planner.

tools.name: PureTables Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Very basic page-only random teams
tools.summary: PureTables Random Group Generator is as basic as it gets: enter names one per line, enter how many teams to generate, click Generate, and read the page result. It works, but there are no output buttons, no group-size mode, no constraints, no balancing, no saved roster, and no polish. The site also uses Google AdSense/Funding Choices and a privacy policy that describes Google Analytics and ad cookies.
tools.pricing: Free web tool. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A working but bare random splitter. It has almost no reason to rank above cleaner or more capable alternatives.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Simple: paste names, enter team count, generate. It would be hard for a tool this thin to be confusing.
ratingComments.design: Plain, dated utility-page design with ads and generic site navigation.
ratingComments.features: Only names and number of teams. No group-size mode, constraints, leaders, balancing, saved rosters, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Page display only. No copy, CSV, print, PDF, share, or export controls.
ratingComments.privacy: Bad. The page loads AdSense/Funding Choices and the privacy policy describes Google Analytics, IP/location/browser data, and Google ad cookies.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random grouping only.

tools.reviewNote: This is a basic random-generator page, not a classroom planning tool.

tools.name: Sheffield Hallam TEL-e-vision Group Maker
tools.bestFor: Very quick no-account random classroom groups
tools.summary: Sheffield Hallam TEL-e-vision Group Maker is a tiny old-school HTML/JavaScript random group generator. Paste one name per line, optionally type a list of roles, choose 1–20 groups, and it shuffles names into groups in the browser. It is refreshingly direct and account-free, but it has almost no product surface beyond basic random splitting.
tools.pricing: Free web page. No account or payment required.
tools.overallComment: A bare-bones random splitter. Useful in a pinch, but it is not competitive with modern group generators and has no real planning/export capability.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Very simple: paste names, choose number of groups, click the button. The only friction is the dated page and confusing optional roles field.
ratingComments.design: Very dated. Large old-style controls, table layout, document.write result page, and no modern polish. It is readable, but barely a product UI.
ratingComments.features: Almost nothing beyond random splitting by number of groups. The roles field is just displayed alongside each group rather than acting like a real assignment or constraint system. No balancing, constraints, imports, repeat handling, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Extremely weak. Results are just written to a new page view. You can manually select/print through the browser, but there is no export, copy button, CSV, spreadsheet, image, PDF, share link, or saved history.
ratingComments.privacy: Better than server-side randomizers because the grouping logic runs in page JavaScript and there is no account. However the page includes Sheffield Hallam Google Tag Manager, and the broader university privacy policy is general website/institutional tracking rather than a dedicated student-roster promise for this tool.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random grouping. It makes even-ish groups by shuffling and distributing names round-robin, but there is no balancing, preferences, keep-apart logic, or repeat reduction.

cons: Roles field is not real role assignment
No group size control beyond number of groups
Google Tag Manager on the page

tools.name: Web0 Random Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Ultra-minimal local random groups
tools.summary: Web0 Random Team Generator is an extremely small browser-side random group page. Paste names, choose the number of groups, click Genera Gruppi, and it displays group cards. The implementation uses client-side JavaScript and no obvious ad/analytics scripts on the tool page, but the UI is partially Italian even on the English URL and there are no output/export controls.
tools.pricing: Free. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A minimal local random splitter. Privacy/page simplicity is good, but the tool is too bare to be competitive.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Easy enough once you understand the Italian labels: paste names, set number of groups, generate. The language mismatch hurts clarity.
ratingComments.design: Simple Bootstrap-style layout. Not ugly, but generic and partly untranslated.
ratingComments.features: Almost no feature depth: paste names and choose number of groups. No group-size mode, constraints, balancing, leaders, exports, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Page display only. No copy, download, CSV, print, share, or saved history.
ratingComments.privacy: Good on observed behavior. The tool page loads local JS plus jQuery/Bootstrap and the grouping code runs client-side with Math.random; no obvious ads/analytics scripts were present in the tested page HTML.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random round-robin grouping. Fine for one-off random teams, nothing more.

pros: No obvious ad/analytics scripts on tested page
cons: Partly Italian UI on English page
tools.reviewNote: Playwright test generated 3 groups from 9 names. JS inspection confirmed client-side Math.random shuffle with no fetch/localStorage/export code.

tools.name: ClassTools Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Very quick classroom group splits
tools.summary: ClassTools is an extremely weak group generator with unusually high search-engine visibility. It technically works for a quick random classroom split: paste names, choose the number of groups, generate, then save/link or print. The UI is dated, the page is cluttered and ad-heavy, the feature set is shallow, and the privacy/ad-tech footprint is bad.
tools.pricing: Free, ad-supported web tool. No account or payment is needed for the random group generator. Premium removes ads and adds broader ClassTools workspace/history benefits, but the reviewed free group-generator workflow is surrounded by monetized site clutter.
tools.overallComment: Generally, one of the weakest group generators on this list. It works, but it is a dated, ad-heavy, shallow random splitter whose search-engine visibility is far higher than its product quality deserves.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The core workflow is easy because there is almost nothing to configure: paste names, choose group count, generate. Ads, consent prompts, premium nudges, and old UI clutter keep it from being a clean 5/5.
ratingComments.design: Terrible, dated, and cluttered. The tool is usable, but the surrounding page feels like an old ad-heavy template site rather than a modern group generator.
ratingComments.features: Very shallow. It has paste names, arbitrary group count, save/link, and print, but no balancing, constraints, repeat avoidance, imports, roster management, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Save/link and print are genuinely useful and better than display-only tools. There is no proper CSV, spreadsheet, PDF, image, or Zoom-friendly export workflow.
ratingComments.privacy: Bad. The page shows a large consent/ad stack, including language about personal data being stored/accessed/shared with 1147 partners, plus Ezoic/Gatekeeper/Google/Funding Choices and multiple tracking identifiers. I would not enter sensitive classroom rosters here.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account is required, but a premium login exists. For the basic group generator workflow, there is basically no signup friction.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Random only. This is what is advertised, but nothing more: no balancing, no constraints, no repeat reduction, and no quality diagnostics.

cons: Extremely weak despite high search-engine visibility
Search ranking far exceeds product quality
tools.reviewNote: This tool ranks extremely high in search-engine results for many related searches despite being a weak, dated, ad-heavy random splitter. Keep the review blunt: search visibility is not evidence of product quality.

tools.name: Musely Team Builder Generator
tools.bestFor: Not recommended unless Musely verification works for the user
tools.summary: Musely Team Builder Generator presents itself as an AI-powered team formation tool with participant paste, number of teams, distribution method, team names, captain assignment, constraints, skill categories, custom requirements, language, quantity, and a smarter-AI toggle. In live testing, participant names and team count could be entered, but the Generate button stayed disabled behind Cloudflare-style verification even after retry, so no actual teams, outputs, or constraint behavior could be verified. The page also loads PostHog/Plausible-style analytics and has broad privacy-policy language about personal data, cookies, tracking, marketing sharing, and account data.
tools.pricing: Public tool page with free-looking generator controls, upgrade/signup surfaces, and a disabled generate flow in live testing. No completed no-account generation was verified.
tools.overallComment: Feature claims are ambitious, but the reviewed generator did not actually generate teams in live testing because verification never completed.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The form is understandable, but live testing could not get past verification; Generate remained disabled after filling inputs and retrying verification.
ratingComments.design: Modern SaaS-style interface with clean fields and advanced settings, though the sidebar, upgrade prompts, verification, and generic AI-tool shell add friction.
ratingComments.features: Many controls are visible or claimed: distribution method, team names, captains, constraints, skill categories, custom requirements, language, quantity, and smarter AI. Because generation never completed, these remain mostly unverified feature claims rather than proven group-generation capability.
ratingComments.outputs: No output was generated in live testing, so no copy/export/share/result format was verified.
ratingComments.privacy: Poor-to-mixed. The page uses Cloudflare verification plus PostHog/Plausible-style analytics, and the privacy policy allows collection of personal data, usage data, cookies/tracking, marketing communications, affiliates/partners, and third-party sharing. Entered participant names may be sent to an AI/service if generation works, but live testing did not reach that point.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No explicit login was required to fill the form, but generation was blocked by verification and the product has prominent signup/upgrade surfaces.
ratingComments.resultQuality: No actual result was verified. If the advertised controls work, it may be more capable than a random splitter, but the reviewed flow produced no teams.

pros: Visible advanced controls for constraints, captains, team names, and skills
cons: Generate stayed disabled behind verification in live testing
No actual teams were produced or verified
No output/export behavior could be evaluated
Broad tracking and data-sharing privacy policy
Unclear whether participant names are processed by AI/server when generation works

tools.name: Schule Dietmannsried Gruppen Generator
tools.bestFor: Bare-bones German classroom group splitting
tools.summary: Schule Dietmannsried Gruppen Generator is a tiny German-language school utility. Paste names, choose either maximum group size or number of groups, generate, and it displays groups plus counts. It appears to be a minimal client-side page with no ads or external scripts on the tool page, but it has no copy/export workflow and almost no features beyond splitting names.
tools.pricing: Free. No account required.
tools.overallComment: A very bare-bones random splitter. Privacy/page simplicity is good, but product capability is almost nonexistent.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Simple if you understand German: paste names, choose mode, enter a number, click generate. The labels are minimal and not localized to English.
ratingComments.design: Extremely plain school utility page. Functional, but visually primitive.
ratingComments.features: Only two basic split modes: maximum group size or number of groups. No constraints, balancing, leaders, exports, saved rosters, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: Page display only. No copy button, download, CSV, print view, share link, or saved result history.
ratingComments.privacy: Good on the tool page itself: no obvious ad/analytics scripts and the grouping appears client-side. The broader school privacy policy mentions cookies/server logs, but this specific page is lightweight.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Plain random grouping with even-ish groups. No assignment quality beyond random splitting.

pros: Maximum group size or number-of-groups mode
No obvious ad/tracking scripts on tool page
tools.reviewNote: Playwright test generated 3 groups from 9 names. The first attempted shell fill used literal backslash-n; retest with real newlines confirmed normal operation.

tools.name: Wheel Decide Random Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Manual wheel-based team selection with add/remove segments
tools.summary: Wheel Decide Random Team Generator is an older wheel-decide page framed as a random team generator. Live testing entered eight names into the Enter segments box; the page showed the wheel, Shuffle, Sort, Add Image, Add Segments, Reset Wheel, and instructions for repeatedly spinning and removing selected names to assemble teams manually. This is not a true automatic team partitioner: it picks names one at a time and expects the organizer to assign them to teams. It can still be useful for public random selection, but it has no automatic balanced team cards, team-size/team-count generation, export, constraints, repeat minimization, multi-round planning, or diagnostics.
tools.pricing: Free web tool.
tools.overallComment: A random wheel repackaged for team picking; useful for manual reveal, weak as a group generator.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Adding segments is simple, but forming teams requires manual repeated spins and bookkeeping.
ratingComments.design: Older wheel page with lots of surrounding navigation and stats.
ratingComments.features: Shuffle/sort/add image/reset wheel; no automatic team generation or grouping controls.
ratingComments.outputs: No team export/copy was observed.
ratingComments.privacy: Fetch was blocked by 403 outside the browser; browser page loaded with warnings and likely standard site scripts. No strong privacy claim.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account required.
ratingComments.resultQuality: One-at-a-time random selection, not balanced automatic team assignment.

cons: Does not automatically create balanced teams

tools.name: Wheel of Names
tools.bestFor: Random picking, raffles, and manual group-building workarounds
tools.summary: Wheel of Names is primarily a random name picker, not a group generator. Teachers and facilitators can use it to pick names and manually assemble teams, but there is no real group-generation workflow beyond workarounds.
tools.pricing: Free, ad-supported spinner. No paywall for premium wheel features, but that mostly benefits random picking/presentation rather than actual group generation.
tools.overallComment: Excellent random picker, but barely a group generator. It only belongs here because people use it as a manual workaround for classroom grouping.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: For random picking, it is about as easy as it gets: paste names and spin. For actual group generation, the workflow becomes manual, but the base tool itself is very easy.
ratingComments.design: Polished, familiar, and good for live presentation. The wheel, sounds, themes, fullscreen/streaming support, and customization are much stronger than most plain utility sites.
ratingComments.features: For group generation specifically, feature depth is very low. Multi-wheel, weighted entries, themes, embedding, API access, and audio customization are useful spinner features, but they do not create balanced groups. There is no real team splitter, no group capacities, no grouping constraints, no multi-session planning, and no repeat optimization.
ratingComments.outputs: Strong output/sharing options for a wheel: short links, embeddable iframes, streaming support, local/cloud saved wheels, and API access. Weak as a group-generator output surface because it does not produce proper team rosters, CSV group assignments, Zoom exports, or schedule-style results.
ratingComments.privacy: Mixed. Better than the worst ad-tech utilities because it has local-file saving, automatic device-local state, explicit privacy/security documentation, and an option to close ads for the session. Not great for sensitive classroom names: the site uses ads and Google Analytics, cloud-saved/shared wheels can put entries in Firestore/Google infrastructure, account login is needed for cloud access, and shared URL examples can include entries directly in the URL.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No account is needed for basic spinning, local use, or local file saving. Cloud-saved wheels and cross-device access require login, so it is not completely account-free.
ratingComments.resultQuality: As a random picker, the randomness is strong. As a group generator, result quality is barely applicable: teams have to be built manually, so there is no balancing, no constraint handling, and no repeat minimization.

pros: Google Sheets import and weighted entries
cons: Manual workaround needed for team splits
No constraints, capacities, or repeat optimization
Shared/cloud wheels can expose entered names
tools.reviewNote: Review strictly as a group-generator candidate. Wheel of Names is a strong presentation/random-picking tool, but its group-generation capability is basically a workaround: pick people one at a time and manually form teams.

tools.name: AhaSlides Random Team Generator
tools.bestFor: AhaSlides users who already want random-team generation inside an interactive presentation workflow
tools.summary: AhaSlides presents this as a random team generator feature, but the public page is mostly a marketing page for the broader presentation platform. It says to try the demo or sign up to unlock more features, but the standalone generator is not clearly exposed as a normal paste-names-and-generate tool on the page. As a group-generator entry, this should be scoped narrowly: AhaSlides may be a capable presentation product, but this page is not a strong standalone group maker.
tools.pricing: AhaSlides has a free plan with limits and paid presentation plans. The pricing page describes up to 5 quiz and 3 poll slides on Free, paid upgrades for unlimited interactive slides, and special education/nonprofit rates.
tools.overallComment: Useful only if you are already in AhaSlides. As a standalone group generator, it is weak and hidden behind platform/account context.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The page does not behave like a straightforward public group generator. It pushes users toward signup and the broader platform.
ratingComments.design: The marketing page is polished, but that does not make the group generator itself good.
ratingComments.features: For group generation specifically, very little is visible. Do not give credit for unrelated presentation, quiz, poll, or AI slide features.
ratingComments.outputs: No useful standalone group output/export workflow is exposed on the public page.
ratingComments.privacy: AhaSlides has a serious privacy/security policy and GDPR/PDPA language, but the marketing page loads GTM, Microsoft Clarity/Bing, CookieScript, Zoho chat, HubSpot/Webflow-style scripts, and platform data is hosted/processed by AhaSlides.
ratingComments.accountFriction: The page explicitly pushes signup to unlock more features. Bad for a quick public group generator.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Assume basic random assignment only from the public description. No visible constraints, balancing, or optimization.

pros: Part of a real presentation platform
Security/privacy documentation is more serious than content-farm tools
cons: Standalone generator is not clearly accessible
Many marketing/analytics/chat scripts on the page
Presentation features are mostly irrelevant to group generation
tools.reviewNote: Review the random-team-generator page/feature only, not AhaSlides as an audience-engagement suite.

tools.name: BasedLabs Random Team Generator with Conditions
tools.bestFor: Prompt ideas for AI-assisted grouping, not a structured group generator
tools.summary: BasedLabs Random Team Generator with Conditions is an AI-tool landing page, not a normal structured team generator. The page describes using Gemini 2.5 Flash and natural-language prompts to create teams with skills, roles, pairings, restrictions, locks, and scores. In live testing, there was no visible roster table, split-mode control, constraint editor, or generated group output on the public page; the experience appears to route users into BasedLabs’ broader AI generation/sign-in ecosystem. It may be usable as a prompt wrapper, but it is not transparent or reliable enough for serious group assignment.
tools.pricing: BasedLabs account/product pricing applies; the public page did not expose a no-account structured generator.
tools.overallComment: Mostly a generic AI prompt page with group-generator marketing; not a practical transparent assignment tool.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The page explains prompts, but no structured roster-to-teams workflow was visible in public testing.
ratingComments.design: Polished BasedLabs marketing page, but unrelated AI/social/media navigation dominates.
ratingComments.features: Claims conditions, skills, roles, locks, rerolls, and scoring, but these were not exposed as verifiable structured controls.
ratingComments.outputs: No generated teams or export workflow were produced in testing.
ratingComments.privacy: Roster data would likely be processed through a hosted AI service. Stripe, Google, BasedLabs CDN/media, affiliate, and model-related domains loaded.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Sign-in/product funnel friction is high compared with no-account group tools.
ratingComments.resultQuality: AI prompt output could be helpful for brainstorming, but it is not deterministic, auditable, or structured like a solver.

pros: Mentions skills, roles, restrictions, locks, and scores
Could help users draft grouping prompts
tools.reviewNote: Do not credit the prompt examples as verified structured product controls.

tools.name: FreeTextTools Random Team Generator
tools.bestFor: Feature-rich-looking team generator that did not produce visible teams in testing
tools.summary: FreeTextTools Random Team Generator presents a richer team-generator UI with team-count/team-size modes, team-name styles, captain/statistics options, drag-and-drop claims, and CSV/JSON export claims. In live testing, the page accepted pasted names and detected 12 participants, but clicking Generate Teams did not produce visible team output. JavaScript errors related to ads were also observed. Because the visible generator flow did not complete in the test browser, this review rates the actual verified behavior low despite the ambitious feature copy.
tools.pricing: Free, ad-supported web tool. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: Promising UI claims, but the tested public flow accepted names and then failed to show generated teams.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: The form is clear, but generation did not produce visible teams in live testing.
ratingComments.design: Modern-looking tool panel and documentation.
ratingComments.features: The page advertises useful features, but many could not be credited because generation did not complete visibly.
ratingComments.outputs: CSV/JSON export is advertised, but no generated output/export was verified.
ratingComments.privacy: Google ads/funding choices, analytics, Cloudflare insights, Ko-fi assets, and remote fonts were observed.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup or login is needed.
ratingComments.resultQuality: No visible teams were produced in the tested flow.

cons: No visible teams generated in testing
tools.reviewNote: Live tests with and without consent saw 12 participants detected, but no visible generated teams after Generate Teams. Page errors: adsbygoogle is not defined.

tools.name: BasedLabs Random Group Generator
tools.bestFor: Prompt examples for AI group formation, not a dependable generator
tools.summary: BasedLabs Random Group Generator is another AI landing page rather than a focused grouping product. It describes AI-powered random groups and gives sample prompts for classrooms, workshops, hackathons, and sports day teams, including skills and keep-apart constraints. The public page did not expose a clear no-account form for pasting a roster, choosing group size/count, generating groups, copying results, or exporting. For actual group-generation work, this is much weaker than simple browser tools because it is prompt-based, opaque, and tied to the BasedLabs AI ecosystem.
tools.pricing: BasedLabs account/product pricing applies; no standalone free structured generator was verified.
tools.overallComment: Useful as prompt inspiration, weak as a transparent group generator.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Reading prompt examples is easy; actually generating groups through a clear roster workflow was not available in testing.
ratingComments.design: Polished marketing page with lots of unrelated BasedLabs AI navigation.
ratingComments.features: Mentions group count/size, skills, and keep-apart constraints in prompts, but no structured controls were verified.
ratingComments.outputs: No generated group output or export was produced in public testing.
ratingComments.privacy: Likely hosted AI processing if used. BasedLabs, Google, Stripe, media/model, and affiliate domains loaded.
ratingComments.accountFriction: High compared with no-account group splitters.
ratingComments.resultQuality: AI text may produce plausible groupings, but it is opaque and not reliably auditable.

pros: Could be used for brainstorming grouping prompts
tools.reviewNote: Review as a BasedLabs AI prompt page, not as a traditional random group webapp.

tools.name: Wordwall Random Team Generator Resource
tools.bestFor: A prebuilt Wordwall spin-wheel activity, not a real group generator
tools.summary: This Wordwall page is a shared Spin the wheel resource titled Random Team Generator. It is not a standalone team generator in the normal sense: it is an open-ended wheel activity with a fixed set of entries, Wordwall resource controls, leaderboard/template settings, visual-style options, print/embed/share surfaces, and login/subscription-gated editing features. It may be useful if someone specifically wants a Wordwall wheel activity, but it does not accept an ad hoc roster and create balanced teams without editing the resource. It has no automatic team-count or team-size generation, exports for team assignments, constraints, repeat avoidance, or diagnostics.
tools.pricing: Viewing the shared resource is free. Editing/assignment/template features involve Wordwall login and subscription-gated surfaces.
tools.overallComment: Barely qualifies as a group generator; it is mostly a shared wheel activity.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Not a paste-and-generate tool. Using it for your own teams requires editing/copying the activity through Wordwall.
ratingComments.design: Wordwall is polished, but the resource page is busy and education-platform-heavy.
ratingComments.features: Wheel/template options, embed/print/share, and visual styles exist, but actual team-generation capability is almost absent.
ratingComments.outputs: Wordwall offers share/embed/print style surfaces, not useful team-assignment exports.
ratingComments.privacy: Wordwall loads Google Tag Manager/gtag, cookie consent, and a broader hosted education platform policy.
ratingComments.accountFriction: Log in/subscription prompts appear for editing and several resource actions.
ratingComments.resultQuality: A wheel can pick entries, but it does not create balanced teams.

tools.name: DecisionMakerTool Team Picker
tools.bestFor: A visual picker page, not a real team generator
tools.summary: DecisionMakerTool Team Picker is presented as a team picker wheel, but live testing did not expose a usable team-generation workflow. The page has a choices textarea, removable current-choice chips, quick templates, a small customization panel, and a wheel-style interface. It does not provide a clear number-of-teams control, group-size control, generated team output, export, constraints, repeat handling, or result diagnostics. For group-generation work, this is effectively a random picker page with misleading team-generator copy.
tools.pricing: Free. No account is required.
tools.overallComment: Weak as a group generator: the page talks about teams but does not deliver a clear team-splitting workflow.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: Adding choices is possible, but the actual team-splitting path is not discoverable in testing.
ratingComments.design: The wheel/picker page is readable and not visually broken, but it is mismatched to the claimed team-generator job.
ratingComments.features: No confirmed group-count or group-size generation flow. No constraints, attributes, repeats, saved rosters, or diagnostics.
ratingComments.outputs: No generated group output or export was produced in testing.
ratingComments.privacy: No account is required, but Google Tag Manager/Analytics and Google font services were loaded.
ratingComments.accountFriction: No signup or login is needed for the page.
ratingComments.resultQuality: Not meaningfully assessable as group generation because it did not create teams.

pros: Accepts a pasted list of choices
cons: No group count or group size control found
tools.reviewNote: Live test found a choices textarea and customization controls, but no reliable team split result. Do not credit the marketing copy as implemented capability.

tools.name: Teach Starter Random Name Picker
tools.bestFor: Picking one student at a time, not generating groups
tools.summary: Teach Starter has a free random name picker/spin-wheel classroom widget, but I could not find a real random group generator from Teach Starter. It can support manual grouping only by picking names one at a time or using saved word lists; it does not split a roster into teams.
tools.pricing: Free random name picker widget; broader Teach Starter resources and saved-account workflows may sit behind free/paid teacher plans, depending on region/resource.
tools.overallComment: Not a real group generator. Keep only if the article wants to mention classroom random-picking workarounds; otherwise it is a removal candidate.

ratingComments.easeOfUse: For picking a single random name, it is easy. For generating groups, the workflow is bad because there is no group-generation workflow at all; you would have to pick names manually.
ratingComments.design: Polished and classroom-friendly compared with many utility sites. The issue is not presentation quality; it is that the relevant tool is a name picker, not a group maker.
ratingComments.features: Almost no group-generation feature depth. It can pick random names and use saved word lists, but it does not choose team count, group size, capacities, balancing, constraints, exports, or repeat avoidance.
ratingComments.outputs: No useful group output. At best it displays one selected name or spinner result. No team roster, copy/export surface, CSV, print groups, or shareable generated assignment.
ratingComments.privacy: Better than ad-tech-heavy utilities: no obvious AdSense mess on the widget page and Teach Starter has detailed education/privacy language including COPPA/PRIVO posture. A hosted education platform with accounts, cookies, server logs, location/IP/device data, marketing uses, analytics/customer tooling, and saved word-list/account behavior.
ratingComments.accountFriction: The random name picker is presented as free, but the broader Teach Starter ecosystem pushes account/resource workflows, and saved class lists depend on Teach Starter account features. No account is needed for the basic idea, but it is not as frictionless as a tiny standalone splitter.
ratingComments.resultQuality: No group result quality to credit. Random single-name selection may be fair, but it does not create balanced teams or group assignments.

pros: Free widget for simple random selection
Saved word lists can help teachers reuse class lists
cons: No team count or group size controls
No group roster output or export
Hosted platform collects normal account/log/cookie data
tools.reviewNote: No working Teach Starter group generator was found. The closest relevant tool is the free Random Name Picker / Spin Wheel, which is only a manual grouping workaround.

Clicking into the flow led to sign-up and Google OAuth, with console errors during the test. Treat it as an AI content/product funnel rather than a dependable group-assignment tool.
tools.pricing: Account/product funnel; no standalone free structured generator was verified.
